component_layouts.layout_narrative: In error or empty states (such as an invalid eventId or data fetch failure), the central content column is dedicated entirely to a clear feedback message. This block begins roughly 48–64px below the main navigation—similar to the starting position of the normal content—to keep the composition familiar. The message is left-aligned within the same narrow, centered column and typically includes a brief heading like 'Event Not Found' or 'Unable to Load Event', followed by one or two short explanatory lines. Vertical spacing between heading and body text is about 8–16px, and the entire message area is surrounded by generous white space, with no registration or event content visible above or below it. An optional, small icon or subtle accent line may appear above the heading, but overall the visual treatment remains very minimal, echoing the rest of the page’s editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces all standard event content within the central column when active. It remains within the natural scroll flow, without fixed or floating behavior, and keeps the same centered, narrow-column layout as other content.

name: Make a Gift
title: Make a Gift
description: Page for creating a one-time gift, recurring gift, or pledge to a selected fund, with optional tribute information, donor contact details, and choice of payment or billing method.

core_features: Load available gift types, funds, tribute types, payment methods, and default currency via getGiftFormOptions and bind them to the form controls.
Initialize the form state (gift type, fund, amount, tribute type, tribute name) based on URL parameters when present and valid, falling back to defaults from getGiftFormOptions.
Allow users to enter or adjust the gift amount and select a fund/designation from the list of active funds.
Provide an optional tribute section where users can choose a tribute type and enter an honoree name when applicable.
Collect donor contact information including first name, last name, email, and optional postal code.
Allow users to select a payment or billing method from the options returned by getGiftFormOptions, including methods that are not processed online.
Require the donor to confirm terms/conditions and optionally opt in to receiving an email receipt.
Submit the completed gift or pledge via submitGiftPledge and handle the success or error response, including showing confirmation messaging.
Provide post-submission navigation options to return to the homepage (index.html) or view scholarships (scholarships.html).

user_workflows.name: Standard gift from main navigation (no preselected options)
steps: User opens give.html from the site header navigation without any URL parameters.
On page load, the client calls getGiftFormOptions.
After getGiftFormOptions returns, the page populates the gift type selector, fund list, tribute types, payment methods, and default currency.
The page sets initial form state using defaults from getGiftFormOptions (for example, default fund and currency, and a default gift type if provided or implied).
User selects a gift type if the default is not what they want.
User enters a gift amount in the amount field.
User selects a fund from the fund/designation dropdown.
User optionally opens the tribute section, selects a tribute type other than 'none', and enters an honoree name.
User enters donor first name, last name, email, and optionally postal code.
User selects a payment method from the available options (online or offline) returned by getGiftFormOptions.
User checks the required consent/terms checkbox and optionally the email receipt consent checkbox.
User submits the form; the client validates required fields and then calls submitGiftPledge with the current form values.
If submitGiftPledge returns success, the page displays a confirmation message using the returned gift data and message, and shows links to index.html and scholarships.html.
If submitGiftPledge indicates a failure or returns an error, the page displays error messaging and keeps the form data so the user can correct and resubmit.

user_workflows.name: Deep-linked gift to a specific fund and amount
steps: User clicks a giving call-to-action on another page (for example, index.html or scholarship_detail.html) that links to give.html with fund_id and amount URL parameters.
User lands on give.html?fund_id=...&amount=... (additional parameters like gift_type may or may not be present).
On page load, the client reads the URL parameters but does not apply them until getGiftFormOptions has returned the list of valid funds and gift types.
The client calls getGiftFormOptions and receives the available funds, gift types, tribute types, payment methods, and default currency.
The client validates the fund_id URL parameter against the returned funds; if it matches a fund, that fund is preselected in the fund selector, otherwise a default fund is selected.

The client validates the amount URL parameter (for example, checks that it is a number and greater than zero); if valid, the amount field is prefilled with that value; if not, the amount field remains empty or uses an appropriate default behavior.
If a gift_type URL parameter is present and matches one of the gift_types values returned by getGiftFormOptions, the corresponding gift type is preselected; otherwise, a default gift type is used.
User reviews the prefilled fund and amount and may adjust them as desired.
User completes the remaining fields (tribute, contact information, payment method, consents) and submits the form, which calls submitGiftPledge.
The success or error handling follows the standard flow (confirmation or error message, with navigation options).

user_workflows.name: Tribute gift deep link from a scholarship detail page
steps: User clicks a 'Make a gift in honor of' link on scholarship_detail.html which links to give.html with URL parameters including fund_id, tribute_type, and tribute_name (and optionally amount).
User lands on give.html with parameters such as fund_id, tribute_type, tribute_name, and optionally amount.
On page load, the client reads the URL parameters, then calls getGiftFormOptions.
After getGiftFormOptions returns, the client validates fund_id against the returned funds and preselects it if valid; otherwise a default fund is used.
The client validates tribute_type against the list of tribute_types from getGiftFormOptions; if it matches a value, it is preselected; otherwise, a default (such as 'none') is used.
If tribute_type is a valid non-'none' value and tribute_name is present, the honoree name field is prefilled with tribute_name.
If an amount parameter is provided and valid, the gift amount field is prefilled; otherwise it remains empty or uses default behavior.
User reviews and possibly edits the tribute type and honoree name, completes the remaining form fields (amount if not prefilled, donor details, payment method, consents), and submits the form.
The client calls submitGiftPledge with all collected data and then displays either a confirmation or error in the same way as other workflows.

user_workflows.name: Using offline payment methods (e.g., bill me later or pay by mail)
steps: User opens give.html (via any path) and the page initializes using getGiftFormOptions and any URL parameters.
User fills out gift amount, fund, optional tribute information, and donor contact information.
User selects a payment method that is marked as not online (is_online_payment false) in the payment_methods list returned by getGiftFormOptions.

If needed, the page reveals or activates a billing instructions text area for the user to provide additional offline payment instructions (for example, preferred billing address or internal reference).
User confirms the required terms and optionally consents to email receipt, then submits the form.
The client calls submitGiftPledge with payment_method set to the selected offline method and billing_instructions populated if provided.

On success, the page displays confirmation, including any generic instructions appropriate for offline payments (for example, that the institution will follow up), and provides links to index.html and scholarships.html.

interactions: On page load, call getGiftFormOptions and, once resolved, initialize selectors and fields (gift types, fund list, tribute types, payment methods, default currency) before the user interacts with the form.
On detecting URL parameters (fund_id, amount, gift_type, tribute_type, tribute_name), apply them only after getGiftFormOptions has returned so they can be validated against the available options.

On change of the gift type selector, update the internal form state for gift_type; the selection may influence contextual help or messaging but does not alter which core fields are required, since all fields submitted map to the GiftPledge model.
On change of the fund selector, update the internal fund_id state that will be passed to submitGiftPledge.
On input in the amount field, update the amount state and ensure that only valid numeric values are allowed before enabling form submission.

On change of tribute type, update tribute_type state; when the value indicates a tribute (not 'none'), keep the tribute name field enabled; when it is 'none', the tribute name field may be cleared or disabled.
On input in the tribute name field, update tribute_name state so it is available for submission when tribute_type is not 'none'.
On input in donor first name, last name, email, and postal code fields, update the corresponding donor_* state values and validate required fields locally prior to submission.
On change of payment method selection, update payment_method state and toggle visibility/enabled state of the billing instructions field when the selected method is not processed online.
On change of the required terms consent checkbox, update consent_terms state and prevent submission while it is not checked.
On change of the optional email receipt consent checkbox, update consent_email_receipt state.

On form submit, perform client-side validation of required fields and then call submitGiftPledge with the current state values; while awaiting the response, set a submitting state to disable the submit button and prevent duplicate submissions.

On successful submitGiftPledge response, display the returned message and gift summary details; switch the main view into a confirmation state where the form is either hidden or locked and only summary, message, and navigation links are interactive.
On failed submitGiftPledge response or network error, display an error message without clearing the form so that the user can adjust their input and submit again.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page maintains a primary form state object containing fields corresponding to the submitGiftPledge parameters (gift_type, amount, currency, fund_id, tribute_type, tribute_name, donor_first_name, donor_last_name, donor_email, donor_postal_code, payment_method, billing_instructions, consent_terms, consent_email_receipt), plus UI states such as loading, submitting, and submission_result. On initial load, the page reads URL parameters (fund_id, amount, gift_type, tribute_type, tribute_name) but does not immediately apply them; instead, it first calls getGiftFormOptions. After getGiftFormOptions returns, the page uses the response to validate and apply URL parameters: if the fund_id parameter matches a fund from funds, it is set as fund_id in the form state; otherwise, fund_id is set to default_fund_id from the response or another appropriate default from the funds list. If the amount parameter is present and is a valid positive number, it is used to initialize amount; otherwise, amount remains unset or uses default behavior. If the gift_type parameter matches one of the gift_types provided by getGiftFormOptions, gift_type in the state is initialized to that value; otherwise, it uses a default gift type (for example, the first entry in gift_types). Similarly, if tribute_type matches a tribute_types.value entry, the state tribute_type is initialized to it; if it is not valid or missing, tribute_type defaults to a non-tribute value such as 'none'. If tribute_type is a tribute value and tribute_name is provided in the URL, tribute_name is initialized with that string. Default_currency from getGiftFormOptions is used to initialize the currency field for submission. Throughout the user's interaction, changes to inputs and selectors update the corresponding fields in the form state, overwriting any initial values sourced from URL parameters. The page does not persist state outside the current page load; it relies exclusively on the current URL parameters and in-memory state, without using localStorage or other client-side storage. After submitGiftPledge returns, the page sets submission_result (e.g., success boolean, returned GiftPledge object, and message) and switches the visible UI from the editable form state to a confirmation or error state. Navigation links in the confirmation state navigate to index.html or scholarships.html using standard URL-based navigation, without passing additional parameters by default.

components.functionality: Overall controller for loading form options, initializing form state from getGiftFormOptions and URL parameters, rendering the gift form sections, and orchestrating submission via submitGiftPledge.
event_handlers: onInit (calls getGiftFormOptions, then applies URL parameters to initialize state)
onFormChange (updates the central form state when any child component changes)
onSubmit (validates state and calls submitGiftPledge with current form values)
onSubmitSuccess (updates submission_result state, triggers confirmation view)
onSubmitError (records error information and keeps the form editable)

components.functionality: Displays the available gift types as options (for example, one-time, recurring, or pledge), sourced from getGiftFormOptions.gift_types, and lets the user choose the gift_type to be submitted.
event_handlers: onInit (populates options from getGiftFormOptions.response.gift_types and applies gift_type URL parameter or default selection)
onChange (updates the form state's gift_type field in the parent gift-form-container)

components.functionality: Presents the list of available funds/designations from getGiftFormOptions.funds, allows the user to select a fund, and manages preselection based on the fund_id URL parameter or the default_fund_id.
event_handlers: onInit (initializes option list from getGiftFormOptions.response.funds and sets selected fund based on validated fund_id URL parameter or default_fund_id)
onChange (updates the form state's fund_id when the user selects a different fund)

components.functionality: Captures the monetary amount of the gift or pledge, optionally prefilled from the amount URL parameter if valid, and labeled or formatted using the default currency from getGiftFormOptions.
event_handlers: onInit (sets the currency from default_currency and applies amount from URL parameters if it is a valid number; otherwise leaves it unset or defaulted)
onInput (updates the form state's amount and may perform live validation such as numeric checks and non-negative constraints)

components.functionality: Manages tribute-related fields, including a tribute type selector and an optional tribute name field, with initial values optionally derived from tribute_type and tribute_name URL parameters.
event_handlers: onInit (populates tribute type options from getGiftFormOptions.response.tribute_types and sets initial tribute_type and tribute_name based on validated URL parameters)
onTributeTypeChange (updates the form state's tribute_type and toggles the enabled/required state of the tribute name field depending on whether the selected type is a tribute or 'none')
onTributeNameInput (updates the form state's tribute_name when the user types an honoree name)

components.functionality: Collects required donor identity information (first name, last name, email) and optional postal code to populate the GiftPledge donor_* fields.
event_handlers: onFirstNameInput (updates donor_first_name in the form state)
onLastNameInput (updates donor_last_name in the form state)
onEmailInput (updates donor_email in the form state and may perform basic email format validation)
onPostalCodeInput (updates donor_postal_code in the form state)

components.functionality: Displays available payment or billing methods from getGiftFormOptions.payment_methods, lets the user choose a payment_method, and conditionally exposes a billing instructions field when the chosen method is not processed online.
event_handlers: onInit (loads payment method options from getGiftFormOptions.response.payment_methods and applies any default behavior required by the institution)
onPaymentMethodChange (updates payment_method in the form state and toggles the visibility and/or requirement of billing_instructions depending on is_online_payment for the selected method)
onBillingInstructionsInput (updates billing_instructions in the form state when the user provides optional offline payment details)

components.functionality: Manages the mandatory terms/conditions consent and optional email receipt consent required to complete the gift or pledge.
event_handlers: onTermsCheckboxChange (updates consent_terms in the form state and may enable or disable the submit button based on whether the terms are accepted)
onEmailReceiptCheckboxChange (updates consent_email_receipt in the form state)

components.functionality: Provides a read-only summary of the current form state (gift type, amount, currency, fund, tribute, donor contact, and payment method) to help the user review before submitting and to display the confirmed details after a successful submission.
event_handlers: onStateChange (re-renders the summary whenever any of the form state fields change)
onSubmitSuccess (switches to showing the confirmed values from the gift object returned by submitGiftPledge instead of the editable form state)

components.functionality: Shows loading, error, or success messages based on the status of getGiftFormOptions and submitGiftPledge operations, including the message returned by submitGiftPledge when available.
event_handlers: onInit (shows a loading state while getGiftFormOptions is in progress and transitions to normal form display on success or an error state on failure)
onSubmitStart (shows a submitting/processing state and disables form resubmission)
onSubmitSuccess (displays the success message from submitGiftPledge.response.message and may indicate that the gift or pledge has been recorded)
onSubmitError (displays an error message when submitGiftPledge fails and allows the user to try again)

components.functionality: Provides post-submission navigation options, including links back to index.html (Return to Home) and scholarships.html (View scholarships), visible only after a successful submission.
event_handlers: onSubmitSuccess (makes the navigation links visible when submitGiftPledge.response.success is true)
onClickReturnHome (navigates to index.html without additional parameters)
onClickViewScholarships (navigates to scholarships.html without additional parameters)

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a full-width header and navigation, and a centered content column spanning approximately 6–8 columns with large side gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing red utility bar and black main navigation, the page continues the mockup’s linear, letter‑like flow: a large vertical gap, then a single, centered content column that holds the entire Make a Gift form. All form sections stack vertically in one column, with clear, editorial section breaks rather than card-like blocks. The form reads from top to bottom like a structured letter: gift basics first (type, fund, amount), then optional tribute information, then donor contact, payment, terms, and finally a compact review and confirmation links.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing red utility bar and black main navigation, the page continues the mockup’s linear, letter‑like flow: a large vertical gap, then a single, centered content column that holds the entire Make a Gift form. All form sections stack vertically in one column, with clear, editorial section breaks rather than card-like blocks. The form reads from top to bottom like a structured letter: gift basics first (type, fund, amount), then optional tribute information, then donor contact, payment, terms, and finally a compact review and confirmation links.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The red utility bar, black navigation bar, and slim sub‑nav together occupy roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport. The remaining 75–80% is a white canvas with a narrow, centered form column that spans about 35–45% of the viewport width (e.g., max-width ~720px), with large left and right gutters. There is a substantial top margin (about 56–64px) between the bottom of the navigation region and the start of the Make a Gift form, echoing the gap before ‘Dear Alumni’ in the mockup. Major form sections are separated by 32–40px of vertical whitespace, while related fields within a section use 16–24px spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: The form uses a low-to-moderate density that keeps the letter‑like, editorial feel. Labels sit above fields, and fields are full‑width within the central column. Only when necessary (e.g., first/last name pairs) are two fields placed side by side within that column, and even then they maintain generous horizontal padding. There are no sidebars, cards, or heavy backgrounds; instead, section titles, consistent spacing, and occasional subtle rules or dividers define structure. Overall, the form feels calm and uncluttered despite the amount of information.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows a strict top-to-bottom path: bright red utility bar at the very top, then the black navigation bar with the active section underlined, then down past the sub‑nav row to the large white area. After a deliberate vertical gap, the centered Make a Gift title and brief introductory text anchor attention at the top of the form container. From there, the eye moves down through the stacked sections in narrative order: gift type, fund, amount, optional tribute, donor contact, payment, terms/consent, and finally the review summary and submission. The submission status banner, when present, appears directly above the form title within the same central column, momentarily becoming the focal point before the reader continues downward. Confirmation navigation links at the very bottom provide the final exit points, echoing the closing lines of a formal letter.

layout.overall_description: After the institutional red utility bar and black navigation bar, a generous band of white space creates a visual reset before the Make a Gift content begins. Centered on this white canvas is a single, relatively narrow column—about two-fifths of the viewport width—that feels like a formal page laid on a desk. At the top of this column, aligned with the central axis, sits the Make a Gift heading, followed by a short, left-aligned explanatory line. Below, the gift form unfolds in a strictly vertical sequence: starting with basic gift configuration (type, fund, amount) laid out as clean, full-width controls; then deeper details such as tribute options, donor contact information, and payment method, each introduced by a simple section heading with ample breathing room above it. Near the bottom, the terms and receipt consent lines stand alone with increased spacing, followed by a concise review summary and the main submission control. If there is a submission status message, it appears as a horizontal strip embedded at the top of this column, just under the navigation’s shadow, without breaking the centered composition. Finally, at the very bottom of the column, a few understated text links guide donors to post-submission destinations, like returning to the alumni homepage or viewing other ways to give. The whole layout is quiet, linear, and editorial, with large side gutters and consistent vertical rhythm defining its structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gift-form-container sits in the main content area directly beneath the stacked header/navigation region. There is a substantial vertical gap of approximately 56–64px from the bottom edge of the black navigation bar/sub-nav to the top of this container, mirroring the space before the ‘Dear Alumni’ text in the mockup. The container itself forms a single centered column on a pure white background, spanning about 35–45% of the viewport width (for example, max-width around 680–720px) and centered horizontally, leaving large, empty gutters on the left and right. All other form components are vertically stacked inside this container, aligned to a common left text edge, with consistent inner vertical spacing (16–24px between fields, 32–40px between major sections). The overall impression is of a formal, letter-like page of content placed in the middle of a wider canvas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block container within the normal document flow; remains vertically scrollable with the page, always centered within the white content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the gift-form-container, the gift-type-selector appears near the top, immediately below the page title and any brief introductory sentence, with a vertical gap of about 24–32px separating it from that text. It spans the full width of the central column, maintaining the same left alignment as the heading. The label (e.g., ‘Gift Type’) sits above the selector with 8–12px of spacing. The options (e.g., One-time, Recurring, Pledge) are arranged horizontally in a single row where space allows, each option padded with roughly 16px horizontal and 8–12px vertical padding, and equal space between them, giving a segmented-control feel without heavy borders. On narrower viewports, the options wrap into multiple lines but remain full-width within the column. The entire selector block reads as a concise, early decision point before the rest of the form unfolds.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal vertical flow; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fund-designation-selector is placed directly beneath the gift-type-selector with a moderate vertical gap of about 24–32px, keeping it clearly grouped with the ‘gift setup’ portion of the form. It is full-width within the central column, aligned with the same left edge. A clear, text-only label (e.g., ‘Fund Designation’) appears above the control with 8–12px of space. The selector itself (dropdown or searchable field) is a single, clean horizontal bar with subtle border and 12–16px internal padding, matching the width of other fields. If additional helper text is needed (e.g., ‘You may search by fund name’), it sits directly below the field in a smaller type, separated by about 4–8px, maintaining a vertical stack that feels like a paragraph of instructions followed by a line in a letter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow block element; adjusts with the central column’s width on responsive layouts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gift-amount-input appears next in the sequence, roughly 24–32px below the fund-designation-selector, continuing the strongly vertical narrative. It occupies the full width of the central column and aligns under the same left edge as prior labels. The label (e.g., ‘Gift Amount’) is placed above the field, separated by 8–12px. The numeric input itself is prominent but not oversized: a single-line field with about 12–16px of internal padding and a subtle border, possibly prefixed with a currency symbol. If preset amount buttons are offered, they appear as a row of small, pill-like buttons directly beneath the field, each with about 8px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding and even spacing between them. The vertical spacing around this whole block remains consistent with other fields so it feels integrated into the textual flow, but its position as the last piece of the ‘gift basics’ trio (type, fund, amount) gives it natural prominence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow element; no special behavior beyond responsive width adjustments.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tribute-options-section is introduced as a new, clearly distinct block, positioned about 32–40px beneath the gift-amount-input to create a noticeable section break. Within the central column, it spans the same full width, but it may be visually framed by a simple section heading such as ‘Tribute Information (Optional)’ set in slightly bolder text, with 8–12px of space below before the fields begin. All tribute-related fields (e.g., honoree name, occasion, notification recipient) are stacked vertically with 16–24px between each. For shorter paired fields, two can be placed side-by-side within the column, each occupying roughly 48% of the column width with an 8–16px gap between them, while still aligning their labels above the inputs. No heavy boxes or backgrounds are used; the section is defined primarily by its heading and the extra vertical margins above and below (32–40px to the previous and next sections), so it reads like a new paragraph block in a formal document.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normal block-level flow; expands vertically as tribute fields are revealed (e.g., if toggled on) but does not affect the central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donor-contact-section follows the tribute-options-section, separated by a consistent 32–40px vertical gap that signals a new major section. It remains within the same centered column, full-width, and starts with a section label like ‘Your Contact Information’ in the same style as other section titles, left-aligned. Under this heading, fields such as name, email, phone, and address are arranged in an orderly vertical stack. For basic identity fields (e.g., First Name, Last Name), two inputs may be placed side-by-side in a single row, each taking about half of the column width, with 8–16px between them, while more complex fields like street address span the column’s full width. Vertical spacing between rows is about 16–24px, ensuring the section feels thorough but breathable. This section occupies a visually substantial but not crowded portion of the central column, echoing the body of a letter that introduces the sender.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow section; grows vertically according to the number of fields but does not alter the single-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-method-section is placed approximately 32–40px below the donor-contact-section, marking the transition from informational data entry to transaction details. It runs the full width of the central column and begins with a clear heading such as ‘Payment Method’ aligned to the left, with 8–12px spacing beneath. If multiple methods are available (e.g., credit card, bank transfer, payroll deduction), they are displayed as a vertical list of radio options, each occupying the full column width with 12–16px vertical padding, ensuring legibility and a calm rhythm. When a method is selected, its associated fields appear directly beneath that option, indented slightly or simply aligned under the selector, preserving the straight left margin and adding 16–24px of vertical spacing around the revealed block. The entire section visually resembles a structured, orderly paragraph of choices rather than a dense payment widget.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow; expanding/collapsing behavior within the section as different payment options reveal their related fields, but no fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-and-receipt-consent-section appears near the bottom of the central column, about 32–40px beneath the payment-method-section, visually separated to highlight its importance. It is compact but given extra white space above and below to stand on its own, similar to a closing note in a letter. The main consent checkbox (e.g., ‘I agree to the terms and conditions’) is placed on a single line or wrapped neatly within the column, with the checkbox aligned to the left margin of other fields. Any linked terms text appears inline or just below in a smaller size, with about 4–8px of spacing. Additional consent items (e.g., email receipt, communication preferences) stack vertically with 12–16px between them. The section uses no heavy borders; it relies on spacing and the critical nature of the text to draw focus before submission.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow block; maintains its relative distance from preceding and following components as the form height changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gift-review-summary sits toward the end of the form, directly following the terms-and-receipt-consent-section with a moderate gap of about 24–32px, acting as the final recap before the user submits. It occupies the full width of the central column but may be visually distinguished with a very subtle treatment—such as a light gray background or a thin top border—while still feeling flat and minimal. Inside, key details (gift type, fund, amount, tribute, and selected payment method) are presented in a simple vertical list, with labels on the left and values either directly below or aligned in a two-column label–value format, using 8–12px spacing between lines and 16–24px between grouped items. The summary height is kept modest so it doesn’t overwhelm the column; it reads like a concise closing paragraph restating the main points of the ‘letter’ before signing. Immediately beneath this summary, the primary submit button is centered or left-aligned within the same column with about 16–24px of spacing, maintaining the clean vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In normal document flow near the bottom of the form; no sticky behavior to preserve the simple, editorial scrolling experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-status-banner appears conditionally at the top of the gift-form-container, just below the large vertical gap that follows the navigation, and directly above the Make a Gift heading. It spans the full width of the central column but not the entire viewport, maintaining the same centered alignment as the form. The banner is a low-profile horizontal strip with about 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding inside, using a background color that contrasts gently with the white content area (e.g., a pale green for success or soft red for error) while still feeling consistent with the institutional palette. Text is left-aligned within the banner and kept to one or two short lines. There is about 16–24px of space between the bottom edge of the banner and the form title, so the title still feels anchored and the banner reads like a brief, inline status line in the editorial flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears within the normal flow; pushes the content below it down when visible. It does not stick to the viewport edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation-navigation-links component sits at the very bottom of the central column, below the gift-review-summary and submit button or below any post-submission confirmation message, separated from the preceding element by about 32–40px of white space. The links are left-aligned, matching the column’s text edge, and presented as a short vertical list of understated text links (e.g., ‘Return to Alumni Home’, ‘View Other Ways to Give’), with 8–12px of spacing between each. There is generous white space beneath the last link (another 32–48px) before the end of the content area, reinforcing the feeling of a formal letter’s closing lines and leaving the bottom of the page visually quiet.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow component; remains at the bottom of the content column and scrolls normally with the page.

name: Stay Connected
title: Stay Connected
description: Page that retrieves and displays dynamic alumni engagement channels and routes users to the appropriate detail pages (newsletters, chapters, events, mentoring) based on their selection and URL parameters.

core_features: Call getStayConnectedOverview on page load to retrieve an introduction text and a dynamic list of engagement channels.
Render all returned channels in a structured list/grid using the channel properties (code, title, description) without hardcoded channel labels or descriptions.
Interpret the optional URL parameter `highlight` to determine which channel should be emphasized or focused when the page loads.
Provide a primary action for the email newsletter channel (identified by its channel.code) that navigates to newsletter_subscribe.html with the required source parameter.
Provide primary actions for other channels (such as local chapters, events, and mentoring) that navigate to their respective detail pages, based on the channel.code.
Maintain internal page state for the currently highlighted/selected channel and update the UI (e.g., focus, scroll, emphasis) accordingly.
user_workflows:
1. User arrives on stay_connected.html (optionally with a `highlight` URL parameter).
2. The page initializes and the data loader component calls getStayConnectedOverview with no parameters.
3. When the interface responds, the page stores the returned introduction text and channels array in page state.
4. The highlight-state controller reads the `highlight` URL parameter, compares it to the channel.code values in the loaded channels, and marks the matching channel (if any) as highlighted in state.
5. The channel list component renders all channels using the dynamic title and description from the interface, visually emphasizing the channel that matches the highlight state.
6. The user reviews the introduction and scans the list of engagement channels to understand available ways to stay connected.

1. From any other page (e.g., index.html), the user clicks a link to stay_connected.html that may include a `highlight=email_newsletters` parameter.
2. stay_connected.html loads, calls getStayConnectedOverview, and sets the returned data in state.
3. The highlight-state controller detects that `highlight` equals `email_newsletters` and finds the corresponding channel in the channels array.
4. The channel list component scrolls or focuses on the email newsletter channel and marks it as highlighted using the channel.code.
5. The user activates the primary action for that channel (e.g., by clicking a button associated with this channel).
6. The navigation controller builds a URL to newsletter_subscribe.html including the query parameter `source=stay_connected_email_newsletters` and redirects the browser to that URL.

1. A user is interested in local alumni groups and follows a link to stay_connected.html with `highlight=local_chapters` (or arrives without the parameter and finds the local chapters channel in the list).
2. The page loads, calls getStayConnectedOverview, and renders the channels.
3. The highlight-state controller either highlights the local chapters channel (if the `highlight` parameter matches its channel.code) or leaves all channels unhighlighted if no parameter or no match.
4. The user clicks the primary action on the local chapters channel card in the channel list.
5. The navigation controller inspects the channel.code, resolves that this channel should open chapters.html, and redirects the browser there without additional parameters.

1. A user navigates to stay_connected.html to find activities and events.
2. getStayConnectedOverview returns channels that may include an events-related channel identified by its channel.code.
3. The user scrolls to the events-related channel, reading the dynamic title and description.
4. The user clicks the channel's primary action (e.g., an "attend" or "view" action generated from the channel data).
5. The navigation controller maps the events-related channel.code to events.html and navigates the browser to that URL.

1. A user visits stay_connected.html to learn about mentoring and volunteering.
2. After getStayConnectedOverview resolves, the user locates the mentoring-related channel in the channel list.
3. The user clicks the primary action for that channel.
4. The navigation controller maps the mentoring-related channel.code to mentoring.html and redirects the browser there.

1. A user lands on stay_connected.html without URL parameters.
2. The page loads and calls getStayConnectedOverview.
3. Once data is loaded, the highlight-state controller finds no `highlight` parameter and sets the highlighted channel state to null (or a default that does not depend on static channel assumptions).
4. The user browses the channels and can click different channel cards.
5. Each click updates the internal selected/highlighted state to the clicked channel.code, so subsequent UI behaviors (focus, detail, or emphasis) respond to the user's selection without manipulating localStorage.
interactions: On page load, a data loader interaction triggers a call to getStayConnectedOverview to populate the page with the current introduction and engagement channels.
On completion of getStayConnectedOverview, the page sets internal state with the introduction string and the channels array and triggers a re-render of dependent components.

On each change of the URL (initial load and subsequent in-app navigations), the highlight-state controller reads the `highlight` parameter and updates the highlighted channel state by matching it against channel.code values from the loaded channels.

When a user clicks a channel card (anywhere on the card surface), the channel list component calls an event handler with the clicked channel.code to set that code as the currently selected or highlighted channel in the page state.

When a user activates the primary action associated with the email newsletter channel (identified by its channel.code), the navigation controller constructs a URL to newsletter_subscribe.html with the query parameter `source=stay_connected_email_newsletters` and performs a client-side redirect.

When a user activates the primary action for a chapters-related channel (identified by its channel.code), the navigation controller constructs a URL to chapters.html (without additional parameters required by this specification) and redirects the browser.
When a user activates the primary action for an events-related channel (identified by its channel.code), the navigation controller constructs a URL to events.html and redirects the browser.
When a user activates the primary action for a mentoring-related channel (identified by its channel.code), the navigation controller constructs a URL to mentoring.html and redirects the browser.

If the channels array includes codes that do not have a predefined navigation mapping, clicking their primary action triggers a fallback interaction (for example, no navigation, or navigation defined elsewhere in the application) governed by the navigation controller, without introducing hardcoded content.

Keyboard navigation and accessibility interactions (e.g., focusing the highlighted channel on load, moving focus between channel cards, and activating actions via keyboard) are tied to the same event handlers as mouse/touch interactions and use channel.code-based state to keep behavior consistent.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state input to this page is the `highlight` URL parameter, which is read from the query string of stay_connected.html. When the page loads, the data loader calls getStayConnectedOverview and stores its response in page-level state, including an `introduction` value and a `channels` array. After the channels are available, the highlight-state controller checks the URL parameter `highlight`. If `highlight` is present, it searches the channels array for an object whose channel.code exactly matches the parameter value. If a match is found, a `highlightedChannelCode` state property is set to that code; if no match is found or if `highlight` is absent, `highlightedChannelCode` is set to null (or a non-highlighted default). Components that render channels (e.g., channel-card and channel-list) use `highlightedChannelCode` to determine which channel should be visually emphasized and programmatically focused. Page state also tracks `selectedChannelCode`, which is updated when a user manually clicks on a channel card; this can either mirror `highlightedChannelCode` or be maintained separately, depending on implementation. No state is persisted in localStorage or other client storage; the source of truth for highlighting is always the current URL and the in-memory state derived from it. If the application's routing changes the `highlight` parameter while the user remains on the page (for example, via client-side navigation), the highlight-state controller re-runs the matching logic and updates `highlightedChannelCode` accordingly, causing a re-render of any dependent components.

components.functionality: Calls getStayConnectedOverview on initialization, stores the returned introduction and channels in page-level state, and exposes that state to child components that need it.
Manages the highlightedChannelCode and selectedChannelCode state properties based on the URL `highlight` parameter and user interactions with channel cards.
Displays the introduction text for the page using the introduction string returned by getStayConnectedOverview, without hardcoding any copy.

Iterates over the channels array returned by getStayConnectedOverview and renders a list or grid of channel-card components, passing each channel object and the current highlightedChannelCode and selectedChannelCode state to each card.

Represents a single engagement channel. Uses the dynamic channel.title and channel.description from getStayConnectedOverview for display and computes whether it is highlighted by comparing channel.code to highlightedChannelCode. Exposes click targets for selecting the card and for triggering the channel's primary action.

Resolves navigation targets when a channel's primary action is activated. For the channel whose channel.code corresponds to the email newsletters channel, it navigates to newsletter_subscribe.html adding `source=stay_connected_email_newsletters` to the query string. For channels whose codes correspond to local chapters, events, or mentoring, it navigates respectively to chapters.html, events.html, or mentoring.html. For any other channel.code values, it can either perform no navigation or use application-defined routing rules, without introducing hardcoded descriptive content.

Reads the `highlight` URL parameter on initial page load and on any client-side route changes, informing the highlight-state controller whenever the parameter changes so that highlightedChannelCode is kept in sync with the URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a full-width header and navigation, and a centered content column spanning approximately 6–8 columns with large side gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: All page-specific content sits below the existing red utility bar and black navigation bar. Within the white content area, everything is arranged in a simple top-to-bottom sequence inside a single, centered column: introduction text at the top, then the list of engagement channels, with each channel presented as its own editorial-style block. There are no sidebars or secondary columns; all components share the same central column to preserve the formal, letter-like reading experience.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All page-specific content sits below the existing red utility bar and black navigation bar. Within the white content area, everything is arranged in a simple top-to-bottom sequence inside a single, centered column: introduction text at the top, then the list of engagement channels, with each channel presented as its own editorial-style block. There are no sidebars or secondary columns; all components share the same central column to preserve the formal, letter-like reading experience.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header and navigation continue to occupy the top ~20–25% of the viewport. The alumni content for this page starts beneath them, in a white canvas that takes up the remaining ~75–80%. A centered content column spans roughly 35–45% of the viewport width (about 6–8 of 12 implicit columns), with large left and right gutters. The vertical gap between the bottom of navigation and the first line of the introduction is generous (around 56–64px). Between the introduction and the channel list there is a smaller but still spacious separation (around 32–40px). Individual paragraphs and channel items are spaced with 16–24px vertical gaps.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is deliberately low. The introduction and each channel card are treated like short, self-contained paragraphs on a formal letter, with ample white space around them, generous line height, and no heavy framing or graphics. Cards are flat, mostly white, with minimal borders or dividers to avoid visual clutter. Text wraps in a narrow measure to keep reading comfortable, and there are no dense multi-column layouts. Interactive elements like links appear as simple text lines, not heavy buttons.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first traverses the bright red utility bar and black main navigation (outside this layout), then descends into the white content area where a centered introduction heading and opening line act as the new anchor, similar to 'Dear Alumni,'. From there, the eye moves down through short paragraphs of copy. The channel list continues this vertical flow: each channel card reads as a concise editorial block, with a single prominent, blue action link within each card acting as the key focal point, analogous to the standalone LinkedIn URL in the mockup. If a channel is targeted via URL, a subtle highlight draws attention to that card without disrupting the linear reading flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the institutional red and black header bars, the page opens into a calm white canvas. Centered within this is a relatively narrow content column—about two-fifths of the viewport wide—with generous gutters on both sides so it feels like reading a printed letter on a clean sheet of paper. After a large vertical gap, the introduction block appears at the top of this column, with a simple heading and a few short paragraphs spaced evenly, setting a formal, editorial tone. Beneath it, separated by a modest white band, a vertical list of engagement channels continues down the same column. Each channel appears as a flat, white textual card that spans the full width of the central column, with its name, a brief description, and a single, clearly visible action link on its own line that routes the user to the appropriate detail page. The cards are stacked one after another with consistent spacing, no side-by-side layouts, and only very subtle dividers or emphasis marks, preserving an uncluttered, institutional feel. Invisible functional components—data loading, state highlighting, URL sync, and routing—operate behind the scenes, ensuring that when users arrive via a parameterized link or click on an action, the appropriate card is gently highlighted and the view scrolls so that card sits just below the navigation, all without breaking the quiet, top-to-bottom reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not occupy any visible space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the Stay Connected page’s content context, just below the global navigation structure, initializing data before any visible content is rendered. It does not introduce any padding, margins, or separators; the first visible element remains the introduction block, which still begins after the large 56–64px gap beneath the navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Hidden and non-rendering; it participates in the layout only logically, not visually, and therefore does not affect the flow or spacing of other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is also a non-visual controller, but its effects are visible on the channel-card components. It does not render any independent block or occupy its own row. Instead, it modulates the appearance of individual cards within the same centered content column—e.g., applying a subtle left-side accent (a 3–4px red vertical bar aligned with the card’s left edge) or a very light background tint inside the card’s existing bounds when a channel is active or targeted. The physical size and position of the cards remain the same; only their internal emphasis styling changes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Hidden and non-rendering; it modifies styles of channel-card elements without changing their dimensions or the vertical rhythm of the stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The introduction-block is the first visible component in the page’s content area. It sits centered horizontally in the white canvas, directly beneath the global navigation and any sub-nav row. From the bottom edge of the black navigation bar to the first text line of this block, there is a large vertical margin of approximately 56–64px, creating a clear, intentional separation. The block itself is constrained to a narrow content column that spans roughly 35–45% of the viewport width (for example, max-width around 640–720px) and is perfectly centered, leaving large white gutters on both sides. Within this block, the heading (e.g., 'Stay Connected') appears as the first line, left-aligned within the column, followed by one to three short paragraphs of body text. Each paragraph is separated by 16–24px of vertical space, and the line height is generous to reinforce the formal, letter-like feel. There is no card frame; the text appears directly on the white background, with only the consistent margins and alignment defining the block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, standard block-level behavior; it participates in the vertical flow, expanding in height based solely on its text content while remaining horizontally centered within the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The channel-list is positioned immediately below the introduction-block, sharing the same centered column and width. A distinct vertical gap of about 32–40px separates the bottom of the introduction text from the top of the first channel card, creating a clear grouping between introductory copy and actionable options. The list itself is a vertical stack of channel-card items, each occupying the full width of the central column. There is no grid or multi-column arrangement; cards are arranged in a single column from top to bottom. Between each card, there is a consistent vertical spacing of approximately 24–32px, maintaining the low-density, editorial rhythm. If a section title for the list is used (e.g., 'Engagement Channels'), it appears as a simple, left-aligned line at the top of the list, within the same column, with a modest 16–24px gap before the first card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and flowing; the list grows vertically as more channel-card items are present, always anchored to the same centered column and preserving consistent spacing between items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each channel-card appears as a minimal, flat, editorial-style block inside the central column defined by the channel-list. The card spans 100% of the column width (approximately 35–45% of the viewport), with no drop shadows and at most a very subtle 1px light gray border or a bottom divider line to delineate it. Inside the card, there is internal padding of roughly 16–24px on all sides. The channel name is displayed on the top line, left-aligned, with slightly stronger weight. Below it, after an 8–12px gap, a brief description is rendered as one or two short paragraphs with 12–16px spacing between them, matching the editorial spacing of the introduction. Further down, separated by about 12–16px of white space, a single primary action line appears: this is a blue text link (e.g., 'View details', 'Join the LinkedIn group') that runs on its own line, left-aligned but visually distinct due to color and possibly a subtle underline, mirroring the standalone LinkedIn URL focal moment from the mockup. The card has ample white space beneath this link before its bottom edge, maintaining a calm, uncluttered appearance. When highlighted by the highlight-state-controller, a narrow red bar on the left edge or a light background tint is applied inside the card’s existing footprint, without changing its size or pushing surrounding elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block elements within the list; they expand vertically based on content but retain consistent width and alignment within the centered column, stacking top-to-bottom with uniform spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Visually, this controller manifests as the action line within each channel-card. It does not exist as a separate block on the page; instead, it is bound to a single line of text near the bottom of each card, typically the blue link that users click to navigate to newsletters, chapters, events, mentoring, or external destinations. This link line sits within the card’s padding, left-aligned with the card’s text, and occupies only one line of content width (no full-width button styling). There is around 12–16px of space above it (separating it from the description) and 8–16px below it (to the card’s bottom padding), giving it breathing room and allowing it to stand out subtly in the otherwise monochrome text block. When the user clicks this line, navigation occurs, but the page’s spatial structure remains unchanged.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the channel-card’s text flow; it behaves as an inline-block or link-level element that does not alter the layout beyond its single line of space, but triggers routing logic behind the scenes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This helper is non-visual and does not render any element on the page. Its effect is felt in how the layout is presented when the page loads or when the URL changes with parameters. When a specific channel is targeted via URL parameters, it ensures that the corresponding channel-card is scrolled into view so that its top aligns just below the bottom of the global navigation and the standard top margin to content is respected (the card appears at or slightly below the position where the introduction-block would normally begin). It also coordinates with the highlight-state-controller to visually emphasize the relevant card within its existing placement. No additional spacing, containers, or headers are introduced by this component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Hidden and non-rendering; it influences scroll position programmatically but does not occupy or reserve any physical space in the document flow.

name: Email Newsletter Subscription
title: Email Newsletter Subscription
description: Page for alumni and friends to subscribe to the university alumni email newsletters, review existing preferences, and update topics, frequency, and consent.

core_features: Load available newsletter topics, frequency choices, school/faculty options, and any existing subscription using getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState.
Display a subscription form that collects email address, first name, last name, graduation year, and school/faculty.
Allow users to select and deselect newsletter topics based on the dynamic list returned from getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState.
Allow users to select a delivery frequency option from the dynamic list returned from getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState.
Interpret URL parameters (source, topics, frequency) to preconfigure the form state for messaging and default selections.
Require explicit consent to receive email communications and prevent submission unless consent is provided.
Create or update the newsletter subscription and topic preferences by calling upsertNewsletterSubscription on form submission.
Provide clear success and error feedback after attempting to save subscription preferences.
Offer navigation back to the Stay Connected page (with highlight parameter) or to the site home page after saving.

user_workflows.name: New subscriber arriving from Stay Connected with pre-selected interests
steps: User clicks an email newsletter link on stay_connected.html that opens newsletter_subscribe.html with source, topics, and frequency URL parameters.
Page initialization component reads URL parameters and calls getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState.
When getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState resolves, the form is populated: topics and frequency options are listed; no existing subscription is found (existing_subscription is null).
The component parses the topics URL parameter into topic codes and matches them to the topics returned from getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState. Matching topics are pre-selected in the topic selector.
The component parses the frequency URL parameter and, if it matches one of the returned frequency_options values, that frequency option is pre-selected.
User fills in required personal information fields (email, first name, last name) and optional fields (graduation year, school) and reviews the pre-selected topics and frequency.
User checks the required consent checkbox indicating consent_email_communications is true.

User submits the form. The form component validates required fields and consent, then calls upsertNewsletterSubscription with the entered personal data, selected topic IDs, chosen frequency, and consent flag.

If upsertNewsletterSubscription returns success, the page shows a success message and offers navigation options: a link back to stay_connected.html with highlight=email_newsletters and a link to index.html.
If the user clicks “Back to Stay Connected,” the browser navigates to stay_connected.html?highlight=email_newsletters. If the user clicks “Return to Home,” the browser navigates to index.html.

user_workflows.name: Existing subscriber updating preferences from a generic link (no URL topics/frequency)
steps: User clicks a generic newsletter subscription link (e.g., in the site footer) that opens newsletter_subscribe.html without topics or frequency URL parameters.
Page initialization component calls getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState.
When data is returned, the component detects existing_subscription is not null and populates the form with the subscription's email, name, graduation year, school, frequency, and selected topics.
All topics from the API are listed; those whose IDs match existing_subscription.selected_topic_ids are pre-selected.
The frequency selector is set to existing_subscription.subscription.frequency.
User updates selections: they can change topics (select/deselect) and choose a different frequency; they can also edit personal information fields if desired.
Consent checkbox is displayed and pre-checked only if existing_subscription.subscription.consent_email_communications is true. If it is false or missing, the user must check it before submitting.
User submits the form. The form validates the fields and consent, then calls upsertNewsletterSubscription with updated values.
On success, a confirmation message indicates that preferences have been updated. Navigation links are available to return to stay_connected.html (with highlight=email_newsletters) or index.html.
On failure, an error message is shown and the user can correct input or retry submission without losing their current form entries.

user_workflows.name: User following a targeted link that updates only frequency
steps: User clicks a link that opens newsletter_subscribe.html with a frequency URL parameter but without a topics parameter.
Page initialization component reads URL parameters and calls getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState.
If an existing subscription is found, the component sets the initial topic selections from existing_subscription.selected_topic_ids.

The component then checks the frequency URL parameter: if it matches any frequency_options.value, that option is set as the currently selected frequency; otherwise, the existing subscription frequency is used.
User reviews pre-loaded information and confirms or adjusts topics and personal details as needed.
User verifies or provides consent, then submits the form, triggering upsertNewsletterSubscription.
On success, the page confirms that the subscription and its frequency have been updated and shows navigation options back to Stay Connected or Home.

user_workflows.name: User arriving with invalid or unsupported URL parameters
steps: User opens newsletter_subscribe.html with topics and/or frequency URL parameters that do not match any available topics or frequency options from getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState.
After getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState returns, the component attempts to match the provided topics codes to available topics. None (or only some) may match.

Only valid matches are used for initial topic selection; invalid codes are ignored without breaking the page. If no matches are found and there is an existing subscription, existing selected topics are used. If there is no existing subscription and no valid matches, no topics are pre-selected.

The component attempts to match the frequency URL parameter to an available frequency_options.value. If no match is found and an existing subscription exists, it uses the subscription's frequency; if there is no existing subscription, it uses a default derived from frequency_options (for example, the first entry in the list).
User proceeds to complete or update the form as normal and submits, triggering upsertNewsletterSubscription.
Success or error feedback is displayed accordingly.

interactions: On page load, the initialization logic reads URL parameters (source, topics, frequency) and calls getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState. Until the response is received, the main form components remain disabled or in a loading state.

When getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState resolves, the topics list component binds to the topics array and renders a selectable control for each topic. Checking or unchecking a topic updates the in-memory array of selected topic IDs.

The frequency selector binds to frequency_options and renders an appropriate control (for example, radio buttons or a dropdown). Selecting a frequency sets the selected frequency value in the form state.
Personal information fields (email, first name, last name, graduation year, school) are bound to corresponding input controls; changes to inputs immediately update the internal form state.
The school selector binds to school_options and renders a list of options; the selected value is stored as the school field used when calling upsertNewsletterSubscription.

The consent checkbox is bound to the consent_email_communications boolean in the form state. The submit button remains disabled or triggers a validation error if consent_email_communications is not true when the user attempts submission.

Clicking the primary submit button triggers client-side validation of required fields (at minimum email, frequency, and consent). If validation passes, the component calls upsertNewsletterSubscription with the current form state.
While upsertNewsletterSubscription is in progress, the submit button is disabled and a pending state is indicated so users cannot submit multiple times.
If upsertNewsletterSubscription returns success=false or an error, an error message is set in a feedback state and displayed above or within the form without clearing the user’s current input.
Clicking the "Back to Stay Connected" control navigates the user to stay_connected.html with a query parameter highlight=email_newsletters.
Clicking the "Return to Home" control navigates the user to index.html without additional parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is determined by a combination of URL parameters, data returned from getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState, and the user's in-form interactions. 1) URL parameter handling: On initial load, the component parses three optional parameters: source, topics, and frequency. The source parameter is stored in local page state and may be used only for on-page contextual messaging (for example, indicating which section the user came from). The topics parameter is split by commas into topic codes. After getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState returns topics, the component matches each provided topic code against the code field of each NewsletterTopic. Only matching topics are included in the initial selected topic IDs; unmatched codes are ignored. The frequency parameter is compared to the value of each frequency option returned in frequency_options; if there is a match, that value is set as the initially selected frequency. 2) Interaction with existing subscription: If existing_subscription is null, the form starts as a new subscription. If existing_subscription is present, its subscription fields (email, first_name, last_name, graduation_year, school, frequency, consent_email_communications) and selected_topic_ids define the default form state. If URL parameters topics or frequency are present, they override the corresponding parts of the existing subscription for initial state only, using the matching logic above. 3) Initial defaults when no URL and no subscription: If no existing_subscription is returned and no URL overrides are present, the form initializes with empty personal information, no topics selected, and a default frequency chosen from frequency_options (for example, the first option in the returned list). The consent checkbox initializes as unchecked. 4) State updates during interaction: Input fields, topic selections, and frequency selections update the in-memory form state immediately. This working state is what is sent to upsertNewsletterSubscription on submission. The form does not write any state to URL parameters; URL parameters are read-only for initialization. 5) State after submission: On a successful upsertNewsletterSubscription call, the returned subscription object can be used to refresh the internal form state (for example, to ensure that timestamps or other derived values are aligned with the backend), but no additional calls are required. The page then presents success messaging and navigation options; it may remain on the same URL. If the user navigates away using the provided links, the next page may set its own highlight parameter (in the case of stay_connected.html) but newsletter_subscribe.html does not modify its own URL parameters.

components.functionality: Controls initial data loading and state derivation from URL parameters. On page load, it parses the source, topics, and frequency URL parameters, calls getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState, and constructs the initial form state including pre-selected topics and frequency based on existing_subscription and/or URL overrides.

Provides the main form for creating or updating a newsletter subscription. It aggregates personal information inputs, topic selection, frequency selection, school selection, and consent confirmation into a single submission action that calls upsertNewsletterSubscription.

Captures and displays subscriber personal details. Fields include email, first name, last name, optional graduation year, and optional school/faculty. When an existing subscription is present, this section initializes with those values, allowing the user to review and edit them.
Displays a dynamic list of school/faculty options derived from the school_options array returned by getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState. Selecting an option sets the school field in the form state.

Lists all available newsletter topics and allows the user to select or deselect them. The options come from the topics array returned by getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState. It initializes the checked state of each topic based on existing_subscription.selected_topic_ids, optionally overridden by the topics URL parameter mapping to topic codes.

Displays newsletter delivery frequency options based on the frequency_options array returned by getNewsletterSubscriptionFormState. It initializes the selected frequency from either existing_subscription.subscription.frequency or the frequency URL parameter if present and valid. Changing the selection updates the frequency field in the form state.

Shows a required consent checkbox indicating the user's agreement to receive email communications from the alumni office. Its state is bound to consent_email_communications in the form state. Form submission is blocked until this checkbox is checked.

Performs client-side validation before calling upsertNewsletterSubscription. Ensures that all required fields (including email, frequency, and consent) are populated and that field formats (such as graduation year) are valid. Produces validation error messages that are consumed by the feedback-banner component.

Transforms the current form state into the payload required by upsertNewsletterSubscription and calls the interface. It compiles email, first_name, last_name, graduation_year, school, frequency, selected topic IDs, and consent_email_communications from the form state, then handles the response to update UI feedback and state.

Displays success, informational, or error messages to the user based on the results of validation and upsertNewsletterSubscription. It may show messages such as confirmation of a new subscription, confirmation of updated preferences, or error descriptions when submission fails.

Provides navigation actions for "Back to Stay Connected" and "Return to Home". The Back to Stay Connected action constructs a URL to stay_connected.html with the query parameter highlight=email_newsletters. The Return to Home action navigates to index.html without additional parameters.

Optionally adjusts contextual text on the page based on the source URL parameter (for example, explaining that the form relates to a particular section the user came from). It reads but does not modify the URL and does not call any interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a full-width header and navigation, and a centered content column spanning approximately 6–8 columns with large side gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global red utility bar and black main navigation (not part of this component list but assumed present), all subscription content is placed in a single, narrow, centered column that reads like an editorial letter. Components stack vertically in a clear sequence: initial load/feedback state at the top of the column, a short contextual note, then the main newsletter subscription form subdivided into logical sections (personal info, school, topics, frequency, consent), followed by the primary submit action and, finally, low-emphasis navigation links at the very bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global red utility bar and black main navigation (not part of this component list but assumed present), all subscription content is placed in a single, narrow, centered column that reads like an editorial letter. Components stack vertically in a clear sequence: initial load/feedback state at the top of the column, a short contextual note, then the main newsletter subscription form subdivided into logical sections (personal info, school, topics, frequency, consent), followed by the primary submit action and, finally, low-emphasis navigation links at the very bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 20–25% of the viewport is reserved for the existing global header and navigation bands. The remaining 75–80% is a white canvas containing one centered column that occupies roughly 35–45% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving large, symmetrical left and right gutters. Within this column, sections are separated by 32–48px vertical gaps, while individual fields and paragraphs use 16–24px spacing. A generous 64px (approx.) gap separates the bottom of the navigation bars from the first line of content, echoing the framed, formal-letter feeling of the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low. The central column uses a comfortable line length with generous line height, and fields are stacked vertically with ample breathing room. There are no sidebars, secondary columns, or decorative elements. Form groupings (sections) are clearly separated by larger vertical gaps, while labels, controls, and helper texts within each section remain closely grouped for usability but never cramped. Feedback and validation messages are concise and aligned with the same column width, maintaining the editorial feel rather than looking like app-style alerts.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is strictly top-to-bottom. The user’s eye moves from the global red utility bar to the black main navigation, then drops into the white content area. The first on-page anchor is the source context note at the top of the central column, analogous to the 'Dear Alumni,' line in the mockup. From there, focus moves down through the form’s sections in order: personal details, school, topic preferences, frequency, consent, and finally the submit button. Any status feedback banner appears near the top of the column, just above the form, so it is seen early without disrupting the linear flow. Secondary navigation links at the bottom provide a soft visual landing and exit path.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the existing full-width red utility bar and black main navigation, the page opens into a calm white canvas. Centered within this space is a relatively narrow column—about 40% of the viewport width on desktop—with wide, empty gutters on both sides, giving the content a formal, letter-like presence. After a generous vertical gap from the navigation, a single line of contextual text at the top of this column sets the stage for why the user is here. Directly below, a compact feedback area appears when needed, followed by a tall, single-column subscription form. This form is broken into clearly separated sections, each stacked one after the other with consistent spacing: personal information at the top, then school, topic preferences, email frequency, and consent. Each section uses simple labels and vertical lists of controls, with no multi-column layouts or visual clutter. Near the bottom of the column, a prominent red submit button anchors the primary action. Beneath it, a small cluster of understated navigation links offers exits and related information. The entire layout feels linear, spacious, and institutionally formal—mirroring a structured letter while quietly supporting the functional needs of updating newsletter preferences.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller governs the initial state of the main content area but is not visually represented as a persistent element. On first load, before data is ready, it can temporarily present a subtle loading indicator centered within the main content column: horizontally centered within the 35–45% width column and vertically placed roughly 25–35% down from the top of the white content area, still respecting the large gap below the navigation. Once loading completes, this temporary indicator disappears and the column is occupied by the source-context note, feedback banner, and subscription form in sequence.
