component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Transient overlay within the central content column; any loading indicator is centered in the column and removed once the page is initialized.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter subscription form is the main structural block within the white content area. It sits in the center of the page, starting approximately 64px below the bottom edge of the main navigation bars. The form’s outer container aligns to a narrow column occupying roughly 40% of the viewport width on desktop, centered horizontally, with equally wide white gutters on the left and right. Its internal layout is strictly vertical: form sections and controls stack from top to bottom with no side columns. The top of the form sits just below the source-context note and any feedback banner, with a 24–32px gap providing clear separation. Inside, sections such as personal information, school, topics, frequency, and consent each span the full width of the column, separated by 32–48px vertical spacing so each grouping feels distinct while still part of a continuous editorial flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container participating in the natural vertical flow of the central column; width constrained to the central column across breakpoints (expanding to nearly full width on narrow screens).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The personal information section appears at the top of the newsletter subscription form column, functioning like the initial body of a formal letter. It spans the full width of the centered column and begins roughly 16–24px below the form’s top edge. Within this section, the heading (if present) appears first, left-aligned, followed by a vertical stack of fields such as name and email, each aligned to the left and occupying roughly 100% of the column width. Vertical spacing between label, input, and helper text is tight but clear (about 8–12px), while 16–24px separates each field group. The bottom of this section has a slightly larger margin (around 32px) before the next section to visually group personal details as a single conceptual block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, topmost form section within the central column, expanding and contracting vertically based on the number of fields but always spanning the column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The school selector is placed directly below the primary personal information fields, still within the upper part of the form column. It appears as a single, full-width select control, aligned to the left edge of the column and occupying nearly the entire column width. There is a 16–24px vertical gap above it from the last personal information field, keeping it visually connected to personal data but readable as its own choice. A concise label is positioned just above the selector, and any helper or error text sits immediately beneath it, all aligned to the same left margin. The area below the school selector leads into the topic selection section, with a slightly larger separator margin of about 32px to signal a transition from identity information to preference choices.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form control, stacked inline within the vertical flow of the personal information and preferences area; no special floating or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic selection list occupies a central, visually significant section of the form, positioned below the school selector with approximately 32–40px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the centered column and is presented as a vertical list of options (e.g., checkboxes or multi-select pills) that align left and wrap naturally if labels are long, but never forming separate side columns. A section heading such as 'Newsletter Topics' appears at the top, left-aligned, with a brief explanatory line directly beneath, both following the editorial text style and line length of the column. Each topic item is separated by about 12–16px vertically, and the entire list is contained within the same narrow column, making the list feel like an enumerated part of the letter. The bottom of this section has 32–48px of vertical spacing before the frequency selector, reinforcing that topics and frequency are related but distinct decisions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list within the central column; items wrap text but do not introduce additional columns or horizontal scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The frequency selector is placed immediately below the topic selection list, separated by a 32–40px gap to indicate a new subdecision. It occupies the same width as the other form elements, spanning the full width of the centered column and aligning to the left margin. The section may include a short title like 'Email Frequency' and a one-line description, followed by a vertical stack of single-choice options (radio buttons or similar), each separated vertically by about 12–16px. The list of options remains tight and compact within the column, avoiding any horizontal arrangement. A moderate 32px space below the last frequency option separates this section from the consent section, maintaining the layered, stepwise editorial flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked control group within the vertical form flow, matching the alignment and width of the topic-selection section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consent section appears near the bottom of the form, just below the frequency selector, with a 32–48px vertical gap creating a distinct final block that feels like the closing terms of a formal letter. It spans the full width of the centered column and is left-aligned. A concise heading or label introduces the consent text, followed by one or more paragraphs of explanatory copy that adhere to the same line length and line height as the rest of the page, reinforcing the editorial aesthetic. Any consent checkboxes or toggle controls are placed directly beneath the explanatory text, aligned left, and stacked vertically with 8–12px between them. Error or required indicators, if present, appear immediately below the relevant checkbox group, without breaking the overall column alignment. A relatively generous 32–40px gap below this section separates it from the submit button, giving the consent text room to breathe and signal its importance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static final content block within the form, expanding vertically to accommodate legal/consent text but always respecting the centered single-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation handler does not occupy an independent block but governs the placement and styling of inline messages within the central column. Field-level validation messages appear directly under their corresponding inputs, indented to the same left margin and matching the column width, with a tight 4–8px gap from the field they describe to keep the relationship visually clear. If a summary error state is used, it appears as a concise text block at the top of the form, immediately below the feedback banner (if present) and above the personal-information section, spanning the full width of the column but contained within the narrow column boundaries. Spacing above and below this summary block is around 16–24px so that it is immediately noticeable yet does not disrupt the overall low-density, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Injects inline messages into the existing vertical flow of the central column; no fixed or floating behavior—messages push surrounding content down as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Visually, this controller corresponds to the primary submit button and any immediate post-click state at the bottom of the form. The button is placed directly below the consent section, with a 32–40px vertical gap. It is left-aligned with the column content but large enough to feel substantial, with horizontal padding of about 16px and vertical padding of about 10–12px, and its width constrained to the intrinsic button size rather than full-column width on desktop. On narrow screens, it can expand to near full width while staying within the column margins. The button uses the brand red, standing out as the key call to action in the otherwise minimal, text-driven layout. There is a 32–48px vertical gap between the bottom of the button and the navigation links below, giving the action a clear visual endpoint before secondary paths are presented.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static actionable control within the form flow; after activation, it may temporarily disable and show a subtle loading state but remains in the same position within the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback banner appears within the white content area at the top of the central column, directly below the large vertical gap from the navigation and above the main form. It spans the full width of the centered column, matching its left and right alignment, but does not extend into the outer gutters. When visible, it sits about 16–24px below the source-context note (or directly below the nav gap if the context note is absent), with 16–24px of padding inside the banner and 24–32px of space between its bottom edge and the start of the form. The banner itself is slim and linear—similar to a single paragraph—using subtle background color and left-aligned text, maintaining the editorial, low-density style. It never overlaps the header or sticks to the viewport; instead, it pushes the form content downward as needed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, context-sensitive block in the central column that appears and disappears based on status; it participates in normal document flow and is not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation links are placed at the very bottom of the central column, below the submit button. They occupy the same column width but are visually toned down, appearing as a small cluster of text links (e.g., 'Back to Alumni Office', 'Privacy Policy', 'Help') aligned to the left. There is a 32–48px vertical gap between the submit button and the first navigation link, creating a clear separation between the primary action and these secondary paths. Links may be arranged in a single line separated by 16–24px of horizontal space or stacked in one or two short lines, still within the narrow column, to preserve the clean editorial look. The bottom of this block has an additional 48–64px of whitespace before the end of the page content, allowing the layout to end softly without crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer-like cluster within the central column; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not fix to the viewport bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The source context note functions as the textual starting point of the page’s main content, analogous to the 'Dear Alumni,' line in the reference mockup. It appears in the central column, approximately 64px below the bottom of the black navigation bar, occupying the full width of the narrow column. The text is left-aligned and limited to one or two short lines, with comfortable line height, making it a clear, simple statement of why the user is on this page (e.g., 'You are updating your email newsletter preferences for...'). A modest 16–24px gap separates this note from the feedback banner below (if present) or directly from the newsletter subscription form, giving it breathing room while still tying it to the following content. The large empty gutters around it and the generous margin from the navigation visually frame this note as the anchor of the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text block at the top of the central column, participating in normal flow and setting the context for all content that follows.

name: Chapters & Clubs
title: Chapters & Clubs
description: Browse alumni chapters and clubs, filter by location and membership fee, sort by different criteria, and open detailed chapter or state events pages.

core_features: Load available chapter filter options (states, regions, membership fee range, sort options) using getChapterFilterOptions.
Read URL parameters (state, region, max_fee, sort_by, page) to initialize the filter, sort, and pagination state.
Search and list chapters using searchChapters with the current filters, sort option, and page.
Allow users to filter chapters by state, region, and maximum membership fee.
Allow users to sort chapters by available sort options returned from getChapterFilterOptions.
Provide pagination based on the total_results and page_size from searchChapters.
Provide navigation from a chapter result to that chapter’s detail page (chapter_detail.html?chapterId=...).
Provide navigation from a chapter result to the events listing filtered by that chapter’s state (events.html?state=...).
Support deep linking and shareable URLs: filters, sort, and page are always represented in URL parameters and restored on load.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Initial page load without pre-selected filters
1. User opens chapters.html without query parameters.
2. The page controller reads the empty query string and determines there are no active filters or sort preferences.
3. The page calls getChapterFilterOptions to retrieve available states, regions, membership fee range, and sort options.
4. After filter options load, the controller derives default filter values (no state/region filter, no max_fee limit, default sort option from the returned sort_options) and sets page=1.
5. The controller calls searchChapters with filters={}, sort_by=defaultSort, page=1, and a configured page_size.
6. The results list displays the returned chapters and the pagination control is initialized using total_results and page_size.
7. The URL is normalized (e.g., only including parameters that differ from defaults if desired by implementation).

Workflow 2: Landing with pre-selected filters from another page
1. User clicks a link from index.html or stay_connected.html that goes to chapters.html with URL parameters such as state, region, max_fee, sort_by, and/or page.
2. The page controller parses the query string and stores initial state: selectedState, selectedRegion, selectedMaxFee, selectedSortBy, currentPage.
3. The page calls getChapterFilterOptions to obtain valid states, regions, membership_fee_range, and sort_options.
4. After receiving filter options, the controller validates incoming URL values against these options (for example, state and region must exist in the returned lists; sort_by must exist in sort_options). Any invalid values are ignored or reset to defaults.
5. The controller calls searchChapters with filters derived from the validated URL parameters (e.g., state, region, maximum membership fee), sort_by from the URL (if valid), and page from the URL (or 1 if invalid/missing).
6. The results list shows only chapters matching the pre-selected filters, and the filter controls reflect those selections so that users can see and modify them.

Workflow 3: Refining filters and sorting
1. User interacts with the filter form: selects a state from the dropdown, optionally a region, and/or adjusts the maximum membership fee input.
2. On filter change (either immediate or on explicit 'Apply' action, depending on implementation), the filter form notifies the page controller of the updated filter values.
3. The controller sets page=1 (reset pagination when filters change), updates the URL query string to reflect the new filters and page (e.g., chapters.html?state=...&region=...&max_fee=...&sort_by=...&page=1), and calls searchChapters with the updated filters and current sort_by.
4. The results list updates to show only chapters matching the new filters.
5. When the user selects a sort option from the sort control, the sort control notifies the controller.
6. The controller sets page=1, updates sort_by in the URL, and calls searchChapters with the existing filters plus the new sort_by.
7. The list and pagination refresh according to the new sort order.

Workflow 4: Pagination across chapter results
1. After a searchChapters call returns a results set, the pagination control computes the number of pages from total_results and page_size.
2. User clicks a page number or next/previous control.
3. The pagination component notifies the controller of the new page number.
4. The controller updates the page parameter in the URL (preserving current filters and sort_by) and calls searchChapters using the same filters and sort_by but with the new page.
5. The results list displays the chapters for that specific page.
6. If the user uses the browser back/forward buttons, the controller listens for URL changes, recalculates state from URL parameters, and re-runs searchChapters with those parameters so the list and pagination stay in sync.

Workflow 5: Navigating to chapter details and events
1. User sees a chapter card in the results list and clicks the chapter name or a 'View chapter' link.
2. The results list component triggers navigation to chapter_detail.html with a chapterId query parameter corresponding to the clicked Chapter.id.
3. The browser opens chapter_detail.html?chapterId=... for that specific chapter.
4. Alternatively, user clicks a 'View events in this state' link on a chapter card.
5. The results list component reads the Chapter.state for that card and triggers navigation to events.html with a state query parameter (events.html?state=...).
6. The events.html page (outside the scope of this design) uses that state parameter to show relevant events.
interactions: Selecting a state filter: User chooses a state from a dropdown bound to getChapterFilterOptions.states. The filter form emits onFilterChange, the controller updates the state URL parameter, resets page to 1, and calls searchChapters with the updated filters.

Selecting a region filter: User chooses a region from a dropdown bound to getChapterFilterOptions.regions. The form emits onFilterChange; controller updates the region URL parameter, resets page to 1, and re-calls searchChapters.

Setting maximum membership fee: User changes a numeric input or slider that is constrained by getChapterFilterOptions.membership_fee_range.min and .max. On change/apply, the form emits onFilterChange; controller updates max_fee in the URL (or removes it if cleared) and re-calls searchChapters.

Clearing all filters: User clicks a 'Reset filters' control in the filter form. The form emits onResetFilters; controller clears state, region, max_fee from URL (and sets page=1), then calls searchChapters with empty filters and current sort or a default sort.

Choosing sort order: User selects a sort option from a dropdown bound to getChapterFilterOptions.sort_options (using each option.value). The sort control emits onSortChange; controller updates sort_by in the URL, sets page=1, and calls searchChapters with existing filters.

Changing pages: User clicks a pagination control (page number, next/previous). The pagination component emits onPageChange; controller updates page in the URL and calls searchChapters with the new page and current filters/sort.

Opening a chapter detail page: User clicks the chapter title or an explicit link on a result card. The results list emits onViewChapterClick with the Chapter.id; the application navigates to chapter_detail.html?chapterId={Chapter.id}.

Viewing events in a chapter's state: User clicks 'View events in this state' on a result card. The results list emits onViewEventsInStateClick with Chapter.state; the application navigates to events.html?state={Chapter.state}.

Responding to browser navigation: User uses the browser back/forward buttons. The controller listens for URL changes (onUrlParamChange), re-parses state, region, max_fee, sort_by, and page from the URL, and calls searchChapters so the visible filters and list always match the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The URL query string is the single source of truth for the page's filters, sorting, and pagination. On page load and on any URL change, the controller parses the parameters: state (string), region (string), max_fee (number), sort_by (string), and page (number). The page first calls getChapterFilterOptions to load valid states, regions, membership_fee_range, and sort_options. Incoming values are validated against these options: state and region must exist in the returned arrays; sort_by must match one of the sort_options.value entries; max_fee, if present, must be numeric and within or adjusted to the membership_fee_range; page must be a positive integer and within appropriate bounds inferred from total_results when available. Invalid or missing values are ignored or replaced with defaults (e.g., no filter, first page, default sort from getChapterFilterOptions). After normalization, the controller constructs a filters object using the validated state, region, and max_fee (mapped to the membership fee filter field expected by searchChapters). It then calls searchChapters with filters, sort_by, current page, and a consistent page_size. When the user changes filters, sort options, or pagination, the controller updates the corresponding URL parameters and then re-calls searchChapters. This ensures that reloading the page or sharing the URL restores the same state, and that browser navigation remains synchronized with the displayed filters and results. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used; all state is derived from the URL and API responses.

components.functionality: Central controller that initializes the page, manages URL-driven state, coordinates calls to getChapterFilterOptions and searchChapters, and passes data and callbacks to child components.

Provides controls to filter chapters by state, region, and maximum membership fee. Reads available options and fee bounds from getChapterFilterOptions, emits changes to the controller, and reflects the current filter state derived from the URL.

Displays sort options returned by getChapterFilterOptions.sort_options and allows the user to select one. Keeps its selected value in sync with the sort_by URL parameter and notifies the controller when the sort preference changes.
Summarizes the currently applied filters (state, region, and maximum fee) based on URL-derived state and provides quick controls to clear individual filters or all filters.

Displays the chapter search results returned by searchChapters. Each chapter entry shows core information from the Chapter model (e.g., name, location, membership fee, upcoming_events_count) and includes interaction points for opening the chapter detail page and viewing events in that chapter's state.
Renders pagination controls based on the page, page_size, and total_results from searchChapters. Allows navigation between pages and keeps the current page synchronized with the page URL parameter.

Indicates loading and error states for both filter options (getChapterFilterOptions) and chapter search results (searchChapters). Displays appropriate messages or retry actions driven by the controller.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a full-width header and navigation, and a centered content column spanning approximately 6–8 columns with large side gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: All components are arranged in a single, centered vertical column that mirrors the formal-letter style of the mockup. After the global utility bar and navigation (red and black bands), there is a generous vertical gap before the first page-specific element. From top to bottom, the flow is: page state controller, filters form, sort control, active filters summary, results list, pagination, and contextual loading/error messages. There are no sidebars; everything sits within the same central column, left-aligned within that column but visually centered in the viewport.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components are arranged in a single, centered vertical column that mirrors the formal-letter style of the mockup. After the global utility bar and navigation (red and black bands), there is a generous vertical gap before the first page-specific element. From top to bottom, the flow is: page state controller, filters form, sort control, active filters summary, results list, pagination, and contextual loading/error messages. There are no sidebars; everything sits within the same central column, left-aligned within that column but visually centered in the viewport.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top utility and navigation bands occupy roughly the upper 20–25% of the viewport as in the mockup. The main content column is centered and relatively narrow, occupying about 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop (approximately 6–7 of 12 grid columns), leaving large left and right gutters for a calm, institutional feel. Vertical spacing follows the established system: ~64px between the navigation and the first content component, 32–48px between major component groups, and 16–24px between related controls within the same group.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps a low-density, editorial feel despite being functional. Controls are grouped but never cramped: each group has ample white space around it and minimal decorative framing. The filters and sort controls appear as concise, flat elements with simple labels and inputs, separated by whitespace rather than heavy borders. The results list is structured but not card-heavy—each item uses subtle rules and spacing, maintaining plenty of breathing room in the central column.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves top-to-bottom in a linear manner: from the red utility bar to the black navigation bar, then down across a generous white gap to the first centered controller. From there, the gaze follows the vertical stack of controls, with a slight pause on the concise active filters summary line (similar to the LinkedIn focal line in the mockup), and then drops into the results list. Pagination concludes the main flow at the bottom. Any loading/error state appears centered in the results region, temporarily becoming the main focal point without disrupting the overall vertical rhythm.

layout.overall_description: Below the global red utility bar and black navigation bar, the page opens into a broad white canvas. After a tall, quiet band of empty space, a single, relatively narrow content column appears in the center of the screen, occupying about 40–50% of the viewport width with generous gutters on both sides. At the top of this column sits a compact state controller, like a subtle line of toggles or segmented controls. A comfortable gap below it introduces the filter form, which stacks its fields vertically, aligned to the left edge of the column, but still clearly framed by white space. Just beneath, aligned with the same column edges, a small sort control sits toward the top-right of the column line, balancing the filters visually without breaking the single-column feel. A short distance below, a single line of active-filter summary text runs across the column, centered horizontally—this line stands alone, mirroring the focal line treatment seen in the mockup. The results list then extends downward in the same central column, each item occupying the full width of that column, separated by consistent vertical spacing and perhaps a light divider line, maintaining an editorial clarity rather than a busy card grid. At the bottom of the list, a simple pagination row appears centered and slightly separated from the last result item. If there is a loading or error state, it appears in the middle of the list area, centered within the column and surrounded by generous white space, maintaining the restrained and institutional character of the design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chapters-page-controller is the first page-specific element in the main content area, positioned directly below the global navigation stack. After the black navigation bar, there is a generous vertical gap of approximately 64px before this controller appears. It sits within a centered content column that spans about 40–50% of the viewport width, aligned to the main grid (roughly 6–7 of 12 columns). The controller itself is a single horizontal row of controls (e.g., radio buttons or segmented switches) that is left-aligned within this column but visually centered in the page thanks to the wide left and right gutters. Vertical padding inside the controller’s touch targets is modest (around 8–12px), matching the conservative styling of the site. There is about 24–32px of vertical spacing between this controller and the filter form below, maintaining clear separation while preserving a continuous top-to-bottom flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page as a normal block-level element; no sticky or fixed behavior. It anchors the start of the content column but does not pin to the top on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chapter-filters-form sits directly below the chapters-page-controller, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical whitespace. It occupies the same centered content column, matching its width (40–50% of the viewport) and left alignment. Filter fields are arranged in a simple vertical stack: label above input, with 16–24px of vertical spacing between each field group, echoing the short-paragraph rhythm of the mockup. There is minimal visual framing—no heavy boxed panels—just flat inputs and subtle underlines or borders, allowing the white background to dominate. When multiple filters are present (e.g., location, membership fee), they are grouped with a slightly tighter internal spacing and a slightly larger gap (24–32px) between logical groups. The bottom of the filter form has around 24px of vertical space before the sort control row begins, preserving an airy, editorial feeling despite the form’s functional nature.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, non-sticky block that expands vertically as filters are added. It always remains inside the central column and reflows naturally on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chapter-sort-control is positioned immediately below the filter form, separated by about 24px of vertical space. It shares the same horizontal column constraints but is visually lighter and more compact. The sort label and dropdown (or equivalent control) are right-aligned within the central column, creating a subtle horizontal tension with the left-aligned filters above while still maintaining the single-column structure. The control itself is a single line of text and a flat dropdown, with 8–12px of internal vertical padding to match other controls. There is approximately 16–24px of whitespace below the sort control before the active filters summary line, keeping the hierarchy clear and preventing the upper control area from feeling crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally with the document and remains inline; no sticky behavior. On narrower screens it can shift to left alignment within the same column while staying in its vertical position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chapters-active-filters-summary appears as a single, concise line of text directly below the sort control, separated by roughly 16–24px of vertical space. It spans the width of the central column but is horizontally centered within that column (the text itself is centered), creating a distinct visual pause similar to the standalone LinkedIn URL in the mockup. This summary line is not boxed; it rests directly on the white background with perhaps a subtle emphasis via typographic treatment (e.g., slightly darker or accented text) rather than additional UI chrome. Above and below this line there is enough whitespace—16–24px above, 24–32px below—to make it feel like a discrete, editorial statement that bridges the control area and the results list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-sticky element that reflows with content. If no filters are active, this area collapses, and the results list moves up, preserving the overall vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chapters-results-list begins below the active filters summary, after a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It occupies the same centered column as the controls, maintaining a consistent width of 40–50% of the viewport with generous side gutters. Each chapter result entry is a horizontally full-width row within this column, left-aligned and separated from the next by 24–32px of vertical spacing and optionally a subtle light-grey divider line to keep the page feeling ordered but not busy. The content within each result (chapter name, location, membership fee, links to details/events) is arranged as text blocks and small inline actions, following a simple top-to-bottom structure inside each item so that no sub-columns break the editorial feel. The list extends downward and becomes the main body of the page, visually analogous to a series of short paragraphs in a formal letter, but with just enough structural cues to support scanning.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content that grows vertically with the number of results. There is no infinite scroll; instead, pagination at the bottom marks the end of each page of results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chapters-pagination component is placed directly below the results list, with a noticeable gap of about 32–40px from the last result item to clearly indicate the end of the current page of content. It is centered within the same content column, with the pagination controls themselves (page numbers, previous/next) horizontally centered as well. The control row is compact, with 8–12px of vertical padding around the click targets, and sits on the white background without heavy framing—perhaps just a thin underline for the active page to echo the active tab underline in the main navigation. Below the pagination, an additional 48–64px of bottom padding ensures the page ends softly rather than abruptly, matching the spacious, institutional mood.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-sticky control at the bottom of the content column. It remains anchored beneath the current results and moves down as additional results are loaded on subsequent pages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chapters-loading-error-state occupies the same central column and visually takes over the area where the results list would normally appear. When active, it is vertically centered within the visible portion of the results region: there is a comfortable margin (at least 48px) above it from the active filters summary and a similar amount below before the pagination area (which can be hidden if irrelevant). The message is centered horizontally within the column, with the primary text centered and any supporting actions (e.g., retry button) stacked below with 16–24px of spacing. Surrounding white space is generous, making the message feel intentional and calm rather than alarming. When in a loading state, a simple spinner or progress indicator appears just above or alongside the message, still within the narrow column. When in an error state, the messaging remains flat and typographically driven, avoiding heavy panels or bright warning blocks to stay consistent with the conservative brand tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual block that replaces or overlays the results list area. It scrolls normally with the page but may appear closer to the viewport center if the list would otherwise be short. When inactive, it collapses completely, allowing the results list to occupy the space.

name: Chapter Detail
title: Chapter Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a specific alumni chapter, shows its upcoming events summary, and provides a membership enrollment form.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a specific chapter based on the "chapterId" URL parameter using the "getChapterDetail" interface.
Show a summary of upcoming events for the chapter using the "upcoming_events" data from "getChapterDetail".
Display chapter membership fee information and available membership options returned by "getChapterDetail".
Provide a membership enrollment form that captures first name, last name, email, role, graduation year, and selected membership option/fee.
Require explicit agreement to membership terms before allowing membership submission.
Submit membership enrollment for the current chapter using the "joinChapter" interface and show success or error feedback.
Provide navigation back to the chapter list filtered by the chapter's state and to the events list filtered by the current chapter.
user_workflows: View chapter details and upcoming events: 1) User navigates to "chapter_detail.html?chapterId={chapterId}" from chapters.html, events.html, or index.html. 2) On page load, the "chapter-detail-loader" component reads the "chapterId" from the URL and calls "getChapterDetail(chapterId)". 3) When the call succeeds, the page populates chapter information (name, location, membership fee, contact details) and a preview list of upcoming events. 4) The user can review the basic chapter information and the upcoming events summary.

Join a chapter as a member: 1) From the loaded chapter page, the user scrolls to the membership section. 2) The user selects a membership option using the "membership-option-selector" component, which fills the membership option name and fee amount fields in the form state. 3) The user enters their first name, last name, email, chooses a role (e.g., alumni, student), and optionally enters graduation year. 4) The user checks the membership terms consent checkbox. 5) The user submits the form, triggering "joinChapter" with the current chapterId and form values. 6) On success, the page shows a confirmation message, may display a summary of the created membership (from the "membership" object returned by "joinChapter"), and disables or resets the form. 7) On error, the page shows an error message and keeps the form data so the user can correct and resubmit.

Navigate to all chapters in the same state: 1) After reviewing chapter details, the user clicks a "Back to chapters" or "View all chapters in this state" link. 2) The "chapter-navigation-links" component builds a URL to "chapters.html?state={chapter.state}" using the state value from the loaded Chapter data. 3) The browser navigates to that URL, where chapters.html can use the "state" parameter to filter its listing.

Navigate to all events for the current chapter: 1) After reviewing the upcoming events preview, the user clicks a "View all events for this chapter" link. 2) The "chapter-navigation-links" or "upcoming-events-preview" component builds a URL to "events.html?chapterId={chapter.id}". 3) The browser navigates to that URL, where events.html can list or filter events by the provided chapterId.

Handle missing or invalid chapterId: 1) User opens chapter_detail.html without a "chapterId" parameter or with an invalid value. 2) On load, the "chapter-detail-loader" component detects that "chapterId" is missing or that "getChapterDetail" failed with an error. 3) The "missing-chapterid-alert" component displays an error message and suggests returning to chapters.html (without a state filter) or checking the link. 4) If "getChapterDetail" failed due to a transient error, the user can click a retry action that re-calls "getChapterDetail" with the same URL parameter.
interactions: On page load: The page reads "chapterId" from the URL query string. If present, it triggers the "getChapterDetail" interface. While waiting, a loading state is shown. On success, components bound to Chapter and Event data render. On error, an error state with a retry interaction appears.

Click "Back to chapters" / "View all chapters in this state": When the user clicks this link, the page constructs a navigation URL to "chapters.html?state={chapter.state}" using the state from the loaded Chapter. The browser then navigates to that URL.
Click "View all events for this chapter": When clicked, the page constructs "events.html?chapterId={chapter.id}" using the chapter id from the loaded Chapter and navigates to that URL.

Select membership option: The user selects one membership option from the available "membership_options" array (for example via radio buttons or a dropdown). The "membership-option-selector" component updates the membership form state with "membership_option_name" and "membership_fee_amount" corresponding to the selected option. If no options are provided by the API, the component can default to using the base chapter membership_fee value for the form state.

Fill membership form fields: The user types into text inputs for first name, last name, and email, chooses a role from a role dropdown that maps to the ChapterMembership.role enum values, and optionally enters graduation year. Each change updates the local form state.

Agree to terms: The user clicks a checkbox to indicate consent to membership terms. Until this checkbox is checked (consent_terms is true), the submit button remains disabled and "joinChapter" cannot be called.

Submit membership form: On clicking the enabled submit button, front-end validations are run (required fields, email format pattern, numeric graduation year if provided). If validations pass, the page calls "joinChapter" with the current chapterId and the form state fields. While waiting, the submit button is disabled and a loading indicator can be shown. On a successful response, success feedback is displayed and form fields can be cleared or locked. On an error response, an error message is displayed and the form remains filled for correction.

Retry loading chapter details: If "getChapterDetail" fails, the error state shows a retry control. Clicking it re-triggers "getChapterDetail(chapterId)" using the same URL parameter without reloading the entire page.

Change URL manually: If the user manually changes the "chapterId" in the URL and reloads the page, the loader component reads the new "chapterId" and runs the full loading process for that chapter. If the implementation listens to URL changes without reload, an "onUrlParamChange" handler can also re-trigger "getChapterDetail".
page_functionality.state_logic: The main state driver for this page is the "chapterId" URL query parameter. On load, the page reads "chapterId" from "chapter_detail.html?chapterId={chapterId}". If "chapterId" is missing, the page enters a missing-parameter error state and does not call "getChapterDetail". If "chapterId" is present, the page calls "getChapterDetail(chapterId)" and stores the returned "chapter" object, "upcoming_events" array, and "membership_options" array in in-memory page state. Derived UI state (such as the default selected membership option) is computed from these objects. Navigation to other pages is also driven via URL parameters: when the user clicks chapter-related navigation, the page constructs URLs with query parameters (e.g., "chapters.html?state={chapter.state}", "events.html?chapterId={chapter.id}") but does not persist any state in localStorage or other persistent client storage. Form state for membership enrollment (first_name, last_name, email, role, graduation_year, membership_option_name, membership_fee_amount, consent_terms) is kept only in memory within the page and is sent to the server exclusively via the "joinChapter" interface. After a successful "joinChapter" call, the response (including the created "membership" object) may be stored in page state for display but is not written to the URL or to persistent storage. If the user changes the "chapterId" in the URL and reloads, the entire page reinitializes its state from the new "chapterId" and calls "getChapterDetail" again.

components.functionality: Reads the "chapterId" URL parameter on page load and retrieves full chapter details using the "getChapterDetail" interface. Manages loading, success, and error states and exposes the loaded Chapter, upcoming events, and membership options to other components.
event_handlers: onLoad: Parse the "chapterId" parameter from the current page URL. If missing, set an error state indicating that no chapter could be loaded. If present, call getChapterDetail(chapterId).
onLoadSuccess: When getChapterDetail returns successfully, store the chapter, upcoming_events, and membership_options in page state and notify dependent components to render.
onLoadError: When getChapterDetail fails, set an error state that can be displayed by the "missing-chapterid-alert" component.
onRetryClick: Reinvoke getChapterDetail with the same chapterId when the user chooses to retry after an error.
onUrlParamChange: If the implementation listens to URL changes without full reload, detect changes to the chapterId query parameter and re-run getChapterDetail for the new id.

components.functionality: Displays an error message and guidance when the chapterId URL parameter is missing or when getChapterDetail fails to load data for the given chapterId.
event_handlers: onClickBackToChapters: Navigate to "chapters.html" without a state parameter when no chapter can be loaded, allowing the user to select a chapter manually.
onClickRetry: Trigger chapter-detail-loader.onRetryClick when a transient error occurred while loading chapter details.

components.functionality: Shows core chapter information such as name, description, location (city, state, region), active status, and optional website URL and contact email to give users context about the chapter.
event_handlers: onClickWebsiteLink: Open the chapter.website_url in a new browser tab or window if provided.
onClickContactEmail: Open the user’s default mail client using a mailto link based on chapter.contact_email when available.

components.functionality: Displays chapter membership fee information, membership fee description, and the total number of upcoming events for this chapter to help users assess the value and activity level of the chapter.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When Chapter data is updated by chapter-detail-loader, refresh displayed membership_fee, membership_fee_description, currency, and upcoming_events_count.

components.functionality: Provides contextual navigation actions related to the current chapter, including returning to all chapters in the same state and viewing all events for this chapter.
event_handlers: onClickBackToChaptersInState: Build a URL "chapters.html?state={chapter.state}" using the current Chapter state field and navigate the browser to that URL.
onClickBackToChaptersUnfiltered: Optionally navigate to "chapters.html" without parameters if state is not available or if the user chooses a general chapters view.
onClickViewAllEventsForChapter: Build a URL "events.html?chapterId={chapter.id}" using the current Chapter id and navigate the browser to that URL.

components.functionality: Shows a limited preview list of upcoming events associated with the chapter, using the "upcoming_events" array from getChapterDetail, including key event attributes such as name, type, datetime, and location. Encourages users to explore more via the full events listing.
data_binding: Chapter (for constructing the "view all events" link)
event_handlers: onRender: Display each event in upcoming_events according to the design rule (for example up to a specific maximum number) while ensuring actual event content comes entirely from the API.
onClickViewAllEventsForChapter: Same behavior as in chapter-navigation-links, navigating to "events.html?chapterId={chapter.id}".
onListDataChange: Re-render the events preview whenever the upcoming_events array from chapter-detail-loader updates.

components.functionality: Presents available membership options specific to this chapter and lets the user choose exactly one. When options are present, each option exposes its option_name, fee_amount, currency, and description. If the membership_options array is empty or not provided, the selector falls back to a default option derived from the Chapter membership_fee.
data_binding: ChapterMembership (for the membership_option_name and membership_fee_amount fields used in the membership form)
event_handlers: onOptionsLoad: When membership_options and Chapter data are first available from chapter-detail-loader, build a list of selectable options. If membership_options is empty, define a single default option using Chapter.membership_fee and currency.
onSelectMembershipOption: When the user selects an option, update the shared membership form state with membership_option_name and membership_fee_amount corresponding to the selected option.
onSelectionChange: Notify the "chapter-membership-form" component when the selected membership option changes so that any displayed fee summary or total updates in real time.

components.functionality: Captures a user’s information to join the current chapter and submits it via the "joinChapter" interface. The form includes first name, last name, email, role, optional graduation year, selected membership option name and fee amount, and a required consent-to-terms checkbox.
data_binding: ChapterMembership (for field structure and for binding to the created membership on success)
membership_options (indirectly, via membership-option-selector to populate membership_option_name and membership_fee_amount in the form state)
event_handlers: onFirstNameChange: Update local form state for first_name as the user types.
onLastNameChange: Update local form state for last_name as the user types.
onEmailChange: Update local form state for email and optionally validate basic email format.
onRoleChange: Update local form state for role, ensuring the selected value maps to one of the ChapterMembership.role enum options ("alumni", "student", "faculty", "staff", "friend_family", "other").
onGraduationYearChange: Update local form state for graduation_year, validating that it is numeric if provided.
onMembershipOptionUpdate: Receive selected membership_option_name and membership_fee_amount from membership-option-selector and update the form state fields with these values.
onConsentTermsToggle: Toggle the consent_terms boolean in form state when the user checks or unchecks the terms agreement checkbox.

onValidateBeforeSubmit: Before calling joinChapter, run client-side checks to ensure required fields (chapterId, first_name, last_name, email, role, consent_terms) are present and that consent_terms is true. If validation fails, prevent submission and show inline errors.

onSubmit: When the user submits the form and validations pass, call joinChapter with parameters: chapterId (from Chapter.id), first_name, last_name, email, role, graduation_year, membership_option_name, membership_fee_amount, and consent_terms from the form state. While waiting, disable the submit button to prevent duplicate submissions.

onSubmitSuccess: When joinChapter returns with success true, store the returned membership object in state, trigger the membership-form-feedback component to display confirmation, and optionally disable or reset the form fields.
onSubmitError: When joinChapter returns with success false or an error, keep the form data intact, surface the message field to the user, and re-enable the submit button.
onReset: Optional handler to clear form fields (except possibly the selected membership option) after a successful submission or when the user chooses to reset the form.

components.functionality: Provides real-time feedback about the outcome of the chapter membership enrollment attempt, including success confirmation and any error messages returned by the joinChapter interface.
data_binding: ChapterMembership (as the created membership from joinChapter response)
joinChapter response metadata (success flag and message)
event_handlers: onSubmitSuccess: When notified by chapter-membership-form that joinChapter succeeded, display a confirmation state, optionally including key details from the returned membership such as membership_start_date, membership_option_name, and membership_fee_amount.
onSubmitError: When notified of an error response, display the message from joinChapter and keep the status panel in an error state until the next submission attempt.
onDismissFeedback: Allow the user to dismiss any non-critical feedback messages (for example, close an alert) without affecting the underlying membership data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a full-width header and navigation, and a centered content column spanning approximately 6–8 columns with large side gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global red utility bar and black main navigation (from the mockup), the Chapter Detail page uses a single, centered content column that behaves like a formal letter. All components stack vertically within this column in a linear top-to-bottom flow: introductory chapter information at the top, then chapter meta details and internal navigation links, followed by upcoming events, and finally membership selection and the enrollment form with its feedback. There are no sidebars; every component aligns to the same central column for a calm, editorial reading experience.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global red utility bar and black main navigation (from the mockup), the Chapter Detail page uses a single, centered content column that behaves like a formal letter. All components stack vertically within this column in a linear top-to-bottom flow: introductory chapter information at the top, then chapter meta details and internal navigation links, followed by upcoming events, and finally membership selection and the enrollment form with its feedback. There are no sidebars; every component aligns to the same central column for a calm, editorial reading experience.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and navigation area occupy the top 20–25% of the viewport as in the mockup. The main content sits in the remaining ~75–80%, with a narrow-centered column occupying roughly 35–45% of the viewport width (about 6–8 of 12 columns, max-width around 720–840px) and large left/right gutters. A large first gap (48–64px) separates the bottom of the navigation bar from the top of the chapter content. Between major component groups (e.g., from basic info to meta summary, from events to membership form) the vertical separation is 32–48px; within tightly related pieces (e.g., inside the form, between paragraphs or fields) spacing follows the 16–24px rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density mirrors the mockup's low-density editorial style. Each component is visually simple and mostly text-based, with generous line height and clear paragraph breaks. Information panels present minimal framing (subtle headings, perhaps a thin rule) rather than heavy boxes. Forms and lists keep ample vertical breathing room between items. No graphics or side panels compete with the main text column, keeping cognitive load low and supporting slow, deliberate reading.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye flows from the bold red utility bar and dark navigation bar down to a wide white field. A large vertical gap leads the eye to the top of the central column, where the chapter's basic info functions like a greeting and title. From there, the reader moves linearly: introductory chapter text, then compact meta details and internal chapter links, then a clearly separated band for upcoming events, and finally the membership area with options and the main enrollment form. Status or feedback messages appear immediately after the elements they relate to, so the reader encounters them in context without breaking the overall vertical rhythm. The loader, when active, momentarily becomes the focal point by centering over the column, then disappears to restore the editorial flow.

layout.overall_description: The page sits beneath the familiar stacked red utility bar and black main navigation, which span the full viewport width and anchor the top 20–25% of the screen. Below them, a generous vertical gap opens into a calm white canvas. At the center of this canvas, a narrow text column—about 40% of the viewport width with large left and right gutters—holds all Chapter Detail content. The column reads like a formal letter: a succinct chapter introduction at the top, followed by neatly separated blocks of chapter meta information, a line of chapter-related links, a quiet preview of upcoming events, and finally a well-spaced membership selection area and enrollment form. Each section is separated by consistent, medium-to-large vertical spacing, so components feel like distinct but related paragraphs in the same document. There are no sidebars, no cards clustering; everything is linear and editorial. Occasionally, a loader may temporarily overlay the central column, or an alert or feedback message may appear inline, but they always respect the same centered alignment and generous spacing, maintaining the conservative, institutional tone of the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears only while chapter data is being fetched. It is positioned centrally within the main white content area, aligned to the same centerline as the text column. Visually, it behaves like a light overlay confined to the column: a translucent white panel or simple spinner centered both horizontally and vertically within the column's bounds, roughly occupying a small central rectangle (about 25–30% of viewport width and a modest height). The large gutters on the left and right remain visible, reinforcing the narrow, editorial column. When active, the loader sits above all other content in the stacking order, dimming or replacing the column contents beneath, but it does not cover the global header or navigation. Once loading finishes, it disappears entirely, returning focus to the top of the column content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the central content column in a centered position; fixed relative to the scroll position of the content area while active, then removed from the layout when data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The missing chapter ID alert occupies the top of the central content column, directly below the large gap that follows the main navigation. It spans the full width of the column (about 35–45% of the viewport, max-width aligned with all other content) and is horizontally centered on the page. There is a generous margin above it (48–64px below the navigation) and a comfortable margin below it (32–40px before any explanatory text or fallback content), so it feels like a distinct, important block of information within the otherwise quiet white space. The alert reads like a single prominent paragraph in the editorial column, without extending into the gutters or creating any sidebars.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Placed in the normal document flow at the top of the content column when the chapter ID is missing; pushes other components downward and may suppress chapter-specific components entirely.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chapter basic info block is the first substantive component in the normal success state. It sits at the top of the centered content column, roughly 48–64px below the bottom edge of the black navigation bar, echoing the mockup’s large space before 'Dear Alumni'. The block spans the full width of the column and is centered between wide left and right gutters. Inside this block, the chapter name appears at the top as a simple, formal heading, aligned flush left within the column, with 16–24px of space below it. Immediately beneath, one or two short introductory paragraphs describe the chapter, each separated by about 16–24px of vertical space. The overall height of this block is modest but visually anchors the column, occupying roughly the upper part of the main white canvas before any meta or functional elements appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In normal document flow as the first content block; it establishes the top alignment and width for all subsequent components in the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chapter meta summary sits directly beneath the chapter basic info, with a clear but modest separation—approximately 32px of vertical space—maintaining the editorial rhythm. It uses the same centered column width and left alignment. Visually, this block reads like a grouped set of short lines: for example, location, number of members, contact information, or key stats, each presented as one line or short phrase. The lines are stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing between them, making the block feel compact and factual, contrasting slightly with the prose of the basic info above. The meta summary overall remains shallow in height, roughly equivalent to one or two paragraphs of text, and does not introduce any strong borders or boxes that would disrupt the minimal letter-like feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows directly after chapter-basic-info in the column; shrinks or grows vertically depending on available meta fields, but always constrained to the column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chapter navigation links appear immediately below the meta summary with about 24–32px of vertical space separating them. They are laid out as a simple horizontal or short vertical list, but constrained to the same central column width. If horizontal, the links run left to right across the column in a single line where possible, with each link separated by about 16–24px of horizontal space and minimal decoration, resembling a quiet sub-navigation row within the letter. If the links wrap or are better presented vertically, they become a short vertical list, left-aligned with 8–12px between items. In either case, they occupy a slim band of vertical space (roughly 1–1.5 text lines tall if single-row, or up to the height of 3–4 lines if multi-row) and sit clearly apart from the content above and below. There is another 32–40px vertical gap after this links block before the upcoming events preview, allowing it to feel like a discrete focal moment, similar to the standalone LinkedIn line in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the normal document flow; the layout may adapt between a single horizontal row and a short vertical stack depending on viewport width while preserving centered column alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The upcoming events preview occupies the next major section of the central column, separated from the chapter navigation links above by about 32–48px of vertical space, signaling a new content group. It spans the full width of the column and is left-aligned within it. A simple, moderately sized section heading (e.g., 'Upcoming Events') appears at the top of this block with 16–24px of space below. Beneath the heading, a small list of event summaries is stacked vertically, each event occupying one compact row or card-like line within the column, with 16–24px between items. The list remains visually light: minimal borders, no heavy card backgrounds, and no images, keeping to the editorial style. Overall, this block might occupy about 20–30% of the vertical space of the main content area on a typical screen, depending on the number of events, but the generous spacing ensures it never feels crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Located in the normal vertical flow; grows in height as more events are shown but always keeps events arranged in a single column, respecting the narrow centered layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The membership option selector sits below the upcoming events preview with a clear, section-level separation of about 32–40px, making the beginning of the membership area feel intentional and distinct. Positioned within the same central column, it spans the full column width and is left-aligned. The selector itself may appear as a short cluster of radio buttons, tabs, or a dropdown, but remains visually light: options arranged vertically or in a single horizontal line within the column, with approximately 16–24px between individual options. The entire selector block is modest in height, roughly equivalent to one or two paragraphs, and serves as an introductory control for the enrollment form that follows. To preserve the letter-like structure, there is around 24–32px of space between the bottom of this selector and the top of the membership form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the column; changes in selection may influence the fields displayed in the membership form below but do not alter the selector’s fixed position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The chapter membership form is one of the most substantial components and appears immediately under the membership option selector, separated by 24–32px of vertical space. It occupies the full width of the central column, aligning flush left and right within that column while leaving the large outer gutters intact. The form fields are stacked vertically in a single column, each label and input pair occupying its own row with 16–24px spacing between rows. Sections within the form (e.g., personal details, contact information, preferences) are subtly separated with slightly larger gaps of about 24–32px, but no heavy frames, maintaining the minimal editorial feel. The submit button is placed left-aligned at the bottom of the form, with 24–32px of space above it and a similar gap below to leave room for feedback messages. On a typical desktop view, the form might fill the lower half of the main content area, but the spacing keeps it airy rather than dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows normally within the column; height expands according to the number of fields. Fields remain in a single vertical stack, adjusting to narrower widths on smaller screens while still centered relative to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The membership form feedback panel appears directly beneath the chapter membership form, with about 16–24px of vertical space separating it from the form’s submit button. It spans the same central column width and is left-aligned, reading like one or two short paragraphs of text. Whether indicating success or error, it is concise and contained in height—typically no more than a few lines. When present, it feels like a natural continuation of the letter-style body, positioned where the reader’s eye lands after submitting. Additional vertical space of about 32–40px is placed below this feedback block before the bottom of the page or any footer content, giving it breathing room and ensuring it is easy to notice without crowding the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the normal flow, appearing only when there is feedback to show; it does not overlap or float, but pushes subsequent content downward if any.

name: Alumni Benefits
title: Alumni Benefits
description: Browse and filter alumni benefits and discounts, including travel offers, using dynamic category, discount, and sort options.

core_features: Fetch available benefit categories, discount ranges, and sort options via getBenefitFilterOptions and use them to render the filter and sort controls.
Read URL parameters (category, min_discount, sort_by, page) on load to initialize filters, sorting, and current page state for deep-linked and returning users.
Search and retrieve a paginated list of benefits via searchBenefits using the active filters, sort option, and page index.
Display a benefits listing with key information from the Benefit model (such as name, category, discount percentage, and partner name).
Allow filtering of benefits by category using the categories returned from getBenefitFilterOptions.
Allow filtering of benefits by minimum discount percentage within the discount_range returned from getBenefitFilterOptions.
Allow sorting of the benefits list using sort options returned from getBenefitFilterOptions (e.g., by discount or alphabetically).
Provide pagination controls to navigate through multiple pages of benefits using searchBenefits page and page_size values.
Enable navigation to benefit_detail.html for any selected benefit card using its Benefit.id as the benefitId URL parameter.
Provide navigation to my_saved.html with section=benefits using a "View Saved Benefits" action.
user_workflows: Browse default benefits: 1) User opens benefits.html without URL parameters. 2) Page controller calls getBenefitFilterOptions to obtain available categories, discount_range, and sort_options. 3) Page controller initializes filters with defaults derived from getBenefitFilterOptions (e.g., no category filter, no minimum discount, and a default sort option). 4) Page controller calls searchBenefits with the default filters, sort option, and page=1. 5) Benefits list and pagination components render results. 6) User scrolls and optionally uses pagination controls to view additional pages; each page selection triggers a new searchBenefits call and updates the page URL parameter.

Use deep-linked filters from another page: 1) User clicks a link from index.html or stay_connected.html that points to benefits.html with category, min_discount, and/or sort_by URL parameters set. 2) On load, page controller reads URL parameters and then calls getBenefitFilterOptions. 3) Page controller validates the incoming category and sort_by against the options returned by getBenefitFilterOptions and clamps or clears min_discount based on discount_range. 4) Normalized values are applied to the filter bar and sort controls. 5) Page controller calls searchBenefits with filters that include the normalized category and min_discount and the specified sort_by and page (defaulting to 1 if missing). 6) User sees a pre-filtered list of benefits aligned with the deep link intent.

Filter and sort benefits interactively: 1) User changes the category selection in the filter bar. 2) Filter bar component emits an onFiltersChange event with the new category, resets page to 1, and updates the category URL parameter; it also removes page from the query if it was present. 3) Page controller receives the updated filters, calls searchBenefits with the new category filter and current min_discount and sort_by. 4) Benefits list and pagination re-render with the updated results. 5) User adjusts the minimum discount filter; after confirming the change (e.g., by blur or an explicit apply action), the filter bar emits onFiltersChange with updated min_discount, and the page controller updates the min_discount URL parameter, resets page to 1, and calls searchBenefits again. 6) User changes the sort option; the filter bar emits onSortChange, which updates sort_by in the URL, resets page to 1, and triggers searchBenefits with the new sort option.

View benefit details from the listing: 1) User identifies a benefit in the list and clicks the benefit card or its associated "View details" link. 2) The benefits list component handles onBenefitClick, reading the selected Benefit.id. 3) The component navigates the browser to benefit_detail.html with benefitId set to the selected id as a URL parameter. 4) The benefit detail page is responsible for showing full terms and allowing saving to the personal list.

Navigate to saved benefits: 1) User wants to see benefits they have saved previously. 2) User clicks the "View Saved Benefits" link or button on the benefits page. 3) The saved-benefits-link component navigates the browser to my_saved.html with section=benefits as a URL parameter. 4) The my_saved.html page handles rendering of saved benefits independent of this page.

Paginate through search results: 1) After a searchBenefits call, the pagination component receives the current page, page_size, and total_results. 2) User clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or a specific page number). 3) Pagination component emits onPageChange with the selected page index. 4) Page controller updates the page URL parameter to the selected page and calls searchBenefits with the current filters and sort_by and the new page. 5) Benefits list re-renders to show the selected page of benefits. 6) If the user changes filters or sort options later, the page is reset to 1 and the page parameter is updated or removed accordingly.
interactions: On page load: The page controller reads category, min_discount, sort_by, and page from window.location.search, then calls getBenefitFilterOptions. After obtaining filter metadata, it normalizes URL parameters, initializes the filter bar and sort controls, and calls searchBenefits with the resulting filters, sort_by, and page.

Category filter change: When the user selects a different category in the filter bar, the component emits an event with the updated category. The page controller updates the category URL parameter (removing it if the user clears the filter), resets page to 1, removes any page parameter from the URL if needed, and calls searchBenefits with the new category filter plus the existing min_discount and sort_by values.

Minimum discount filter change: When the user changes the minimum discount value (via numeric input or slider) and the interaction is committed (e.g., input blur, pressing an "Apply" button, or debounce timeout), the filter bar emits an event with the new min_discount. The page controller validates and clamps this value within the discount_range from getBenefitFilterOptions, updates the min_discount URL parameter (or removes it if the filter is cleared), resets page to 1, and calls searchBenefits with the updated minimum discount filter.

Sort option change: When the user selects a different sort option in the sort control, the filter bar emits an onSortChange event with the new sort_by value. The page controller confirms that the selection is one of the sort_options from getBenefitFilterOptions, updates the sort_by URL parameter, resets page to 1, and calls searchBenefits with the current filters and the new sort_by value.

Pagination click: When the user clicks next, previous, or a page number, the pagination component emits onPageChange with the target page index. The page controller updates the page URL parameter, then calls searchBenefits with the current filters and sort_by and the new page. Results and pagination are updated accordingly.

Benefit card click: When the user clicks on a benefit card area, the benefits list component handles onBenefitClick, retrieving the Benefit.id for that card and navigating to benefit_detail.html with benefitId set to that id in the URL query string.

"View details" link click: When the user clicks a dedicated "View details" link within a benefit card, the same onBenefitClick handler is triggered, navigating to benefit_detail.html with the appropriate benefitId parameter.

"View Saved Benefits" click: When the user clicks the "View Saved Benefits" action, the saved-benefits-link component handles onClick and navigates to my_saved.html with section=benefits as a URL parameter.

Empty-state interaction: If searchBenefits returns total_results equal to zero, the benefits list component displays an empty-state message and may offer a control to clear all filters. If the user chooses to clear filters, the filter bar emits a clear event that removes category and min_discount URL parameters, resets page to 1, keeps or resets sort_by per a default, and the page controller calls searchBenefits with the cleared filters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The single source of truth for page state is the URL query string combined with data returned from getBenefitFilterOptions and searchBenefits. On initialization, the page reads category, min_discount, sort_by, and page from the URL. It then calls getBenefitFilterOptions to retrieve allowed categories, discount_range, and sort_options. The incoming category is valid only if it matches one of the categories.value entries from getBenefitFilterOptions; otherwise it is treated as unset and removed from the URL. The incoming sort_by is valid only if it matches one of the sort_options.value entries; otherwise a default sort option from sort_options is selected and written to the URL (or left implicit, depending on configuration). The incoming min_discount is parsed as a number and clamped within discount_range.min and discount_range.max if necessary. If parsing fails or the value is logically equivalent to 'no filter', the min_discount parameter is removed from the URL and not applied in filters. The page parameter is parsed as a number greater than or equal to 1; if invalid or missing, the page is set to 1 and the URL is updated accordingly or left without a page parameter. After normalization, the filters object passed to searchBenefits contains category only when a valid category is selected and min_discount (as a minimum threshold) only when a valid value is set; the sort_by parameter is set to a valid sort option; and page is at least 1. page_size is obtained from external configuration or higher-level props and is not hardcoded here. Every user interaction that changes filters, sorting, or pagination updates the URL parameters first, ensuring that browser navigation (back/forward) and bookmarks can fully restore the same state. When filters or sort options change, the page is reset to 1, and the page parameter is either set to 1 or removed, depending on the chosen URL strategy. No client-side persistence mechanisms such as localStorage are used; state is derived exclusively from the URL and current API responses. searchBenefits results (benefits array, page, page_size, total_results) are considered transient view state and are not persisted across reloads except via the URL-encoded filters and pagination settings.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, reads and normalizes URL parameters, calls getBenefitFilterOptions and searchBenefits, and distributes filter metadata and search results to child components.

Displays category and minimum discount filters, plus a sort option selector, using metadata from getBenefitFilterOptions and current selections from the page controller; emits events when the user updates filters or sort options.

Renders the list of Benefit records returned from searchBenefits, showing key details such as name, partner, category, and discount, and provides click targets to navigate to individual benefit detail pages.
Displays pagination controls based on page, page_size, and total_results from searchBenefits, and allows users to navigate between pages.
Provides a control that navigates the user to their saved benefits section on my_saved.html with section=benefits in the query string.
Conditionally displays when searchBenefits returns zero results, informing the user that no benefits match the current filters and optionally offering a way to clear filters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a full-width header and navigation, and a centered content column spanning approximately 6–8 columns with large side gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing red utility bar and black main navigation, all Alumni Benefits content is arranged in a simple, top-to-bottom, single-column flow. A centered content column (about 40% of the viewport width on desktop) holds everything: first the page controller (title and brief description), then a standalone Saved Benefits link as a focal line, then the filter bar, followed by the results list, and finally the pagination controls. The empty state occupies the same central column and replaces the results list and pagination when no benefits match the filters.
