layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing red utility bar and black main navigation, all Alumni Benefits content is arranged in a simple, top-to-bottom, single-column flow. A centered content column (about 40% of the viewport width on desktop) holds everything: first the page controller (title and brief description), then a standalone Saved Benefits link as a focal line, then the filter bar, followed by the results list, and finally the pagination controls. The empty state occupies the same central column and replaces the results list and pagination when no benefits match the filters.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header and navigation continue to occupy roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport. The Alumni Benefits content sits in the remaining 75–80%, framed by generous top whitespace: there is a large vertical gap (around 64px) from the bottom of the navigation to the page title. The central content column spans about 35–45% of the viewport, centered, with large white gutters on both sides. Vertical spacing between stacked sections (title block, saved link, filters, list, pagination) follows a 24–48px rhythm, with the biggest gaps around the initial title area and between major functional groups.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps a low-density, editorial feel: minimal visual decoration, a lot of white space, and a restrained number of elements visible at once. The page controller and saved link areas are very sparse with large margins. The filter bar is more compact but still breathable, with clear padding. The results list shows vertically separated rows with generous line height and item spacing, avoiding a card-heavy look. The empty state is treated like a letter-style message block, with plenty of surrounding whitespace.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye travels from the bright red utility bar and black navigation down into the white content area, where a single, left-aligned heading ('Alumni Benefits') within the centered column anchors attention much like 'Dear Alumni,' in the mockup. Below it, a concise description guides understanding, then the standalone Saved Benefits link acts as a focal text moment similar to the LinkedIn URL in the mockup. From there, attention moves to the horizontal filter bar, then flows downward through the stacked benefit entries, pausing briefly at each title. Pagination at the bottom provides a clear visual endpoint. In the empty state, after the filter bar the eye lands on a centered, message-like block that acts as the main conclusion of the page.

layout.overall_description: On desktop, the page opens with the familiar institutional header: the full-width red utility bar at the very top, then the black main navigation bar, and any slim sub-navigation beneath. Below this, the Alumni Benefits page content begins after a deliberate, airy gap, so the first line of 'Alumni Benefits' appears roughly one-quarter down the viewport in a narrow, centered column. This column is left-aligned internally but framed by wide white gutters on both sides, echoing a formal letter on a clean sheet. Directly under the title and a brief explanatory line, a single line of linked text for saved benefits stands alone, acting as a subtle but clear focal point in the otherwise sparse area. Further down, the filter bar forms a compact horizontal band within the same column, containing drop-downs and controls laid out in a simple row. Beneath that, the benefits appear as a vertical sequence of understated rows confined to this central column, each separated by comfortable whitespace, allowing the user to read them almost like items in a printed list. At the very bottom of this stack, pagination controls sit centered within the column, followed by a generous margin of empty white space to visually close the page. When no benefits match the filters, instead of the list and pagination, a single empty-state message block appears below the filters in the same column, framed with generous top and bottom padding, preserving the calm, editorial tone of the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller sits at the top of the main content area, directly beneath the site’s stacked header/navigation bands, but separated from them by a pronounced vertical gap of about 64px. It lives inside a centered content column that spans roughly 40% of the viewport width on desktop (constrained with a max width, e.g., 720–840px), with large, equal white gutters to the left and right. Within this column, the main heading 'Alumni Benefits' is left-aligned, near the top of the column, followed immediately by one or two short lines of descriptive text beneath it. Vertical spacing within this block is restrained but clear: about 8–16px between heading and description, and about 24px of space below the description before the next element. The entire block feels like the opening salutation of a formal letter—no boxes, no background shading, just clean typography and white space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, participates in the normal document flow under the global header. On smaller viewports, the column width adapts up to nearly full width (with 16–24px side padding), but the vertical spacing relationships and left-aligned text are preserved.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved benefits link appears directly below the page controller, within the same centered content column. It is rendered as a single, standalone line of text—e.g., 'View your saved benefits'—styled as a simple text link in the brand’s accent color. It is left-aligned with the heading and paragraph above, with approximately 24px of vertical space separating it from the page description. There is another 24–32px of whitespace below it before the filter bar begins, so the link visually floats in its own band of white space. This mirrors the mockup’s standalone LinkedIn URL: it becomes a clear focal moment in the middle of an otherwise text-only section, but without any decorative framing. No icons or buttons are necessary; its visual impact comes from being the only colored, underlined text in a very clean area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the vertical flow. It does not stick or float; as the page scrolls, it moves normally with the content. On mobile, it remains left-aligned within the responsive content padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is positioned below the saved benefits link, still within the same centered content column, maintaining the 40% viewport width on desktop. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical space between the link and the top edge of the filter bar, so the bar feels clearly separated as a functional band following the more editorial intro. The bar itself is a horizontal strip containing filtering controls (such as category dropdowns, discount range selectors, and sort options) arranged left to right. Each control has generous horizontal padding (about 16px) and vertical padding in the 8–12px range, respecting the mockup’s button and nav spacing. The bar can optionally use a very subtle bottom border or light divider line to visually anchor it, but the background remains white to maintain the minimal, flat aesthetic. The right side of the bar may contain secondary controls like 'Clear filters', aligned to the right edge of the column. The total height of the bar is modest, around 48–64px, so it feels compact but not cramped. Below the bar, another 24px of whitespace leads into the results list, maintaining the linear, segmented flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static band within the content column; it scrolls with the page and does not remain sticky. On medium and small screens, controls wrap to multiple rows within the same band, still aligned to the left with consistent vertical spacing (8–16px between rows), keeping the bar visually cohesive without introducing new background panels.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list sits directly beneath the filter bar, within the same centered content column. A modest gap of about 24px separates the bottom of the filter bar from the top of the first result. Each benefit is displayed as a full-width row within the column, left-aligned, resembling a simple textual listing rather than a card grid. A typical row might show the benefit name in a slightly larger or bolder type on the first line, with supporting details (provider, discount percentage, category tags, brief description) on subsequent lines. Vertical spacing within each row is generous (8–16px between its internal text lines), and there is 16–24px of space between one benefit row and the next. Optionally, a very light divider line can appear beneath each row to maintain order without disrupting the minimal editorial feel. The list extends downward, occupying most of the remaining vertical space; it can scroll as long as needed. The wide left and right gutters keep the list visually contained, so the user’s attention is channeled straight down the center column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, scrollable content within the central column. It expands vertically based on the number of results. When there are no results, this component is not rendered or is visually hidden, and the empty-state component is shown instead in the same position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are placed below the results list, acting as the visual endpoint of the page content. There is a clear vertical separation of about 32px from the last benefit row to the top of the pagination area. The controls (page numbers, next/previous arrows) are horizontally centered within the same content column, forming a compact control cluster rather than stretching edge to edge. Each pagination chip or button respects the spacing pattern seen in navigation items: around 16px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, with adequate spacing (8–12px) between adjacent page numbers. The set of controls fits comfortably within one line on desktop; if it wraps on smaller screens, the second line sits just below with 8px spacing. Below the pagination, a larger bottom margin of 48–64px leaves a comfortable white buffer before the end of the page, reinforcing the calm, editorial finish.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, appears only when there are enough results to require multiple pages. It scrolls with the rest of the content and remains within the central column on all viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state display occupies the same spatial slot as the results list and pagination when no benefits match the current filters. It appears directly below the filter bar, at the same 24–32px vertical distance the list would typically start, within the centered content column. The empty state is treated like a short, formal message: a brief heading or statement (e.g., 'No benefits match your criteria') with one or two lines of explanatory text and possibly a quiet call to action (like 'Reset filters'). The text is left-aligned to echo the letter-like style, but the block is visually centered within a generous vertical envelope: around 48–64px of padding above the first line of the message and 48–64px below the last line, so the content feels intentional and framed by white space. No large illustrations or colorful graphics are used; at most, a small, simple icon might appear to the left of the heading, aligned with the text baseline. The overall effect is calm and understated, consistent with the institutional, editorial tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the results list and pagination—rendered only when there are zero matching benefits. It scrolls with the rest of the content and remains within the central column, adapting responsively on smaller screens while preserving the generous vertical framing.

name: Benefit Detail
title: Benefit Detail
description: Displays full details for a single alumni benefit, shows whether it is saved by the current user, and allows saving or removing it from the user's saved benefits list, with navigation back to benefits results or the saved benefits page.

core_features: Read the benefitId (and optional return_to) from the URL query string and load full benefit details using getBenefitDetail.
Display all available benefit information including name, partner, category, discount information, eligibility, redemption instructions, exclusions, geographic limitations, and date range.
Indicate whether the benefit is currently active or outside its valid date range, based on start_date and end_date fields.
Show the current save state for this benefit (saved or not) using is_saved and saved_benefit_id from getBenefitDetail.
Allow the user to save the benefit to their personal list, optionally including a note, via saveBenefit.
Allow the user to remove the benefit from their saved list via unsaveBenefit.
Allow the user to view and update any existing note associated with the saved benefit using saveBenefit.
Provide a Back to results link that uses the return_to URL parameter when available, or falls back to the main benefits listing.
Provide a link to the My Saved page, pre-focused on benefits (my_saved.html?section=benefits).
user_workflows:
View benefit details from a listing:
1. User opens benefit_detail.html with a benefitId parameter in the URL (optionally with return_to).
2. On initial load, the page reads benefitId (and return_to if present) from the query string.
3. The page calls getBenefitDetail with benefitId.
4. While data is loading, a loading state is shown and all interactive controls are disabled.
5. When the response returns, the page displays benefit fields (name, category, discount, eligibility, redemption instructions, exclusions, geographic and date limitations, terms link if available).
6. The page also reads is_saved, saved_benefit_id, and saved_note to configure the Save/Remove button and note field.
7. The user can click Back to results to return to the previous filtered benefits list (if return_to present) or to the generic benefits listing.

Save a benefit from the detail page:
1. User is viewing a benefit that is not currently saved (is_saved is false).
2. The Save/Remove control shows a state indicating the benefit is not saved (e.g., a 'Save to my benefits' button and an optional note field).
3. The user optionally types a note in the note field.
4. The user activates the Save action (e.g., clicks the Save button).
5. The page triggers saveBenefit with benefitId and the note value.
6. While the saveBenefit call is in progress, the Save action is disabled and a progress indication is shown.
7. On success, the component uses the returned SavedBenefit to update local state (mark is_saved true, store saved_benefit.id and note, clear any error message).
8. The UI updates the control to indicate that the benefit is now saved (e.g., button label changes to 'Remove from my saved benefits').

Unsave a benefit from the detail page:
1. User is viewing a benefit that is currently saved (is_saved is true from getBenefitDetail or a prior saveBenefit call).
2. The Save/Remove control shows that the benefit is saved (e.g., a 'Remove from my saved benefits' button and a note field prefilled with saved_note).
3. The user activates the Remove action.
4. The page triggers unsaveBenefit with benefitId.
5. While unsaveBenefit is in progress, the Remove action is disabled and a progress indication is shown.
6. On success, the component updates the local saved state (is_saved false, clear saved_benefit_id, clear note field or mark as non-persisted) and shows a confirmation message.
7. The UI returns to the 'not saved' state, allowing the user to save the benefit again if desired.

Edit or add a note for a saved benefit:
1. User views a benefit that is already saved (is_saved true, saved_note may be present).
2. The note field is populated with saved_note from getBenefitDetail.
3. The user updates the text in the note field.
4. The user activates an Update/Save Note action.
5. The page calls saveBenefit with benefitId and the updated note.
6. While saveBenefit is in progress, the note action is disabled and a loading indication is shown.
7. On success, the component updates the stored note from saved_benefit.note and shows a success message without changing the saved/unsaved state.

Navigate to My Saved benefits:
1. From the Benefit Detail page, the user activates the 'View My Saved Benefits' link or button.
2. The page navigates to my_saved.html with query parameter section=benefits.
3. The user sees their saved benefits list and can navigate back to Benefit Detail pages as needed.
interactions: Page load with URL parameters: On initial load or when the benefitId query parameter changes, the page triggers getBenefitDetail using the current benefitId. If benefitId is missing or empty, the page shows an error state and disables all benefit-specific UI.

Back to results link: When the user clicks the Back to results control, if a return_to parameter is present in the current URL, the page navigates to that URL or context; if not present, the page navigates to benefits.html without additional parameters.
View My Saved Benefits link: When the user clicks the 'View My Saved Benefits' control, the page navigates to my_saved.html?section=benefits.

Save benefit action: When the user clicks the Save button while the benefit is not saved, the page validates that benefitId is available, reads the note input value, and calls saveBenefit(benefitId, note). On success, local state is updated to reflect that the benefit is now saved, the note field remains populated with the saved note, and a confirmation message is shown.

Unsave benefit action: When the user clicks the Remove button while the benefit is saved, the page validates that benefitId is available and calls unsaveBenefit(benefitId). On success, local state is updated to reflect that the benefit is no longer saved, the note field is cleared or disabled, and a confirmation message is shown.

Note field interactions: The note input is editable when the benefit is saved or about to be saved. Editing the note does not immediately call an interface; the call is only made when the user explicitly triggers a Save or Update action, which calls saveBenefit with the current note text.

Error handling: If getBenefitDetail, saveBenefit, or unsaveBenefit returns an error condition (e.g., success = false), the page displays an error message component and leaves the previous state visible. The user can retry the action by clicking the corresponding button again.

Reload behavior: If the user changes the URL benefitId manually or navigates via an internal link that updates benefitId, the page re-runs getBenefitDetail for the new benefitId and resets local saved-state UI based on the new response.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for the Benefit Detail page is determined by the URL query parameters and the data returned from getBenefitDetail. The benefitId parameter is required; without it, the page shows an error and does not call any interfaces. On each load, the page reads benefitId from the query string and calls getBenefitDetail(benefitId). The response provides benefit (Benefit entity) and save-state metadata (is_saved, saved_benefit_id, saved_note). These values initialize page components (display content, save/unsave button state, and note value). The return_to parameter is optional and is treated as an opaque token or encoded URL string. If present, it is used as the destination for the Back to results link. If not present, the Back to results link defaults to benefits.html. Navigation to My Saved uses a fixed URL with a query parameter (my_saved.html?section=benefits). After a successful saveBenefit or unsaveBenefit call, the page updates its in-memory state (is_saved, saved_benefit_id, saved_note) to match the returned data; it is not necessary to modify the URL parameters. Reloading the page will always re-derive the authoritative state from getBenefitDetail using the current benefitId in the URL.

components.functionality: On initial page load and whenever the benefitId query parameter changes, this component reads benefitId from the URL, invokes getBenefitDetail, and exposes the loaded data (benefit, is_saved, saved_benefit_id, saved_note) along with loading and error states to child components.

Displays the primary benefit information from the loaded Benefit object, such as name, headline, partner_name, category, discount_percentage, discount_description, description, and any terms_url link. It is read-only and updates whenever the bound Benefit data changes.

Shows structured metadata for the benefit including category, eligibility text, geographic_limitations, start_date, end_date, and a computed status indicator (e.g., active, not yet started, expired) based on the dates. It updates whenever the Benefit data changes.

Presents redemption_instructions, exclusions, and any additional descriptive fields from the Benefit entity in a dedicated section so users can understand how to use the benefit. This section is purely informational and read-only.

Manages the saved/unsaved state for the current benefit. It renders a button whose label and behavior depend on is_saved, and an optional note text area pre-populated with saved_note when applicable. When the user saves, it calls saveBenefit with benefitId and the current note. When the user removes the benefit, it calls unsaveBenefit with benefitId. It handles loading states during API calls and updates local saved state based on responses.

Allows a user to update the note associated with an already saved benefit without changing its saved/unsaved state. It listens to saved_note from getBenefitDetail or from the latest saveBenefit response, pre-fills an input with that value, and on submission calls saveBenefit with benefitId and the updated note value. On success it updates the displayed note and a status message.

Provides navigation elements related to the Benefit Detail context: a Back to results link whose destination is derived from the return_to URL parameter when present (otherwise benefits.html), and a 'View My Saved Benefits' link that navigates to my_saved.html with section=benefits. It reads the current URL parameters but does not modify them.

Displays transient or persistent messages based on the outcomes of getBenefitDetail, saveBenefit, and unsaveBenefit calls (e.g., load errors, save success, remove success, validation errors). It subscribes to events from the data-loader and saved-benefit-controller components and shows messages accordingly.

Coordinates visibility and interactivity of other components based on the loading and error state from benefit-detail-data-loader. While data is loading, it shows a loading indicator and disables save/unsave controls. If an error occurs (e.g., missing benefitId or failed getBenefitDetail), it hides the detail and save components and shows an error message instead.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a full-width header and navigation, and a centered content column spanning approximately 6–8 columns with large side gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global utility header, main navigation, and slim sub-nav, the Benefit Detail page content is presented as a single, narrow, centered column that reads like a formal informational letter. Within this column, components are stacked vertically in a linear top-to-bottom order: first the benefit-navigation-controls, then any benefit-status-messages, followed by the benefit-main-detail-panel (including the title and primary description), with the benefit-metadata-panel and saved-benefit-controller (plus saved-note-update-control) integrated into the upper portion of this main block. The benefit-redemption-panel appears as a clearly separated section later in the same column. The data-loader and loading-and-error-guard sit conceptually over or in place of this stack when active rather than occupying their own permanent space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global utility header, main navigation, and slim sub-nav, the Benefit Detail page content is presented as a single, narrow, centered column that reads like a formal informational letter. Within this column, components are stacked vertically in a linear top-to-bottom order: first the benefit-navigation-controls, then any benefit-status-messages, followed by the benefit-main-detail-panel (including the title and primary description), with the benefit-metadata-panel and saved-benefit-controller (plus saved-note-update-control) integrated into the upper portion of this main block. The benefit-redemption-panel appears as a clearly separated section later in the same column. The data-loader and loading-and-error-guard sit conceptually over or in place of this stack when active rather than occupying their own permanent space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global red utility bar, black main navigation bar, and slim sub-nav collectively occupy the top ~20–25% of the viewport, as in the mockup. The remaining ~75–80% is a white content canvas. Within this canvas, the benefit detail content is constrained to a centered column occupying approximately 35–45% of the viewport width (max-width around a typical 6–8-column span in a 12-column grid), leaving large left and right gutters. Vertical spacing from the bottom of the nav stack to the first in-column element (benefit-navigation-controls) is generous (about 48–64px), mirroring the large gap before 'Dear Alumni' in the mockup. Within the column, different sections are separated by 32–48px of vertical space, while paragraphs and closely related controls have more modest gaps of 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout emphasizes low content density, especially in the main detail and redemption text: generous line height, short paragraphs, and consistent 16–24px spacing between paragraphs, with no sidebars or visual clutter. Interactive elements (save toggle, navigation links) are kept visually light and text-like, with flat styling and modest paddings consistent with the 16px horizontal and 8–12px vertical conventions. Message states and metadata are compact and unobtrusive, preserving the overall editorial, letter-like feel of the central column.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user's eye follows a strong top-to-bottom flow. After scanning the bright red utility bar and dark main navigation (outside this specification), the eye drops into the white content area and first encounters the subtle, text-based benefit-navigation-controls at the top left of the centered column. Any benefit-status-messages directly beneath form a brief point of attention. The eye then moves down to the main benefit title and introductory text within the benefit-main-detail-panel, with the saved-benefit-controller aligned to the right as a discreet but clear action point. The benefit-metadata-panel is scanned quickly as supporting detail before the reader continues through the main paragraphs. Later, the benefit-redemption-panel forms a distinct focal section, separated by generous whitespace, with any key redemption URL or code presented on its own line as a focal moment akin to the LinkedIn link in the mockup. The page concludes with any closing informational text, maintaining a quiet, linear reading experience.

layout.overall_description: The page sits beneath the existing institutional header: a red utility strip at the very top, a black main navigation bar directly under it, and a slim sub-nav row (for items like Announcements and Events) forming a dense but orderly band that fills roughly the upper quarter of the viewport. Below this, the layout opens into a largely white canvas. Centered within this canvas is a narrow text column, about two-fifths of the viewport wide, with large, calm gutters on both sides that echo the appearance of a printed letter centered on a desk. At the top of this column, after a generous vertical gap, a small, understated line of navigation links allows the user to go back to benefit results or to the saved benefits page; these links are left-aligned and text-like, acting as a quiet wayfinding element rather than a bold menu. Immediately below, occasional status messages appear as a single, subtle line or lightly tinted band. Further down, the benefit’s title and introduction anchor the column: the title appears at the upper left of the column, while the save/unsave control sits aligned to the right edge on the same line or just below, giving a sense of corporate utility without disturbing the editorial tone. Supporting metadata is set as a compact block near the title, followed by the main body text: short paragraphs of copy, each separated by consistent, comfortable spacing, forming a readable narrative about the benefit. Slightly further down, a distinct but still typographically driven redemption section begins, separated from the main description by a larger vertical gap, and may include a single-line URL or code on its own line, mirroring the LinkedIn focal line in the original mockup. There are no cards or sidebars; all components stack vertically inside this central column. Loading and error states temporarily replace or overlay this column with centered messages or spinners, but when the data is ready, the page settles back into its calm, linear, letter-like composition.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader does not occupy a permanent visible slot in the layout; instead, when active, it overlays the main centered content column. It is anchored to the same horizontal center as the benefit content, constrained to the width of the column (about 35–45% of the viewport), and appears approximately in the upper-middle of the remaining viewport (roughly one-third down from the bottom of the nav stack). Visually, it might present as a small spinner and a single line of text, centered both horizontally within the column and given ample white space above and below so that it feels like a quiet, temporary state rather than a heavy card. The surrounding white canvas and gutters remain visible, emphasizing the loader as a transient layer on top of the future content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional overlay centered within the main content column; appears on top of other components during loading and disappears completely once data is ready, without shifting the underlying layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main detail panel forms the core, letter-like body of the page and is positioned in the center of the white content area. It begins after the benefit-navigation-controls and any status messages, with a vertical gap of roughly 32–48px above its title. The panel is not a card; instead, it is a typographic block contained within a centered column occupying approximately 35–45% of the viewport width, aligned with the general 6–8-column span of the grid. At its top left, the benefit title is rendered as the first prominent line, aligning with the left edge of the text column. The saved-benefit-controller is visually tied to this panel, typically aligned to the right edge of the same horizontal band as the title or just below it, maintaining an unobtrusive but accessible CTA. Directly beneath the title area, the benefit-metadata-panel appears as compact supporting information, followed by the main textual description. The body of the detail panel is composed of multiple short paragraphs, each separated by about 16–24px of vertical space and set with generous line height, creating an airy, editorial feel. There are no borders or background blocks separating this panel from the page; it simply inhabits the central column, with large left and right gutters that frame the text in white space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the normal document flow, centered within the main column; expands vertically according to content while maintaining fixed horizontal alignment and max width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metadata panel is integrated near the top of the main detail panel, acting like the small, formal header details of a letter (e.g., date, category, provider). It spans the full width of the central content column but remains visually compact. It is positioned directly below the benefit title (and aligned under the left edge of the title), with about 8–16px of vertical space separating it from the title line and another 16–24px before the first main paragraph. Its content is arranged as one or two lines of small, muted text—possibly using separators or a light typographic divider—but without any heavy borders or backgrounds. This keeps the metadata readable yet unobtrusive, letting the main paragraphs below remain the primary focus while still maintaining a clear, ordered hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline block within the main detail panel’s flow; it does not alter the width of the column and simply adds a short vertical segment between the title and the descriptive paragraphs.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The redemption panel is a distinct textual section positioned below the main descriptive paragraphs within the same centered column. After the final paragraph of the main detail panel, there is a generous vertical gap of roughly 32–48px, creating a clear visual separation before the redemption instructions begin. The panel’s heading (e.g., 'How to Redeem') aligns with the left edge of the column, using similar typographic treatment to a section heading but remaining in plain, flat style—no card framing. Below the heading, the redemption content is presented as short paragraphs or a concise list, each item separated by 16–24px of vertical space. Any crucial URL, code, or email address is placed on its own line near the middle of this panel, left-aligned and possibly styled in a corporate blue or underlined, creating a focal moment similar to the standalone LinkedIn link in the mockup. The redemption panel occupies the same width as the rest of the column, with white gutters on both sides, and visually reads as the second major block of the letter-like narrative.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the main column, stacking below the main detail panel; its height grows with content, while maintaining the same horizontal alignment and width as the rest of the editorial text.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-benefit-controller is positioned at the top of the main detail area, visually paired with the benefit title. It sits inside the same centered column as the text, aligned to the right edge of that column. On wider screens, it is placed on the same visual row as the title or directly beneath it with a small 8–12px gap, creating a subtle left-right balance: title on the left, action on the right. The control itself appears as a flat, text-forward button or toggle (e.g., 'Save benefit' / 'Saved') with horizontal padding of about 16px and vertical padding of about 8–12px, consistent with the mockup’s button rhythm, but without heavy shadows or gradients. A small icon may precede or follow the label, but the component overall stays low-profile so it doesn’t overpower the editorial text. On narrower viewports, the controller wraps beneath the title and aligns left within the column, maintaining comfortable 16–24px vertical spacing before the metadata and body text that follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline action aligned to the right within the main column; remains in the normal flow (no fixed or sticky behavior), with responsive stacking under the title on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-note-update-control appears immediately below the saved-benefit-controller, within the upper portion of the main detail panel, but only when the benefit is in a saved state. It is aligned with the left edge of the text column, creating a visual hierarchy where the action toggle sits to the right/top and the note editor is integrated into the body flow. Vertically, it sits about 16px below the save toggle and about 16–24px above the first main descriptive paragraph. The control itself is a minimal inline form: a single-line field or small text area that spans most of the column width, using a simple hairline border and comfortable internal padding (around 8px), avoiding any card-like framing. It reads as an extension of the letter, almost like a marginal note, and does not introduce extra visual weight beyond its simple form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional inline form within the main column’s flow; appears and disappears without altering the column’s width, and when present, it simply increases the vertical height between the top controls and the main text.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The benefit-navigation-controls form the first visible content element within the white content area beneath the global header and nav. They are positioned at the very top of the centered column, following a large vertical gap of approximately 48–64px from the bottom edge of the slim sub-nav bar, mirroring the mockup’s framing before 'Dear Alumni'. The controls are left-aligned text links or small buttons (e.g., '← Back to benefits' and 'View saved benefits'), arranged horizontally with about 16–24px of horizontal spacing between them. They occupy only a small portion of the column’s vertical space, with no background bar or card, to remain understated and not compete with the main content. The width is constrained to the central column; the gutters on the left and right remain white and empty, reinforcing the sense that these are local navigation aids for this specific 'letter'. A modest 16–24px of vertical space separates these controls from any status messages that may follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigation row at the top of the content column; remains in the normal flow and scrolls away with the page (no sticky behavior).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status messages area sits directly below the benefit-navigation-controls within the same centered column. It spans the full width of the column but typically has a low height, just enough for a line or two of text. When active (e.g., after saving or encountering a minor error), it appears as either a single line of colored text or a lightly tinted, full-width strip with modest vertical padding (around 8–12px) and 8–16px of vertical margin above and below, keeping with the restrained, editorial style. The top margin to the navigation controls is small (about 16px), while the bottom margin leading into the main detail panel is slightly larger (about 24–32px), visually separating transient messages from the long-lived content. When there are no messages, this block effectively collapses, leaving the navigation controls and main detail panel closer together without disrupting the overall vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Dynamic block in the normal flow; its presence or absence adjusts the vertical spacing between navigation controls and the main detail panel but does not affect the column width or alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-and-error-guard governs the entire central content column. When an error occurs or when the data cannot be displayed, it replaces the usual stack of components (navigation controls, messages, detail, redemption) with a single, centered message inside the same column. This message block is aligned with the column’s left edge but vertically centered within the usable white canvas beneath the global header—roughly the middle of the 75–80% content area. It may contain a short heading and explanatory text, laid out in the same line-height and paragraph spacing as the rest of the page to preserve the letter-like feel. Large white margins surround this message, and no other content is visible in the column, reinforcing the idea that this state is a full contextual replacement rather than just another line in the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional gate for the content column; when triggered, it hides or prevents rendering of other column components and occupies the central column as a single block. It scrolls normally but typically fits within the initial viewport height.

name: Alumni Directory Search
title: Alumni Directory Search
description: Search and filter the public alumni directory by name, location, industry, and graduation year range, with sortable, paginated results that link to individual alumni profiles.

core_features: Initialize directory filters (industries, location suggestions, graduation year range, sort options) using getDirectoryFilterOptions and pre-populate the search form from URL parameters.
Allow users to perform basic and advanced searches across alumni profiles using a combined search form (free-text query, city, state, industry, graduation year minimum and maximum).
Execute directory searches via searchAlumniProfiles using the current filter values, sort option, and pagination state, and display results as a list of alumni profiles.
Provide sortable results using a sort control whose options come from getDirectoryFilterOptions.sort_options, updating the searchAlumniProfiles call when sort order changes.
Display a paginated results list using searchAlumniProfiles pagination fields (page, page_size, total_results) and allow navigation between pages via URL parameter page.
Let users navigate to a selected alumnus/alumna profile detail page (alumni_profile.html) using the alumniProfileId from each result.
Provide a direct navigation link to the user's saved contact list (my_saved.html) with section=contacts.
Support deep linking by reading incoming URL parameters for filters, sort, and page, and keeping the URL in sync with the current search state (no localStorage usage).
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Landing on the directory with pre-configured filters from another page: (1) User clicks a link to alumni_directory.html on index.html that includes one or more URL parameters (e.g., city, state, industry, grad_year_min, grad_year_max, sort_by, q). (2) On load, the page reads these URL parameters and initializes the search form fields and sort control accordingly. (3) The page calls getDirectoryFilterOptions to retrieve available industries, location suggestions, graduation year bounds, and sort options, then completes form setup. (4) Once filters and sort options are available, the page constructs a filters object from the URL parameters and current form values and calls searchAlumniProfiles with filters, sort_by, page (from URL or default), and a configured page_size. (5) The results list and pagination controls render based on the searchAlumniProfiles response. (6) The user can further refine filters or sort order; any change updates the URL parameters and triggers a new search.

Workflow 2 – Running a new search from the directory page: (1) User edits one or more fields in the search form (e.g., enters a name or employer in the free-text field, adjusts city/state, selects an industry, or updates graduation year minimum/maximum). (2) User submits the search form. (3) The form submission handler sets page to 1, merges the current form values into the query string (city, state, industry, grad_year_min, grad_year_max, q, sort_by), and updates the browser URL. (4) The handler builds a filters object from these values and calls searchAlumniProfiles with the selected sort_by and page=1. (5) The results and pagination controls re-render with the new data. (6) The user may repeat this process to iteratively refine the search.

Workflow 3 – Changing sort order on the results: (1) User selects a different sort option from the sort control (e.g., switching from one sort criteria to another). (2) The sort control handler updates the sort_by URL parameter, resets page to 1, and preserves the current filter parameters (city, state, industry, grad_year_min, grad_year_max, q). (3) The handler calls searchAlumniProfiles with the unchanged filters, the new sort_by, and page=1. (4) The results list re-renders in the new order and the pagination controls update accordingly.

Workflow 4 – Paginating through search results: (1) From a search results view, the user clicks a pagination control (e.g., next page, previous page, or a specific page number). (2) The pagination handler reads the requested page number and updates the page URL parameter while preserving the current filter and sort_by parameters. (3) The handler calls searchAlumniProfiles with the existing filters, current sort_by, and the new page value. (4) The results list scrolls or updates to display the requested page, and the pagination controls highlight the new current page. (5) The user can continue navigating across pages, with each interaction updating the URL and searchAlumniProfiles call.

Workflow 5 – Opening an alumni profile from the results: (1) In the results list, the user clicks on either the alumnus/alumna name or a dedicated "View profile" link within a result row. (2) The click handler reads the id from the selected AlumniProfile object in the profiles array returned by searchAlumniProfiles. (3) The browser navigates to alumni_profile.html with the alumniProfileId parameter set to that id (e.g., alumni_profile.html?alumniProfileId={id}). (4) The profile page is responsible for loading and displaying details for that alumniProfileId.

Workflow 6 – Navigating to the saved contact list: (1) User clicks a "View My Contact List" link on the directory page. (2) The click handler navigates to my_saved.html with the URL parameter section=contacts (e.g., my_saved.html?section=contacts). (3) The my_saved.html page uses section=contacts to show the user's contact list; no data is modified from the directory page itself.

Workflow 7 – Clearing all filters and starting over: (1) User clicks a "Clear filters" or equivalent control in the results summary or search form. (2) The handler removes filter-related URL parameters (city, state, industry, grad_year_min, grad_year_max, q) and resets page to 1 and sort_by to an application-defined default, updating the URL. (3) The form fields are reset to match the cleared state. (4) The handler calls searchAlumniProfiles with an empty or minimal filters object, the default sort_by, and page=1. (5) The page displays the unfiltered (or minimally filtered) alumni results based on application rules.
interactions: Typing into the free-text search field updates the local form state for q; the search is applied when the form is submitted.

Typing into city and state fields can trigger optional client-side suggestions based on getDirectoryFilterOptions.city_suggestions and state_suggestions; selecting a suggestion populates the corresponding field.

Selecting an industry from a dropdown populated via getDirectoryFilterOptions.industries updates the local form state and, upon form submission, updates the industry URL parameter and filters in the searchAlumniProfiles call.

Adjusting graduation year minimum and maximum fields (e.g., via numeric inputs or a range UI) updates local form state; on submission these values map to grad_year_min and grad_year_max URL parameters and to filters in searchAlumniProfiles.
Clicking an "Advanced filters" toggle (if implemented) expands or collapses additional filter fields (e.g., industry, graduation year range) purely in client state without additional API calls.

Submitting the search form consolidates all current form inputs, writes them into the URL parameters (city, state, industry, grad_year_min, grad_year_max, q, sort_by, page=1), and then calls searchAlumniProfiles using those values.
Changing the sort dropdown fires an onChange handler that updates the sort_by URL parameter, resets page=1, and re-issues the searchAlumniProfiles request with current filters and the new sort_by.

Clicking pagination controls (next, previous, numbered page links) fires an onClick handler that updates the page URL parameter and calls searchAlumniProfiles with the same filters and sort_by but the new page value.
Clicking an alumni name or "View profile" link in a result row navigates to alumni_profile.html with alumniProfileId set from the selected AlumniProfile.id.
Clicking the "View My Contact List" link navigates to my_saved.html with section=contacts, without modifying any search or profile data.

Clicking a "Clear filters" control resets form fields, removes relevant filter parameters from the URL, resets page to 1, optionally sets sort_by to a default from getDirectoryFilterOptions.sort_options, and triggers a new search via searchAlumniProfiles.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page reads URL parameters city, state, industry, grad_year_min, grad_year_max, sort_by, q, and page. These values initialize the internal search state and form fields. The page then calls getDirectoryFilterOptions once to obtain industries, location suggestions, graduation_year_range bounds, and sort_options; these values populate dropdowns and provide validation ranges (e.g., clamping user-entered graduation years to the min/max provided). After filter options are available, the page constructs a filters object for searchAlumniProfiles by conditionally including fields that are present in the current state or URL: filters may include city, state, industry, graduation_year_min, graduation_year_max, and q (for free-text search). The sort_by parameter for searchAlumniProfiles comes from the URL's sort_by value if present; otherwise, the component uses an application-defined default sort option that can be derived from getDirectoryFilterOptions.sort_options or global configuration (not hardcoded in this specification). The page parameter for searchAlumniProfiles is taken from the URL page parameter if valid; otherwise it falls back to a default page index (e.g., 1) determined by application configuration. The page_size parameter for searchAlumniProfiles is supplied from application-level configuration or context, not hardcoded here. Whenever the user submits the search form, changes the sort option, or interacts with pagination, the handler updates the browser URL query string to reflect the new state (ensuring city, state, industry, grad_year_min, grad_year_max, q, sort_by, and page are current) and then immediately calls searchAlumniProfiles with a filters object built from those same values. The UI components always render from the latest searchAlumniProfiles response, not from cached or localStorage data. Deep links are supported because any time the page loads with URL parameters present, those parameters drive both form initialization and the first searchAlumniProfiles call. If the URL contains parameters that are inconsistent with getDirectoryFilterOptions (e.g., a sort_by value not found in sort_options), the page can either ignore or normalize those values (e.g., revert to a default) before calling searchAlumniProfiles, and should update the URL accordingly. All state is thus synchronized through URL parameters and live interface responses, with no reliance on localStorage or hardcoded static data.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of URL parameters related to the directory search (city, state, industry, grad_year_min, grad_year_max, sort_by, q, page). On load, it parses existing URL parameters into an internal state object that other components consume. On any filter, sort, or pagination change, it updates the URL query string to reflect the current state and exposes the updated state to trigger a new searchAlumniProfiles call.
data_binding: getDirectoryFilterOptions (for validating sort_by and filter ranges)

components.functionality: Fetches directory filter metadata via getDirectoryFilterOptions when the page initializes. Supplies industries, graduation_year_range, city_suggestions, state_suggestions, country_suggestions, and sort_options to other components such as the search form and sort control.
data_binding: AlumniProfile (indirectly, via compatible fields like industry and graduation_year)

components.functionality: Provides basic and advanced search controls for the alumni directory. Fields include a free-text query (mapped to q), city, state, industry (dropdown populated from getDirectoryFilterOptions.industries), and graduation year minimum and maximum (guided by getDirectoryFilterOptions.graduation_year_range). It initializes each field from the current URL state and, on submission, composes an updated search state (filters, sort_by, page=1), writes it to the URL via the state manager, and triggers a new searchAlumniProfiles call.

Renders a sort dropdown for the results, using options from getDirectoryFilterOptions.sort_options. It initializes the selected option from the URL sort_by parameter (or an application default if none is present or valid). When the user selects a new sort option, it updates sort_by in the URL state, resets page to the first page, and triggers searchAlumniProfiles with the unchanged filters and new sort_by.

Orchestrates calls to searchAlumniProfiles whenever search-relevant state changes (filters, sort_by, page). It listens for updates from the URL state manager and the filter/sort components, constructs the filters object (including fields such as city, state, industry, graduation_year_min, graduation_year_max, q if present), applies the current sort_by and page, and uses a configured page_size. It then exposes profiles, page, page_size, and total_results to downstream components (summary, list, pagination).

Displays a textual summary of the current search results (e.g., total_results and current page context) and a concise representation of active filters (such as city, state, industry, and graduation year range) based on the current URL state and latest searchAlumniProfiles response. Provides a "Clear filters" control that resets filter-related URL parameters, sets page to the first page, optionally normalizes sort_by to a default, and triggers a new search.

Renders the list of alumni profiles returned by searchAlumniProfiles. For each AlumniProfile in profiles, it shows selected searchable attributes (such as name and fields relevant to the search) and provides a clickable name and/or explicit "View profile" link. Clicking either navigates to alumni_profile.html with alumniProfileId set to that profile's id, without altering directory search state.

Displays pagination controls based on searchAlumniProfiles.page, searchAlumniProfiles.page_size, and searchAlumniProfiles.total_results. When the user selects a different page, it updates the page URL parameter via the state manager and triggers searchAlumniProfiles with the same filters and sort_by but the new page value.

Provides a persistent link on the directory page that navigates the user to their saved contacts section. When clicked, it routes to my_saved.html with the URL parameter section=contacts, without modifying any directory filters or results.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a full-width header and navigation, and a centered content column spanning approximately 6–8 columns with large side gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing red utility bar, black main navigation, and slim sub-nav, the page uses a single, centered content column that runs vertically from top to bottom. All functional elements of the alumni directory (search form, link, summary, sort control, list, pagination) are stacked in a linear, top-to-bottom sequence within this column, similar to the way paragraphs are stacked in the formal-letter mockup. There are no sidebars or competing columns; occasional small horizontal alignment (e.g., summary left, sort right) happens only within this single column, preserving the overall linear editorial feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing red utility bar, black main navigation, and slim sub-nav, the page uses a single, centered content column that runs vertically from top to bottom. All functional elements of the alumni directory (search form, link, summary, sort control, list, pagination) are stacked in a linear, top-to-bottom sequence within this column, similar to the way paragraphs are stacked in the formal-letter mockup. There are no sidebars or competing columns; occasional small horizontal alignment (e.g., summary left, sort right) happens only within this single column, preserving the overall linear editorial feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and nav region occupy the top ~20–25% of the viewport. The alumni directory content sits in the remaining ~75–80% on a white background. The main content column is centered and relatively narrow, spanning roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving generous left and right gutters of 25–30% each. A large vertical gap (~56–64px) separates the nav area from the top of the directory search content, visually framing it. Within the column, major functional sections (search form, mid-link, results summary/sort area, results list, pagination, final link) are separated by 24–40px vertical spacing that echoes the paragraph spacing of the original mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: The directory maintains a low-density, spacious layout. Inputs and text are not tightly packed; each control group in the search form has generous padding and clear vertical separation. The results list uses comfortable line height and spacing between rows, and there are no dense tables or grid layouts. All elements sit within the central column, with ample white space above, below, and to the sides, mirroring the editorial, letter-like feel while still accommodating search and list functionality.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the bright red utility bar and black navigation at the very top down into the white content area. A generous white gap leads the eye to the top of the central column where the search form anchors attention, playing the role that 'Dear Alumni,' plays in the mockup. After scanning the search inputs, the eye encounters a stand-alone navigation link line that acts as a focal pause (analogous to the LinkedIn URL in the mockup). From there, the flow continues downward to the results summary (left) and sort control (right), then into the vertically stacked list of alumni results, and finally to the pagination controls and closing link at the bottom, echoing the letter’s concluding sign-off line.

layout.overall_description: Below the institution’s red utility bar, black main navigation, and slim sub-nav, the Alumni Directory Search page opens into a calm white canvas. Centered on this canvas is a relatively narrow column, about half the viewport wide, with a generous 56–64px gap before the first interactive element. At the top of this column, a clean, understated search form stretches the full width of the column, its fields stacked with comfortable breathing room. Just beneath it, a single-line navigation link stands alone, separated by extra vertical whitespace so it reads as a deliberate focal moment in the center of the layout. Further down, a simple results summary line and a right-aligned sort control share a slim horizontal band, before the eye drops into a vertical stack of alumni results rows, each spaced to feel readable and unhurried. At the bottom of the column, centered pagination controls provide navigation through the result pages, followed by a final, subtle navigation link that feels like a closing line. Large left and right gutters frame the entire column, preserving the formal, editorial character of the original mockup while organizing the directory tools in a clear, linear progression.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component does not render visible UI. Conceptually, it sits behind the entire central content column, managing URL parameters and state for the search experience. It occupies no physical width or height in the layout and does not affect spacing; all other components are positioned as if it were not there.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, purely logical component with no footprint in the document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader also does not produce visible UI on its own. It is logically associated with the top portion of the central column, preparing filter options before the search form appears. It has no explicit height or width and does not alter the spacing between the navigation area and the search form; the search form is positioned as the first visible element in the central column regardless of this loader.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, purely logical component with no footprint in the document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form is the first visible component in the main content area and anchors the central column. It is centered horizontally on the page, spanning the full width of the central column (about 40–50% of the viewport on desktop) with large left and right gutters of 25–30% each. There is a substantial vertical gap of roughly 56–64px between the bottom edge of the black navigation/sub-nav stack and the top of the search form, echoing the large margin before 'Dear Alumni' in the mockup. Within the form, fields (e.g., name, location, industry, graduation year range) are arranged in a simple vertical stack or in lightweight two-column rows, but always respecting generous vertical spacing of 16–24px between field groups and 12–16px internal padding in inputs. The overall form presents as a single, clean block with a clear left alignment for labels and fields, but visually framed and centered within the white page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, participates in normal document flow; shrinks to fit smaller screens while remaining centered with consistent outer margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Immediately beneath the search form, after a comfortable vertical gap of about 24–32px, a stand-alone text link labeled along the lines of 'View My Contact List' appears as a single line within the same central column. It is left-aligned with the search form fields but separated enough vertically that it reads as its own paragraph-like line, similar to the standalone LinkedIn URL in the mockup. Above and below this link is slightly more whitespace than between form fields, so the eye naturally pauses on it as a mid-page focal moment. It does not have surrounding boxes or icons—just clean, underlined (or brand-colored) text within the narrow column, reinforcing the editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline within the central column; wraps gracefully on smaller screens but remains within the same single-column flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary appears below the search area and mid-page link, acting like the opening line of the 'body' of content. It is positioned about 32–40px beneath the view-contact-list-link, left-aligned within the central column. The text might read 'Showing 1–20 of 134 alumni' and spans only as wide as its content requires, leaving generous white space to the right. Visually it sits in a slim horizontal band that sets up the results section, maintaining the same text alignment and width as the rest of the column, and is surrounded by enough white space so it feels like a discrete line rather than crammed into controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; sits in the normal flow, often sharing a horizontal row with the sort control on wider viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: On desktop widths, the sort control shares the same horizontal row as the results summary. It is right-aligned within the central column, positioned on the same baseline as the summary text. The summary anchors the left side; the sort dropdown or button group anchors the right, creating a subtle but controlled horizontal tension within the otherwise vertical layout. This row occupies the full width of the central column but remains visually light, with about 16–24px vertical padding above and below to keep it airy. On smaller screens, the sort control gracefully stacks beneath the summary with a modest 8–16px gap, preserving the single-column flow. Its visual weight is restrained—simple label plus dropdown—so it complements rather than competes with the main results list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; aligns right within the central column on larger screens and stacks below the summary on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When results are being fetched, this loader occupies the area where the alumni results list would normally appear. It is centered both horizontally within the central column and vertically within a reserved block roughly 120–160px tall immediately beneath the summary/sort row. This ensures that even the loading state feels spacious and intentional, with plenty of white space around any spinner or 'Loading…' text. Once results load, this area is replaced by the actual alumni-results-list content without shifting the established column alignment or gutters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static placeholder occupying the results region during loading; its height keeps the layout from jumping as data appears.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The alumni results list is the dominant block of content beneath the summary/sort row and loader. It is fully contained within the central column, left-aligned and spanning the column width, with no side borders, maintaining the clean editorial look. Each alumni entry appears as a simple row (or lightly framed card) with the name, graduation year, location, and industry stacked or arranged in a modest hierarchy, but never in a dense table. Rows are separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing or a very subtle divider line, matching the paragraph rhythm from the mockup. The list itself may extend vertically well beyond the fold, but because of the strong single-column structure and wide side gutters, the scroll remains calm and linear. The top of the first result row sits about 24–32px below the summary/sort area, reinforcing clear grouping.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack within the central column; content simply extends downward, with no fixed or floating elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the last visible alumni result, after a generous 24–32px vertical gap, the pagination controls occupy a narrow horizontal strip. They are centered within the central column, so the pagination numbers and next/previous buttons sit roughly at the column’s horizontal center, with equal white space to the left and right. The controls themselves are compact and understated, with 8–12px inner padding around each page number and about 16px spacing between numbers, maintaining low visual density. This band feels like a natural endpoint for the current result set, clearly separated from the list above and any closing link below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; remains at the bottom of the current result set and scrolls normally, with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: At the very bottom of the central column, after the pagination controls, a final instance of the 'View My Contact List' navigation link (or a similarly purposed link, if not reused above) can appear as a closing line. It is positioned about 32–40px below the pagination band, left-aligned with the text in the rest of the column, and given enough white space beneath (24–32px) to feel like the last sentence or sign-off in a letter. The link is simple text with a subtle style consistent with the site’s links, offering a gentle navigational escape without visually competing with the main search and results interaction above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline; it marks the end of the scrollable central content column with no sticky behavior.

name: Alumni Profile Detail
title: Alumni Profile
description: Displays detailed information for a single alumni directory profile and lets the user add, remove, or update that alumnus/alumna in their personal contact list, with navigation back to search results or the saved contact list.

core_features: Load and display a single alumni profile using getAlumniProfileDetail based on the alumniProfileId URL parameter.
Show whether the profile is already in the user’s default contact list, using is_in_default_contact_list and contact_list_entry_id from getAlumniProfileDetail.
Provide an action to add the profile to the default contact list via addToContactList, optionally capturing a user-entered note and tags.
Provide an action to remove the profile from the default contact list via removeContactListEntry when it is already saved.
Provide an action to update the note and tags for an existing contact list entry via updateContactListEntry.
Offer navigation back to the directory search results using the return_to URL parameter when available.
Offer navigation to the user’s saved contacts section in My Saved (my_saved.html?section=contacts).
Handle loading, error, and success states for API interactions without relying on any hardcoded profile or contact data.
user_workflows:
View an alumni profile and see saved status:
1. User navigates to alumni_profile.html with an alumniProfileId URL parameter (and optional return_to parameter).
2. On page load, the page calls getAlumniProfileDetail with alumniProfileId.
3. While the request is in progress, a loading state is shown in the profile detail area.
4. When the response returns, the page populates profile information (name, graduation_year, location, industry, job_title, employer, linkedin_url, etc.) and saves is_in_default_contact_list and contact_list_entry_id into page state.
5. The contact actions area updates to show either an "Add to Contact List" action (if not saved) or a "Saved in My Contacts" state with a "Remove" and "Edit note/tags" option (if saved).

Add an alumnus/alumna to the contact list with optional note and tags:
1. User is viewing a profile where is_in_default_contact_list is false.
2. User enters an optional note and tags in the note/tags form.
3. User clicks the "Add to Contact List" button.
4. The page disables the button and triggers addToContactList with parameters: alumniProfileId from URL, contactListId from the broader application context or configuration (or as defined by the API contract for the default list), and the current note and tags values from the form.
5. On success, the response contact_list_entry is stored in page state, is_in_default_contact_list is set to true, and contact_list_entry_id is updated to the returned entry id.
6. The UI changes the button state to indicate the profile is saved and exposes options to edit note/tags and remove from the list.
7. On error, the page displays the returned message in a feedback area and re-enables the button without changing the saved status.

Remove an alumnus/alumna from the contact list:
1. User is viewing a profile where is_in_default_contact_list is true and contact_list_entry_id is available in page state.
2. User clicks the "Remove from Contact List" action.
3. The page shows a confirmation interaction (e.g., a simple confirm step) and, upon confirmation, calls removeContactListEntry with contactListEntryId set to the stored contact_list_entry_id.
4. While the request is in progress, the remove control is disabled to prevent duplicate actions.
5. On success, the page sets is_in_default_contact_list to false, clears contact_list_entry_id, note, and tags from the local contact state, and updates the UI to show the "Add to Contact List" option again.
6. On error, the page keeps the existing saved state and shows the error message in the feedback area.

Edit note and tags for an existing saved contact:
1. User is viewing a profile where is_in_default_contact_list is true.
2. The note/tag form is initialized with the existing note and tags from the current contact_list_entry held in page state.
3. User modifies the note and/or tags and submits the form using an "Update" action.
4. The page calls updateContactListEntry with contactListEntryId set to the current contact_list_entry_id and the updated note and tags from the form.
5. While saving, the form indicates a saving state and disables repeat submissions.
6. On success, the page replaces the stored contact_list_entry data in state with the version returned from updateContactListEntry and shows a success message.
7. On error, the existing values remain in place, and an error message is shown; the user may adjust and try again.

Navigate back to search results or to My Saved contacts:
1. If the return_to URL parameter is present, the page constructs a "Back to search results" link using the decoded value of return_to.
2. If return_to is not present, the "Back to search results" link either is not shown or falls back to a generic alumni_directory.html link (per application-wide navigation rules).
3. When the user clicks "Back to search results", the page performs a navigation to the constructed URL (or fallback) without altering the current contact list state.
4. The page also displays a "View My Contact List" link which navigates to my_saved.html?section=contacts when clicked, allowing the user to see all saved contacts including this one if it has been added.
interactions:
Page load with alumniProfileId:
- Trigger: Browser loads alumni_profile.html with an alumniProfileId URL parameter.
- Behavior: Page-init logic validates the presence of alumniProfileId; if valid, it calls getAlumniProfileDetail(alumniProfileId). If missing or invalid, the page shows an error state in the profile area and does not call any APIs.
- Result: On success, data bindings for profile display and contact status are populated; on failure, an error message is shown.

Click "Add to Contact List":
- Trigger: User clicks the add-to-contact action when the profile is not yet saved.
- Behavior: The click handler reads alumniProfileId from the URL, note and tags from the note/tag form, and contactListId from the application context or configuration. It then calls addToContactList(alumniProfileId, contactListId, note, tags).
- Result: On success, the contact_list_entry from the response is stored, is_in_default_contact_list becomes true, and the UI switches to the saved state; on error, the UI remains in the unsaved state and shows a message.

Click "Remove from Contact List":
- Trigger: User clicks the remove action when the profile is currently saved.
- Behavior: The click handler retrieves contact_list_entry_id from the stored page state and calls removeContactListEntry(contactListEntryId).
- Result: On success, is_in_default_contact_list is set to false and contact_list_entry_id is cleared, reverting the UI to the unsaved state; on error, the saved state remains and an error message is shown.

Submit note/tags update for an existing contact:
- Trigger: User submits the note/tag form while the profile is saved in the contact list.
- Behavior: The submit handler reads the current note and tags form values and contact_list_entry_id from state, then calls updateContactListEntry(contactListEntryId, note, tags).
- Result: On success, the updated contact_list_entry object replaces the existing state and a confirmation message is displayed; on error, existing values remain and an error message appears.

Change note/tags before adding to contact list:
- Trigger: User types into the note textarea and/or tags input while the profile is not yet in the contact list.
- Behavior: Each change updates local form state but does not trigger any API until the user clicks "Add to Contact List".
- Result: When addToContactList is eventually called, it uses the current note and tags values from this form state.

Click "Back to search results":
- Trigger: User clicks the back-link when it is available.
- Behavior: The handler checks the presence and validity of the return_to parameter; if valid, it navigates the browser to the decoded return_to URL. If not available or invalid, it navigates to a default alumni_directory.html URL.
- Result: User returns to the previous or default search results page without any API calls.

Click "View My Contact List":
- Trigger: User clicks the "View My Contact List" link.
- Behavior: Navigates the browser to my_saved.html?section=contacts.
- Result: The user is taken to their saved contacts view in My Saved.

Click LinkedIn or other external profile links:
- Trigger: User clicks a LinkedIn or similar external profile link if linkedin_url is available from the profile data.
- Behavior: Browser opens the external URL in a new tab/window per standard link behavior.
- Result: No API calls; user can view the external professional profile.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters and in-page state jointly govern behavior:
- alumniProfileId (required):
  - On every page load, the script reads alumniProfileId from the query string (alumni_profile.html?alumniProfileId={id}).
  - If alumniProfileId is present, getAlumniProfileDetail(alumniProfileId) is called once to populate the page.
  - If alumniProfileId is missing or invalid, the page enters an error state with no profile content and no contact actions.
- return_to (optional):
  - If present, the raw return_to value is used to reconstruct the "Back to search results" link (decoding or resolving the token according to application rules outside this page's scope).
  - If return_to is absent, the back-link is either hidden or linked to a default alumni_directory.html location.
- Derived runtime state (not stored in URL or localStorage):
  - loadingProfile: true while getAlumniProfileDetail is in progress; controls display of loading indicators for the profile area.
  - profileError: set when getAlumniProfileDetail fails; controls display of an error message and hides contact actions.
  - profile: populated with the AlumniProfile object returned from getAlumniProfileDetail; bound to display components.
  - isInDefaultContactList: initialized from is_in_default_contact_list in the getAlumniProfileDetail response; updated after add/remove actions.
  - contactListEntryId: initialized from contact_list_entry_id in the getAlumniProfileDetail response; updated when addToContactList or updateContactListEntry return successfully; cleared on successful removeContactListEntry.
  - contactNote and contactTags (form state): initialized from the current contact_list_entry.note and contact_list_entry.tags if available, or left empty when not saved; updated on each form input change.
  - isSavingAdd, isSavingUpdate, isRemoving: booleans used to disable relevant buttons and show progress indicators while respective API calls (addToContactList, updateContactListEntry, removeContactListEntry) are in flight.
  - feedbackMessage / feedbackType: runtime fields for showing success or error messages after API interactions.
- No state is persisted in localStorage or other client storage; all persistent data resides in backend models (AlumniProfile and ContactListEntry) and is retrieved via the assigned interfaces on each page load as needed.

components.functionality: On page initialization, reads alumniProfileId (and return_to) from the URL, validates alumniProfileId, and calls getAlumniProfileDetail to retrieve the alumni profile and its contact-list status. Manages loading and error states and populates shared page state for downstream components.
