Displays key profile summary information such as name, preferred_name if available, graduation_year, degree, school, and current location using the loaded AlumniProfile data. This component is read-only and updates automatically when the profile data changes.

Shows professional and industry-related information from the AlumniProfile, including industry, job_title, employer, and linkedin_url (if present). Provides an actionable link to the LinkedIn profile that opens in a new tab.

Reflects whether the current profile is in the user's default contact list. It listens to isInDefaultContactList and contactListEntryId from shared state set by profile-data-loader and contact actions. It shows a clear saved or not-saved status and any relevant description (e.g., that the profile is in the user's contact list).

Provides controls for entering or editing a note and tags associated with the contact list entry. When the profile is not saved, values are used by the add-to-contact action; when saved, the form reflects the existing note and tags and can be submitted to update them. It manages local form state (contactNote and contactTags) and exposes them to other components.

Displays and manages the main contact list actions for the profile. When isInDefaultContactList is false, it shows an "Add to Contact List" action that, when clicked, calls addToContactList using alumniProfileId from the URL and the current note and tags from contact-note-tag-form. When isInDefaultContactList is true, it shows a saved state, provides a "Remove" action that calls removeContactListEntry, and uses the same form for updating note and tags via updateContactListEntry.

Constructs and renders the "Back to search results" navigation link based on the optional return_to URL parameter. If return_to is present and valid, it decodes and uses it as the target; otherwise, it uses a fallback alumni_directory.html URL or hides the link, depending on global rules.
Provides a persistent "View My Contact List" link that navigates the user to my_saved.html with the section=contacts parameter so they can see all of their saved alumni contacts.

Shows success and error messages resulting from calls to addToContactList, removeContactListEntry, and updateContactListEntry, as well as errors from getAlumniProfileDetail. It listens to feedbackMessage and feedbackType in shared state and renders messages accordingly.

Controls whether profile-detail components (summary, professional details, contact actions) are shown based on loadingProfile and profileError states. While loading, it shows a loading indicator; if profileError is set, it only shows an error message and hides interactive contact components.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a full-width header and navigation, and a centered content column spanning approximately 6–8 columns with large side gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing red utility bar and black main navigation, all Alumni Profile Detail components sit in a single, narrow, centered column that reads like an editorial letter. The flow is strictly vertical: a subtle navigation row (back to results / go to contacts), then the profile header with name and status, then the add/remove actions, followed by the main professional details text, and finally the contact note/tag form. Feedback (API messages, loading/error gating) appears within this same central column, replacing or preceding the content while preserving the single-column, letter-like feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing red utility bar and black main navigation, all Alumni Profile Detail components sit in a single, narrow, centered column that reads like an editorial letter. The flow is strictly vertical: a subtle navigation row (back to results / go to contacts), then the profile header with name and status, then the add/remove actions, followed by the main professional details text, and finally the contact note/tag form. Feedback (API messages, loading/error gating) appears within this same central column, replacing or preceding the content while preserving the single-column, letter-like feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header and navigation occupy the top ~20–25% of the viewport. The Alumni Profile Detail content starts with a large white gap (about 56–64px) below the black nav bar, then the centered column occupies roughly 35–45% of the viewport width, leaving wide left and right gutters. All components respect this column: they never break into sidebars or multiple columns. Vertical gaps between major blocks (nav row, header, actions, details, form) follow a 24–48px rhythm, with the first text block sitting noticeably lower to create a framed, intentional white space similar to the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is low and calm: generous line height, short paragraphs, and clear spacing between elements. The header region (name, status, and actions) is the densest area but still uses 8–16px padding and spacing to keep items legible. The professional details area is very light, resembling a formal letter, with paragraphs separated by about 16–24px. Interactive elements like buttons and form fields are few and visually restrained, avoiding clutter and preserving the editorial quality.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the bright institutional header and navigation down into the centered content column. Within that column, the eye first encounters the small navigation row (back link on the left, my contacts link on the right), then drops to the alumnus/alumna name as the primary anchor. From there, attention glides to the contact status on the right of the header, then to the add/remove contact actions just below. The reader then follows the vertically stacked professional details paragraphs, pausing on any LinkedIn URL presented as a standalone line, and ends on the contact note/tag form at the bottom, which feels like a closing action after reading a letter. When loading or errors occur, the gate component temporarily becomes the central focal point in the same column, maintaining the top-to-bottom flow.

layout.overall_description: On the Alumni Profile Detail page, the familiar red utility bar and black navigation bar span the full browser width at the top, occupying about a fifth of the viewport. Beneath them, a generous white field opens up, with a narrow, centered column of content aligned in the middle of the page and surrounded by wide, empty gutters on both sides. At the top of this column, a discreet row offers navigation back to search results on the left and to the user’s contact list on the right. Below this, the alumnus/alumna’s name and key summary details appear as a simple, text-first header, with a compact contact status pill aligned to the right. Just underneath, add/remove contact actions are laid out horizontally but still within the same column, keeping the focus vertical. The main professional details read like a formal letter: short paragraphs, consistent spacing, and perhaps a LinkedIn link on its own line in the middle. Near the bottom of the column, a tidy contact note/tag form lets users annotate the contact, acting as a quiet conclusion to the page. Any loading states, errors, or API feedback messages are presented within this same narrow column—either at the top of it or temporarily replacing the inner content—so the overall impression remains that of a clean, institutional, letter-like document framed by white space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile data loader is a non-visual orchestration layer that conceptually occupies the full width of the centered content column. When it manifests visually (e.g., with a subtle inline loading indicator separate from the main gate), it appears as a small, centered text or spinner just below the navigation row and above the profile header, aligned to the middle of the column and not exceeding a single line of height. It never breaks the single-column structure and uses minimal vertical space, maintaining the calm, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the normal vertical document flow of the centered column; it does not overlay or fix to the viewport, and when inactive it effectively occupies no visible space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile header summary is the first major text block in the centered column, sitting roughly 56–64px below the bottom edge of the black navigation bar (or just below any visible feedback banner, maintaining a total top offset in that range). It spans the full width of the content column (about 35–45% of the viewport), with the alumnus/alumna name as a bold, left-aligned line at the top and any secondary details (degree, class year, department) stacked beneath as one or two shorter lines. The block has about 16–24px of vertical padding between its internal lines, creating a compact but breathable header. Horizontally, the name and core text anchor to the left edge of the column, while space is reserved towards the right for the contact status indicator so they share a visual band without breaking the single-column feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a standard block-level element in the centered column, anchoring subsequent components; it does not scroll independently or stick, and it responds to smaller viewports by allowing its text to wrap within the same column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The professional details section sits directly below the contact actions, separated by roughly 32px of vertical space to create a clear transition from controls to content. It occupies the full width of the centered content column and reads like a formal letter body: multiple short paragraphs, each left-aligned and separated by consistent 16–24px vertical spacing. The line length is moderate due to the narrow column, and line height is generous to support comfortable reading. Any LinkedIn URL or key external profile link appears as its own line in the middle of this block, left-aligned but visually emphasized through color (e.g., blue link), mirroring the focal LinkedIn moment in the reference mockup. The bottom of this text block leaves a 32–40px gap before the contact note/tag form, allowing the narrative content to feel complete before the user encounters input fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves like a standard, scrollable text block within the single column, expanding vertically as needed while maintaining fixed horizontal bounds and consistent paragraph spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact status indicator appears within the same horizontal band as the profile header summary, aligned to the right edge of the centered column. It is rendered as a compact pill or label (e.g., “In My Contacts” / “Not in My Contacts”) with minimal height, so it sits on the first or second line’s baseline of the header without overpowering the alumnus/alumna name. There is about 8–12px of horizontal breathing room between the status label and the right boundary of the column, and about 4–8px of vertical offset from surrounding text, keeping it crisp but unobtrusive. This placement lets the reader register status in their peripheral vision while keeping the name as the main focus.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline/anchored within the header summary’s block; it wraps or moves to the line below on smaller viewports but always remains right-aligned within the content column and does not float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact note/tag form is placed near the bottom of the centered content column, approximately 32–40px below the last paragraph of the professional details. It spans the full width of the column but maintains the editorial restraint: fields are stacked vertically (e.g., a note textarea followed by tag inputs) with 16px vertical gaps between them, left-aligned labels, and no heavy borders. The overall height is modest; it reads as a concise, single-block form rather than a large panel. The form’s top aligns with the rest of the column content, and its bottom leaves an additional 32–48px of white space before the end of the page or any footer, reinforcing a sense of a considered conclusion.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element in the vertical flow, expanding downward as more content (e.g., tags) is added; does not stick or overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add/remove contact actions sit directly below the profile header summary and status indicator, with about 16–24px of vertical gap separating them from the bottom of the header. Within the centered column, they are left-aligned with the header text, forming a short horizontal row of 1–3 buttons: a primary red button for the main action (e.g., “Add to My Contacts” or “Remove from My Contacts”) and, if applicable, a secondary, neutral-text button to its right. Each button has about 16px of horizontal padding and 8–12px of vertical padding, and there is roughly 16px of space between adjacent buttons. The whole action cluster occupies only the left half to two-thirds of the column width, leaving open space to the right and preserving the letter-like feel rather than looking like a full-width control bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as part of the normal vertical stacking of elements; on narrower screens, buttons wrap to the next line but remain left-aligned within the same column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back navigation link is positioned at the very top of the centered content column, approximately 56–64px below the bottom of the black navigation bar. It appears as a small, understated text link (e.g., “← Back to search results”) aligned to the left edge of the column. Vertically, it sits 16–24px above the top of the profile header summary, acting as a subtle breadcrumb without crowding the main name. The link is on a single line and does not extend beyond the bounds of the column, keeping inline with the conservative, institutional tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline/link element in the vertical flow; it scrolls away with the rest of the content and is not sticky or fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-contacts navigation link shares the same horizontal row as the back-navigation link at the top of the centered content column. While the back link is left-aligned, the my-contacts link (e.g., “My Contacts”) is right-aligned to the opposite edge of the column, creating a thin, balanced navigation bar within the white canvas. The two links sit on a single line when space permits, with the same vertical baseline and about 8–12px of vertical padding above and below this row, preserving readability. A vertical gap of 16–24px then separates this nav row from the profile header summary below, maintaining the calm, editorial spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow; it remains aligned to the right of the content column and may wrap to its own line below the back link on smaller viewports, but always within the same top nav row area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API feedback banner appears at the top of the centered content column when there is a recent success or error from profile-related actions. It is a low-height horizontal band (roughly 40–56px tall) that spans the full width of the column, not the full viewport, maintaining the central white-page aesthetic. The banner sits either directly above the back-navigation row or, with a small adjustment, between the global navigation and the back-navigation row, preserving an overall top margin of about 56–64px from the black nav to the first substantial content. Text inside is left-aligned with 16px of internal horizontal padding, and the banner has 16–24px of vertical spacing below it before the nav row or header to avoid feeling cramped. Colors are subdued institutional tones appropriate for success or error states, without heavy shadows or gradients.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in the normal flow, pushing the rest of the column content down when visible; not sticky or fixed, and does not overlay other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading and error gate occupies the same central column space that the profile content would normally use. During loading, instead of showing the header, details, and form, the gate displays a centered spinner and a brief status message, positioned roughly in the upper-middle of the white canvas: horizontally centered in the column and vertically about 15–20% below the bottom of the global navigation, echoing the framed feeling of the mockup’s main text. During error states, the gate presents a concise error message and any retry control within a lightly bordered or subtly shaded box, still limited to the column width and centered on the page. Above the gate, the back/my-contacts navigation row may remain visible or be replaced based on implementation, but the gate itself keeps the visual experience consistent by not breaking out of the editorial column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the main profile content within the centered column; it is not fixed to the viewport but effectively becomes the primary content block while active, pushing other elements above or below it as needed.

name: Volunteer & Mentor Overview
title: Volunteer and Mentoring Programs
description: Page for exploring alumni volunteer and mentoring programs, comparing key attributes, and navigating to detailed program pages.

core_features: Load mentoring program overview content using getMentoringProgramsOverview and display the returned introduction text.

Display all mentoring programs returned by getMentoringProgramsOverview.programs in a list and a comparison view, including key fields such as name, audience, time commitment, allowed_meeting_formats, application_open_date, application_close_date, is_active, and contact_email.
Use the incoming audience URL parameter to highlight or pre-filter mentoring programs whose audience field matches the parameter value.
Allow users to filter the visible mentoring programs by audience, using audience values derived dynamically from the loaded MentoringProgram records.
Allow users to compare mentoring programs side-by-side by their attributes (time commitment, meeting formats, and application period) using a comparison-view component.
Provide navigation from each mentoring program entry to its detail page mentoring_program.html using the program id as the mentoringProgramId URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Arrive with an audience pre-selection (from index.html or stay_connected.html)
1. User clicks a link on index.html or stay_connected.html that points to mentoring.html with an audience query parameter (for example: mentoring.html?audience=<audience_code>). 
2. When mentoring.html loads, the page controller reads the audience query parameter value from the URL. 
3. The page controller calls getMentoringProgramsOverview with no arguments. 
4. After getMentoringProgramsOverview resolves, the page stores the returned introduction and the full programs array. 
5. The audience filter component initializes its selected value based on the URL audience parameter and filters/highlights programs where program.audience equals the audience parameter. 
6. The comparison and list components render only the programs matching the selected audience (or visually emphasize them), while still allowing the user to clear or change the audience filter. 
7. The user reviews program attributes in the comparison view and clicks a program name or its "Learn more" action. 
8. The page navigates to mentoring_program.html with mentoringProgramId set to the clicked program's id (e.g., mentoring_program.html?mentoringProgramId=<program.id>).

Workflow 2: Arrive without an audience parameter and explore programs
1. User navigates to mentoring.html via the header "Volunteer & Mentor" navigation or a direct link without any query parameters. 
2. On page load, the page controller detects that no audience parameter is present and sets the initial audience selection state to show all programs. 
3. The page controller calls getMentoringProgramsOverview. 
4. When data is received, the introduction text is displayed and all programs from programs are rendered in the list and comparison views. 
5. The user optionally uses the audience filter controls to narrow programs by audience (e.g., to current_students or recent_graduates, using actual codes from the data). 
6. Changing the audience filter updates the URL audience parameter accordingly (adding, changing, or removing it) so that the filter state is reflected in the URL. 
7. The user can switch between list view and comparison view (if implemented) to see different levels of detail, without triggering additional API calls. 
8. The user clicks a program entry to navigate to mentoring_program.html with mentoringProgramId set to the selected program's id.

Workflow 3: Compare time commitment and application dates across multiple programs
1. User arrives on mentoring.html (with or without an audience parameter) and the page loads data via getMentoringProgramsOverview. 
2. The user optionally sets or adjusts the audience filter, which updates the visible subset of programs and the audience query parameter. 
3. The user switches to the comparison view using a view toggle control. 
4. The comparison component displays one column per program and rows for attributes such as min_time_commitment_hours_per_month, max_time_commitment_hours_per_month, allowed_meeting_formats, application_open_date, and application_close_date. 
5. The user can sort the comparison view by one of these attributes (for example, ascending time commitment) using client-side sorting of the currently visible programs, without making new API calls. 
6. From the comparison view, the user clicks a specific program's header or action link to navigate to mentoring_program.html, passing mentoringProgramId equal to that program's id in the URL.
interactions: On initial page load, the page controller reads the audience query parameter from the URL. It then invokes getMentoringProgramsOverview once, and passes the resulting data to child components via internal state or props.
The audience filter component initializes its selected audience based on the audience URL parameter (if present). If there is no parameter, it initializes to a state that includes all programs.

When the user changes the audience selection in the filter component, the component updates the internal selected audience state, triggers re-filtering of the programs shown in the list and comparison views, and updates the URL audience query parameter using URL navigation (add, replace, or remove).
When the user clears the audience filter, the filter component sets the selected audience state to show all programs and removes the audience query parameter from the URL.

When the user interacts with a view toggle (e.g., list vs comparison), the page updates an internal viewMode state (and optionally a corresponding URL parameter such as view=list or view=comparison) to control which main content component is visible. This does not trigger any additional API calls.

When the user clicks on a program card, program row, or a dedicated "Learn more" link in any program component, the click handler reads the associated program.id and navigates the browser to mentoring_program.html with mentoringProgramId set to that id as a URL query parameter.

When the user chooses a sort option in the comparison view (e.g., sort by time commitment), the comparison component updates its sort criteria state and reorders the array of programs it renders. This is done entirely on the client using the already loaded programs data.

If the audience URL parameter does not match any program.audience value returned by getMentoringProgramsOverview, the audience filter component can fall back to showing all programs while indicating that the current audience value did not filter any results. The URL parameter may be left intact so that back/forward navigation remains consistent.

Browser back and forward navigation is supported by relying on URL parameters: when the user navigates back to a previous state, the page controller re-reads the audience parameter on load and re-applies the appropriate filter/highlight without making extra API calls beyond the initial getMentoringProgramsOverview call.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is stored in URL query parameters. The audience parameter (string) is used to determine which mentoring programs to highlight or filter. On page load, the page controller parses the audience parameter from the URL. After calling getMentoringProgramsOverview and receiving the programs array, the controller computes a derived state value selectedAudience, initialized to the URL's audience parameter value (if present) or to a special value meaning 'all audiences'. The currently visiblePrograms list is then derived by filtering programs where program.audience equals selectedAudience, or by using all programs if selectedAudience represents 'all audiences' or if the audience parameter does not match any program values. When the user changes the audience filter via the UI, the selectedAudience state is updated and the URL is updated accordingly: if a specific audience is selected, audience is set to that value; if the filter is cleared, the audience parameter is removed from the URL. No localStorage or other persistent client storage is used. Optionally, an additional internal viewMode state may be managed to control whether the page shows a list view or a comparison view; this viewMode can be reflected in a view URL query parameter to make the view state bookmarkable (e.g., mentoring.html?audience=<code>&view=comparison). In that case, the page controller reads the view parameter on load and initializes viewMode. All other per-component states (such as sort criteria in the comparison view or which program is visually expanded) are managed purely in-memory and are derived from the initial data returned by getMentoringProgramsOverview, without additional API calls.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading and state for the Volunteer and Mentoring Programs page. On initialization, it reads the audience (and optional view) URL parameters, calls getMentoringProgramsOverview once, and stores the returned introduction string and programs array. It derives the selectedAudience and visiblePrograms based on the audience parameter and passes these, along with handlers for filter and navigation actions, to child components.

Displays the introduction text for the Volunteer & Mentor Overview page using the introduction string returned from getMentoringProgramsOverview. This component renders static text only and does not manage interaction beyond read-only display.

Allows the user to select an audience filter for mentoring programs. It derives available audience options from the unique program.audience values in the loaded MentoringProgram data. On initialization, it sets its selected audience to match the audience URL parameter if present. When the selection changes, it notifies the page controller to update selectedAudience, recompute the visiblePrograms subset, and synchronize the audience parameter in the URL.

Provides contextual feedback about the current audience selection and how it affects the displayed mentoring programs. It receives the currently selectedAudience and the full programs list from the page controller and renders a short message summarizing which audience is active and how many programs match. If the URL audience parameter does not match any program.audience values, it can indicate that no programs match the current audience while defaulting the visiblePrograms to all programs.

Allows the user to switch between a simple list view and a comparison table view of the mentoring programs. It sends view changes to the page controller, which updates the internal viewMode state and optionally updates a view URL parameter to make the chosen view bookmarkable.

Displays the currently visiblePrograms as a scrollable list of entries. Each entry shows key MentoringProgram fields such as name, audience, a short description (if available), indicative time commitment derived from min_time_commitment_hours_per_month and max_time_commitment_hours_per_month, allowed_meeting_formats, and the application period based on application_open_date and application_close_date. Each entry includes an action (e.g., clicking the name or a "Learn more" control) that navigates to the corresponding detail page mentoring_program.html with mentoringProgramId set to the program.id.

Renders the currently visiblePrograms as a side-by-side comparison table. Each program corresponds to a column, and the component displays rows for key attributes including audience, min_time_commitment_hours_per_month, max_time_commitment_hours_per_month, allowed_meeting_formats, application_open_date, application_close_date, is_active, and contact_email. The component supports client-side sorting of columns based on selected numeric or date attributes and allows the user to navigate to a program's detail page by clicking the program name or a dedicated action in its column.

Centralizes navigation logic from this overview page to the mentoring_program.html detail page. It receives a MentoringProgram id from child components (such as mentoring-program-list and mentoring-program-comparison-table) and constructs a URL of the form mentoring_program.html?mentoringProgramId=<id>. It then performs navigation using that URL so that detail pages consistently receive the correct mentoringProgramId parameter.

Manages the display of fallback states when getMentoringProgramsOverview returns no programs or when the API call fails. For an empty programs array, it shows an informational message that no mentoring programs are currently available, optionally influenced by the current audience filter. For error conditions, it displays a generic error message and may provide a means for the user to retry loading, which triggers the page controller to call getMentoringProgramsOverview again.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a full-width header and navigation, and a centered content column spanning approximately 6–8 columns with large side gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global red utility bar and black navigation bar, the page continues the mockup’s linear, editorial pattern: a large vertical gap, then a single, centered content column. At the top of this column sits the introductory text block (serving as the letter-style overview). Immediately beneath it, a compact cluster of interaction controls (filters, highlight indicator, and view toggle) is arranged in one or two light horizontal bands. Below that, a single main content zone shows either the program list or the comparison table (never both), with the navigation and state-handling behaviors embedded into that same column. No sidebars or secondary columns are introduced; everything lives in one vertical stack.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global red utility bar and black navigation bar, the page continues the mockup’s linear, editorial pattern: a large vertical gap, then a single, centered content column. At the top of this column sits the introductory text block (serving as the letter-style overview). Immediately beneath it, a compact cluster of interaction controls (filters, highlight indicator, and view toggle) is arranged in one or two light horizontal bands. Below that, a single main content zone shows either the program list or the comparison table (never both), with the navigation and state-handling behaviors embedded into that same column. No sidebars or secondary columns are introduced; everything lives in one vertical stack.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header (red utility bar + black main nav + slim sub-nav row) occupies the top 20–25% of the viewport on first load. The rest of the page is a white canvas. The main content column is centered and uses roughly 35–45% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving generous left and right gutters that echo the mockup’s letter-like feeling. The introductory text uses the top of this column, with a large 56–64px gap from the bottom of the navigation region to the first line of text. The controls block consumes a shallow band just below the intro, and the largest vertical share beneath that is reserved for the list/table content area. Spacing between vertical sections follows the 32–48px rhythm, with 16–24px between related sub-elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: Like the mockup, the page is low-density and editorial. The introduction is set as a clean block of text with comfortable line height and 16–24px between paragraphs. Controls are presented as a minimal, single-row or two-row band without heavy boxes or card chrome—just labels, simple inputs, and a subtle toggle. The program list uses simple stacked rows in the same column, with ample white space and limited visual clutter. The comparison table is restrained, with light rules and plenty of cell padding rather than dense data grids. Icons, labels, and CTAs remain understated to preserve the formal, institutional tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows a strong top-to-bottom sequence: bright red utility bar, then black navigation with the active section underline, then a calm white expanse. Within that white area, the centered introduction heading and opening lines of text act as the primary anchor, similar to ‘Dear Alumni’ in the mockup. After reading the intro, the eye naturally drops to the controls band (filters on the left, highlight indicator and view toggle on the right). The active view toggle subtly signals which main body (list vs. comparison table) is in focus. From there, the user scans down the vertical stack of programs or the rows of the comparison table, with individual program links or chevrons providing localized focal points for navigation. Empty/error states reuse the same central column, keeping attention in the middle of the page.

layout.overall_description: After the familiar red utility bar and black navigation bar, the page opens into a wide white field with a single, narrow column of content centered on the screen, evoking a formal letter on a clean sheet. A generous band of whitespace separates the navigation from the first text, leading to a brief, letter-like introduction that explains the Volunteer & Mentor offerings. Just below this text, a low-profile horizontal band hosts the audience filter controls on the left and a subtle highlight indicator plus a compact view toggle on the right, all confined within the same central column. Beneath this, the main content area occupies the rest of the vertical space: either a simple stacked list of mentoring programs or a restrained comparison table, each aligned to the same column width and surrounded by wide left and right gutters. Interactions for viewing program details are woven into each row or entry without breaking the editorial simplicity. When filters yield no results or an error occurs, a centered message appears in this same column, preserving the quiet, institutional feel. The overall impression is a calm, linear reading experience with a single, focused column and light, unobtrusive controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller is not visually represented but conceptually sits at the very top of the main content column, immediately below the global navigation region. It governs which of the two main views (program list or comparison table) occupies the central content area. Spatially, it does not consume visible space; instead, it defines that the central column—from the bottom edge of the controls band downward—is reserved for a single primary content view at any time. All other components (introduction, filters, toggles, states) flow around this controlled content region within the same centered column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, standard in-flow controller; logically anchored to the main content column but not rendering visible UI or affecting spacing directly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mentoring introduction appears as the first visible element in the main content area, positioned directly beneath the stacked header/navigation region. There is a large vertical gap of roughly 56–64px from the bottom of the black navigation bar/sub-nav row to the top line of this text, reinforcing the sense of a separate, letter-like page. The introduction is centered horizontally and restricted to a narrow column that spans approximately 6–8 of the implicit 12 columns (about 40% of the viewport width on desktop), with generous white gutters on both sides. The text is left-aligned within this column, set in multiple short paragraphs with 16–24px of vertical spacing between them. Any page title, such as “Volunteer & Mentor Overview,” appears as the first line or heading of this block, with a slightly larger font size and about 16–24px of space above the first paragraph, echoing the formality and hierarchy of the original mockup’s ‘Dear Alumni’ section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow block element; it expands vertically as content requires while maintaining a fixed maximum width and centered alignment on larger screens. On smaller screens, it gracefully expands closer to full width while preserving top and paragraph spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The audience filter controls sit directly below the mentoring introduction within the same centered column, separated from the last intro paragraph by approximately 32px of vertical space. They occupy the full width of the central column but are visually concentrated toward the left half to maintain a light, editorial feel. The primary filter label (e.g., “Audience”) is left-aligned along the column’s left edge, with its associated control elements (dropdowns, segmented chips, or simple links) laid out horizontally to the right of the label, using 16px horizontal gaps between controls. The entire filter row is relatively shallow, with about 8–12px of vertical padding above and below the controls, and no surrounding card or box, keeping the band visually minimal. On narrower viewports, these controls wrap to a second line within the same column, maintaining left alignment while preserving at least 8px vertical spacing between wrapped rows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow horizontal control row; remains within the central column and adapts responsively by wrapping controls onto additional lines while maintaining its position between the introduction and the view toggle/main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The audience highlight indicator is positioned on the same horizontal level as the audience filter controls, but anchored to the right edge of the central content column. Visually, it appears as a concise piece of text or a subtle pill-shaped label (e.g., “Showing: Alumni & Students”) that is vertically centered relative to the filter controls on the left. The indicator occupies the rightmost 20–25% of the column, leaving a comfortable amount of negative space between it and the filters. This creates a single, balanced row: filters aligned to the left, highlight indicator aligned to the right, all within the same column width. On smaller screens, when the filters wrap, the indicator shifts to a second line beneath the filters but remains right-aligned within the column, with about 8–16px of vertical separation from the filter controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow element aligned to the right within the same row or stacked just below the filters on narrow viewports; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view toggle sits just below the filters/highlight row, still within the central column, providing a subtle control to switch between the program list and comparison table. It is right-aligned to visually pair with the highlight indicator and is separated from the filter row by roughly 16–24px of vertical space. The toggle itself is compact—a two-option segmented control or side-by-side buttons (e.g., “List View” and “Comparison View”)—occupying about 20–30% of the column width. The inactive option is outlined or subtly underlined, while the active option uses a simple filled or bold state, keeping styling flat and clean. The left side of this horizontal space, beneath the filters, is purposefully left empty to maintain the airy, editorial feel. On smaller screens, the toggle may expand to near-full column width but remains anchored to the top-right portion of the main content area, above the first program row or table header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow element that governs which main content block (list vs. table) is visible directly beneath it. No sticky behavior; it scrolls naturally with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mentoring program list occupies the primary content area beneath the controls cluster, appearing only when the list view is selected. There is roughly 32–40px of vertical space between the view toggle and the first list item, reinforcing a clear separation between controls and content. The list is confined to the same centered column as the introduction, spanning about 6–8 of the 12-grid columns (roughly 40% of the viewport width on desktop). Each program entry is a full-width row within this column, with a simple, flat design: program title on the left at the top, a brief one- or two-line description beneath, and supporting metadata (e.g., audience, time commitment) arranged in a small, clean text stack below or to the side. Rows are separated by 24–32px of vertical space and possibly a light, thin dividing line to maintain visual structure without adding weight. The overall list reads like a series of structured paragraphs rather than heavy cards, preserving the sparse, editorial aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow vertical stack that scrolls within the page. It shares the same spatial slot as the comparison table—only one of them is rendered at a time, based on the view toggle and page controller state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison table uses the same central column footprint as the program list and appears only when the comparison view is active. The top of the table sits approximately 32–40px below the view toggle, mirroring the spacing used above the list view. The table’s width is constrained to the central column (about 6–8 grid columns), centered on the page with wide gutters on either side. Column headers form a single horizontal band at the top with slightly heavier weight text and a subtle bottom rule, while table rows align vertically underneath. Cell padding is generous (about 12–16px on all sides), and horizontal and vertical lines are fine and light, so the table remains readable yet understated. Important comparison attributes align in columns from left to right, but the overall density remains low; text wraps instead of shrinking to preserve legibility. On smaller screens, the table can either scroll horizontally within the central column or simplify into stacked comparison blocks, always centered and surrounded by white space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow block that replaces the program list within the main content region. On narrower viewports, may introduce an internal horizontal scroll area while remaining centered, but it does not become fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program detail navigation handler is expressed through consistent, understated affordances within each program entry in the list or each row of the comparison table. In the list view, each row either has a right-aligned text link (e.g., “View details”) or a small chevron icon placed at the far right edge of the program row, aligned with the program title’s baseline or slightly below, and set about 8–16px from the row’s right padding edge. The rest of the row may also be clickable, but visually this right-edge cue signals navigability without overwhelming the clean layout. In the comparison table, a similar right-aligned link or icon appears in the last column of each row, keeping visual weight minimal. All these navigational elements remain within the central column, aligned to its right edge, and follow the same vertical spacing as their parent rows, ensuring the flow remains linear and letter-like.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow interactive affordances embedded within list rows or table rows; they do not alter layout flow, and they scroll with their parent content. No floating or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When filters produce no results or an error occurs, the empty/error state content appears in place of the program list or comparison table, occupying the same central column. It is positioned beneath the view toggle with approximately 32–40px of vertical space, mirroring the start of normal content. The message is centered horizontally but left-aligned in terms of text within the column—similar to the introduction—so it feels like a short editorial note. If an icon is used, it is small and placed above the message text, with about 16px of spacing; otherwise, a concise heading (e.g., “No programs match your selection”) followed by a short explanatory sentence appears, with 12–16px between lines. The overall height of the empty state is modest, but it sits within a generous vertical band of white space, keeping the page feeling calm and uncluttered. Any suggested actions (like “Clear filters”) appear as understated links or buttons beneath the text, aligned left within the column and separated by about 16px of vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow block that replaces the main content (list/table) region when triggered. It remains centered within the same column and scrolls naturally with the page; no fixed or overlay behavior is used.

name: Mentoring Program Detail
title: Mentoring Program Detail and Enrollment
description: Displays details for a specific mentoring program and provides a form for alumni and other eligible participants to enroll as mentors.

core_features: Load and display mentoring program details based on the mentoringProgramId URL parameter using getMentoringProgramDetail.
Show the program’s purpose, audience, application window, time commitment expectations, and supported meeting formats from the MentoringProgram data.
Display all available mentor expertise topics for this program and allow users to select multiple topics from the expertise_topics returned by getMentoringProgramDetail.

Present time commitment options (label and hours per month) using the time_commitment_options from getMentoringProgramDetail, and bind the selection to availability_hours_per_month and time_commitment_option_label for enrollment.
Present meeting format options using meeting_format_options from getMentoringProgramDetail and restrict the selection to values supported by the program.
Provide a mentor enrollment form that collects name, email, affiliation, graduation year, and other required enrollment fields.
Require consent to terms/privacy (consent_terms) before allowing submission.
Submit the enrollment as a mentor using enrollAsMentor and show success or error feedback based on the response.
Provide navigation back to the mentoring programs overview (mentoring.html) with an audience URL parameter derived from the program’s audience field.
user_workflows:
View program details:
1. User arrives on mentoring_program.html with a mentoringProgramId URL parameter.
2. On page initialization, the page calls getMentoringProgramDetail with mentoringProgramId.
3. While loading, a loading state is displayed and all interactive elements are disabled.
4. Once data is returned, the page shows the program name, audience, description, time commitment expectations, application dates, and meeting format information.
5. If the program is inactive or outside the application window, the page displays a non-interactive notice and disables the enrollment form.

Enroll as a mentor:
1. After program details load and if the program is open for enrollment, the user scrolls to the enrollment form.
2. The user enters first name, last name, email, selects affiliation, and optionally enters graduation year.
3. The user selects one time commitment option from the time_commitment_options list; this sets availability_hours_per_month and time_commitment_option_label in the form state.
4. The user selects exactly one preferred meeting format from meeting_format_options (radio buttons or similar).
5. The user selects one or more expertise topics from the expertise_topics list (multi-select checkboxes), which populate expertise_topic_ids in the form state.
6. The user checks the required consent checkbox (consent_terms).
7. The user submits the form, triggering enrollAsMentor with all required parameters.
8. On a successful API response, the page displays a success message and may update the URL (e.g., add enrollmentStatus=success) to reflect the new state.
9. On an error response, the page displays error messages and keeps the form values so the user can correct and resubmit.

Return to mentoring programs:
1. At any point after details have loaded, the user can click the "Back to mentoring programs" link.
2. The click handler reads the program.audience value from the loaded MentoringProgram.
3. The page navigates to mentoring.html with the audience parameter set to the program’s audience (e.g., mentoring.html?audience={program.audience}).
4. If program.audience is not available, the link navigates to mentoring.html without additional parameters.

Reload or share the page:
1. The user copies or bookmarks the current URL, which includes mentoringProgramId and possibly enrollmentStatus.
2. Another user or the same user later loads that URL.
3. On load, the page reads mentoringProgramId and calls getMentoringProgramDetail.
4. If enrollmentStatus=success is present in the URL, the page displays a persistent success state banner in addition to the loaded program details, without re-submitting any data.
interactions: On page load, the code reads mentoringProgramId from the URL and triggers getMentoringProgramDetail. If mentoringProgramId is missing or empty, the page displays an error message and hides or disables the enrollment form.
When getMentoringProgramDetail is in progress, the page shows a loading indicator and prevents form submission.
If getMentoringProgramDetail returns an error or no program data, the page shows an error state explaining that the program could not be loaded and hides the form.

If the returned MentoringProgram is inactive or outside the application_open_date and application_close_date range (when those fields are provided), the page marks the program as not accepting new mentors and disables the enrollment form submit handler.
When the user selects a time commitment option, the selected option’s hours_per_month and label are stored in the form state as availability_hours_per_month and time_commitment_option_label.
When the user selects or deselects expertise topics, the form state maintains an array of selected expertise_topic_ids that will be passed to enrollAsMentor.

When the user selects a meeting format option, the selected value is stored in the preferred_meeting_format field in the form state; only options coming from meeting_format_options and compatible with the program are selectable.

When the user edits text fields (first name, last name, email, graduation year), the form state is updated and basic client-side validation rules are applied (e.g., required fields, email format, numeric year).
When the user toggles the consent checkbox, the consent_terms field is updated; if it is false at submission time, the submit handler prevents the API call and shows an inline validation message.

On form submission, the submit handler validates required fields, then calls enrollAsMentor with mentoringProgramId and the current form state fields. During submission, the submit button is disabled and a submitting indicator is shown.

If enrollAsMentor returns success=true, the page displays a success message, optionally clears or locks the form, and may update the URL to include enrollmentStatus=success without reloading the page.
If enrollAsMentor returns success=false or an error occurs, the page displays error feedback (general and/or field-level where applicable) and re-enables the submit button for another attempt.
Clicking the "Back to mentoring programs" link triggers navigation to mentoring.html with the audience parameter derived from the loaded MentoringProgram.audience value.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the mentoringProgramId URL parameter. On page initialization, the code reads mentoringProgramId from the query string (e.g., mentoring_program.html?mentoringProgramId={id}). If mentoringProgramId is missing, empty, or invalid, the page enters an error state where no API calls are made, and the enrollment form is hidden or disabled. If mentoringProgramId is present, the page calls getMentoringProgramDetail(mentoringProgramId) to retrieve the MentoringProgram and related data (expertise_topics, time_commitment_options, meeting_format_options). The resulting data populates the program detail and form controls. Additional transient UI state, such as loading flags, validation messages, and in-progress form values, is kept only in memory for the current page instance and is not persisted. A secondary URL parameter, such as enrollmentStatus, can be used to represent post-enrollment state (e.g., enrollmentStatus=success). When this parameter is detected on load, the page shows a success banner even if the enrollment was completed in a prior session, but it does not attempt to resubmit or reload any enrollment data, as no such interface is available. When navigating back to mentoring.html, the page builds a URL including an audience parameter drawn from program.audience, ensuring that the user lands on an overview filtered or focused on the same audience. All state transitions (loading, loaded, error, enrollment-success, enrollment-error) depend on the presence and values of URL parameters and the results of the two assigned interfaces; no localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used.

components.functionality: On initialization, reads mentoringProgramId from the URL query string and calls getMentoringProgramDetail to fetch the MentoringProgram, expertise_topics, time_commitment_options, and meeting_format_options. Manages loading, success, and error states and provides the loaded data to child components.

Displays core information about the mentoring program, including the program name, audience, description, application open/close dates, min/max time commitment expectations, and a summary of allowed meeting formats. Also communicates whether enrollment is currently open based on is_active and the application date range.

Shows the list of mentor expertise topics returned in expertise_topics. Each topic can be selected or deselected by the user, updating an internal collection of selected expertise_topic_ids that will be passed to enrollAsMentor.

Renders the time commitment options from time_commitment_options as mutually exclusive choices (e.g., radio buttons). When a user selects an option, the component updates the form state for availability_hours_per_month with the chosen hours_per_month and time_commitment_option_label with the option’s label.

Displays meeting format options using meeting_format_options, ensuring that only formats compatible with the current program are shown. The user selects exactly one preferred meeting format, which sets preferred_meeting_format in the enrollment form state.

Collects the mentor’s personal and program-specific enrollment information. Fields include first_name, last_name, email, affiliation, optional graduation_year, selected time commitment (via time-commitment-selector), selected preferred meeting format (via meeting-format-selector), selected expertise_topic_ids (via expertise-topics-selector), and a required consent_terms checkbox. Performs client-side validation and, on submit, calls enrollAsMentor with the aggregated form data and mentoringProgramId.

Displays feedback after an enrollment attempt. When enrollAsMentor returns, this component shows either a success message using the response.message or an error message. It can also read an enrollmentStatus URL parameter on load to display a success state for previously completed enrollments.

Aggregates and displays validation errors for the mentor enrollment form, including missing required fields, invalid email format, missing time commitment selection, missing meeting format selection, at least one expertise topic selection (if required by business rules), and unchecked consent_terms. Prevents form submission until all critical errors are resolved.

Provides a "Back to mentoring programs" action. When clicked, constructs a URL to mentoring.html with an audience query parameter set from the loaded MentoringProgram.audience, if available, and navigates the browser to that URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a full-width header and navigation, and a centered content column spanning approximately 6–8 columns with large side gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: All mentoring program components sit within the main content area below the existing red utility bar and black navigation bar. Inside this content area, everything is organized as a single, narrow, centered column that reads top-to-bottom like a formal letter. The back link appears first at the top of this column, followed by a generous vertical gap, then the program overview text (playing the role of the 'letter body'). Directly beneath the overview sits the enrollment status banner when present, standing alone as a single highlighted block. Below that, the mentor enrollment form flows in one vertical sequence, with the expertise topics selector, time commitment selector, and meeting format selector treated as vertically stacked sub-sections of the same form. Validation messages are integrated into this column, either at the top of the form or directly under relevant fields. The data loader temporarily overlays this central content area when needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All mentoring program components sit within the main content area below the existing red utility bar and black navigation bar. Inside this content area, everything is organized as a single, narrow, centered column that reads top-to-bottom like a formal letter. The back link appears first at the top of this column, followed by a generous vertical gap, then the program overview text (playing the role of the 'letter body'). Directly beneath the overview sits the enrollment status banner when present, standing alone as a single highlighted block. Below that, the mentor enrollment form flows in one vertical sequence, with the expertise topics selector, time commitment selector, and meeting format selector treated as vertically stacked sub-sections of the same form. Validation messages are integrated into this column, either at the top of the form or directly under relevant fields. The data loader temporarily overlays this central content area when needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header and navigation still occupy roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport when visible, preserving the stacked red and black bars. Below them, the mentoring page content uses a centered column that spans about 35–45% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving large left and right white gutters. Between the black navigation bar and the first line of main content (the back link and then the program title/intro), there is a large white gap of about 56–64px to echo the 'framed letter' feeling. Within the content column, vertical spacing between related elements (labels and fields, paragraphs, options) stays around 16–24px, while spacing between larger blocks (program overview, status banner, and form sections) is in the 32–48px range. No component stretches edge-to-edge; everything is visually constrained to the narrow central column.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content is intentionally low-density and editorial. Text, form fields, and selectors are stacked with generous line height and comfortable spacing, avoiding any multi-column arrangements. Form sections are short and separated by clear breathing room so that no area feels crowded. The program overview reads like a short letter with distinct paragraphs; the enrollment form appears as a calm, linear sequence of questions with minimal visual noise, flat styling, and clear separation between field groups. The status banner and validation messages are concise and avoid heavy bordering or decoration, aligning with the conservative, institutional tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first moves across the bright red utility bar and black navigation bar at the top. From there it drops into the white content area and lands on the subtle 'Back to programs' link at the top left of the centered column, then quickly to the program title and initial overview text beneath it, which act as the main entry into the page. After reading the overview paragraphs from top to bottom, the eye naturally encounters the enrollment status panel as a standalone horizontal block—a distinct focal moment before the form. Attention then continues downward into the form, moving sequentially through the expertise topics selector, then time commitment, then meeting format, and finally the remaining form fields and the submit button. Any validation messages appear exactly where the eye is already focused (under the form title or beneath a specific field), so they do not disrupt the linear top-to-bottom flow. When data is loading, the loader temporarily becomes the visual focus, centered in the white area, while the surrounding header and nav remain visible but secondary.

layout.overall_description: Below the institutional red utility bar and black navigation bar, the page opens into a calm, white canvas. In the middle of this canvas sits a relatively narrow content column—about two-fifths the width of the screen—centered horizontally with large, quiet gutters on both sides. At the very top of this column, a modest 'Back to programs' link anchors the left edge, hinting at navigation without competing for attention. After a generous block of vertical whitespace, the program overview begins, set in left-aligned text that mirrors the cadence of a formal letter: a heading, then short, well-spaced paragraphs. Directly under this overview, a single status banner, if present, stretches across the same column width, softly highlighted but still minimal, creating a clear turning point between reading and action. Below it, the enrollment form occupies the rest of the column and is arranged as a simple downward flow of sections. The expertise topics selector appears first, followed by time commitment and meeting format, each occupying the full column width and stacked in a predictable rhythm. Additional personal or contact fields follow in the same vertical line, with labels and inputs aligned to the left and plenty of space between each group. Validation messages slide in directly under relevant headings or fields, in red but unobtrusive text, maintaining the overall clarity. While data is loading, a centered loader appears in the middle of this white column, momentarily dominating attention but still framed by the same generous margins. The entire layout feels like a formal, considered letter paired with a straightforward institutional form, with visual weight concentrated in the middle and strong horizontal bands only at the very top of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears within the main white content area, centered both horizontally and vertically relative to the visible portion of the content column. It does not replace the red utility bar or black navigation bar; instead, it overlays or occupies only the white zone beneath them. The loader (spinner and any accompanying text) sits exactly on the central axis of the narrow content column, which itself is centered on the page and spans roughly 40% of the viewport width. Above and below the loader, there is ample white space, preserving the editorial, low-density feel—even during loading. If an overlay is used, it is a subtle, semi-transparent white layer covering the content column only, not the full viewport, allowing the header bars to remain crisp and unaffected at the top.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a centered overlay or placeholder within the content column. It stays fixed to the center of the content column area while loading, then is fully removed from the layout once the data is available, allowing the underlying back link, overview, and form sections to appear and take their static positions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program overview section is the first substantial content block in the centered column, positioned below the navigation bars with a generous top margin of approximately 56–64px from the bottom of the black navigation bar. It aligns to the same left margin as all other content in the column. The section spans the full width of the central column (about 35–45% of the viewport), with symmetrical white gutters on both sides. At the top of this section, the program title or heading appears, left-aligned, followed by 16–24px of vertical space before the descriptive paragraphs begin. These paragraphs are short and separated by consistent 16–24px gaps, mimicking the structure of a formal letter. The overall block height is modest, with clean whitespace above and below, and there are no sidebars or images competing alongside it. The bottom of the overview is separated from the next major block (the enrollment status panel or the form if no status) by a clear 32–40px vertical gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky block element anchored near the top of the content column. It does not scroll independently; it scrolls with the page as part of a continuous vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The expertise topics selector sits within the main form area, occupying the full width of the central content column and positioned as the first major question block of the enrollment form. Vertically, it begins roughly 32–40px below the bottom of the program overview section (or below the enrollment status panel if that is present). At the top of this block, a clear label or subheading appears left-aligned, spaced about 8–12px above the multi-select control itself. The list of expertise topics is stacked vertically in one column—no side-by-side chips or multi-column lists—so each option runs from the left margin of the content column to the right margin of the column. Vertical spacing between options is around 8–12px, maintaining a low-density feel. The entire selector block is visually grouped by its consistent left alignment and a comfortable 24–32px gap before the next form section (time commitment).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the form that expands vertically as options wrap or increase. It reflows naturally with the page content and does not fix or float; on smaller viewports it simply narrows with the column, preserving the single-column stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time commitment selector is placed directly below the expertise topics selector within the same central column, separated by a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px to clearly distinguish it as the next question. It shares the same left alignment and width as the expertise selector, filling the entire central column (around 40% of the viewport) with wide white gutters to its left and right. The label or question text appears first, left-aligned, with about 8–12px of vertical space before the options. The options (e.g., radio buttons or simple choice rows) are arranged in a single vertical list, each option occupying the full column width so that the eye moves straight down. Spacing between individual options is approximately 8–16px, maintaining comfortable readability and avoiding visual crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the vertical form flow. It does not have independent scroll or sticky behavior; it follows the page’s top-to-bottom reading order and shrinks or expands naturally with text length and number of options.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The meeting format selector is positioned directly beneath the time commitment selector, following the same structural rules to maintain a steady vertical rhythm. A vertical margin of about 24–32px separates it from the time commitment block, reinforcing that it is a distinct but related question. It uses the full width of the central column, left-aligned, with no other components sitting to its right or left. The label or question is at the top of the block, followed by 8–12px of white space and then the list of possible formats (e.g., online, in-person, hybrid), each option forming one row in a vertical list. Each row starts aligned with the left edge of the form labels and extends toward the right edge of the column. The spacing between rows is consistent (8–16px) to keep the section airy and easy to scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-floating element in the form’s sequence. It flows with the page; on smaller screens it simply reduces in width with the column, maintaining its one-column vertical list of options.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mentor enrollment form acts as the main container of interaction within the content column and begins immediately below the program overview section, or below the enrollment status panel if that panel is visible. There is a clear vertical offset of roughly 32–40px between the overview and the top of the form, giving the sense of moving from reading to action. The form header (e.g., 'Enroll as a Mentor') is left-aligned at the top of this block, spanning the width of the central column, with about 16–24px of space below it before any introductory text or the first field. Within the form, all fields are arranged in a single vertical stack: labels above inputs, with 8–12px between each label and its field, and 16–24px between distinct field groups. The expertise topics selector, time commitment selector, and meeting format selector are visually integrated into this stack as major subsections, each separated from adjacent fields by 24–32px, creating a calm, ordered progression down the page. The submit button is placed near the bottom of the form, aligned with the left edge of the text column rather than centered or full-width, with about 24px of clear space above it and 32–48px below it before the end of the content column. No element within the form extends beyond the central column; the wide side gutters remain white and empty, reinforcing the editorial, focused feeling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that scrolls as a whole with the page. It does not have sticky headers or multi-column behavior. On narrower viewports, the form simply narrows with the column and continues to stack all fields vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The enrollment status panel appears as a single, self-contained horizontal block within the central column, placed between the program overview section and the main body of the enrollment form. It starts approximately 24–32px below the last line of the program overview, occupying 100% of the central column width (around 35–45% of the viewport) and maintaining the same left and right alignment as the text above and below it. The panel has a slightly differentiated background (e.g., a very light tint) and internal padding of about 16–24px on all sides, creating a distinct yet understated band of information that the eye naturally lands on after reading the overview. The message text is left-aligned and may occupy one to three short lines, preserving the low-density feel. Below the status panel, another 24–32px gap separates it from the top of the enrollment form fields, so it reads as a clear transition point between information and next steps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline status block. It appears or disappears based on enrollment state but otherwise behaves like a normal block element in the vertical flow. It does not overlap other content nor does it stick to the top; it scrolls as part of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Form validation messages are integrated into the central column, directly associated with the elements they refer to. If there is a global validation summary, it appears inside the enrollment form, aligned with the left edge of the form content and positioned just below the form title or immediately above the first field, with about 8–12px of vertical space above and below to keep it legible but not heavy. Field-level validation messages sit directly beneath their corresponding input, selector, or option group, separated by 4–8px of vertical space from the field and using a smaller font size and red text to align with the institutional error color. These messages never extend outside the width of the central column and do not create new columns or side margins; they simply expand the vertical height of the form where necessary. Spacing between subsequent fields or sections is preserved as much as possible (16–24px), so even when multiple messages are visible, the form retains a clear, linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, reactive elements that appear and disappear without changing the overall layout structure beyond increasing local vertical height. They scroll naturally with the form and do not float or overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-programs link is positioned at the very top of the main content column, just below the large white gap that separates the header/navigation from the content. It is left-aligned within the central column, occupying only as much horizontal space as the text (and an optional left-pointing arrow icon), and sits approximately 24–32px below the bottom edge of the black navigation bar. This places it above the program overview section, with about 16–24px of vertical space between the link and the start of the overview heading. The link is styled as a simple textual control consistent with the conservative design—no large button or heavy decoration—so it remains visually subtle and does not compete with the program title. The wide white gutters to the left and right remain empty, further emphasizing the linear, editorial center.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline link that scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page. It is not sticky and does not float; it behaves like a simple top-of-content navigation crumb within the single-column flow.

name: Scholarships & Support
title: Scholarships & Support
description: Functional listing page where users can filter, sort, and browse scholarships, then navigate to detailed scholarship information or giving options.

core_features: Load available filter and sort options for scholarships using getScholarshipFilterOptions.
Display a paginated list of scholarships using searchScholarships, showing key fields such as award amount, eligibility group, level of study, and deadline.
Allow users to filter scholarships by eligibility group, level of study, and minimum deadline date.
Allow users to sort scholarships by award amount and other sort options provided by getScholarshipFilterOptions.
Support deep-linking and sharable URLs by encoding filter, sort, and page state in URL parameters.
Allow navigation to an individual scholarship detail page when the user selects a scholarship.
Provide an action to navigate to the giving page pre-configured for student scholarship support, when configured by the implementation.

user_workflows.name: Browse scholarships with default or pre-selected filters
steps: User navigates to scholarships.html, either directly from the site navigation or via a link that may include URL parameters (eligibility_group, level_of_study, min_deadline, sort_by, page).
The page controller reads URL parameters and stores the current state for eligibility_group, level_of_study, min_deadline, sort_by, and page.
The page calls getScholarshipFilterOptions to retrieve available eligibility groups, levels of study, deadline range, and sort options.

The filter controls are initialized: values matching URL parameters are pre-selected; if a URL parameter value is not present in the corresponding options, that parameter is ignored or reset according to implementation rules.

The page calls searchScholarships with a filters object derived from the current state (eligibility_group, level_of_study, min_deadline), along with sort_by, page, and a page_size determined by implementation configuration.
The scholarship list displays the returned scholarships for the current page, along with total result count and pagination controls.
User scrolls and reviews scholarship cards, each showing key details such as name, eligibility group, level of study, award amount, and deadline.

user_workflows.name: Refine scholarships using filters and sorting
steps: User interacts with the eligibility group control (e.g., dropdown or radio) and selects a different option.
The filter component updates the local filter state and updates the eligibility_group parameter in the URL, resetting the page parameter to the first page.
The page controller calls searchScholarships again with the updated filters and the current sort_by and page (typically reset to the first page on filter change).
The scholarship list refreshes to show results that match the new eligibility group.
User optionally adjusts level of study in a similar way; the level_of_study parameter is updated in the URL, page is reset, and searchScholarships is called again with the updated filters.

User adjusts the minimum deadline filter (e.g., by selecting a date within the allowed deadline_range). The min_deadline parameter in the URL is updated. searchScholarships is called with the updated min_deadline filter, and the list refreshes.

User selects a sort option (such as sorting by award amount or deadline) from the sort control. The sort_by parameter in the URL is updated, page is reset, and searchScholarships is called with the new sort_by value.
Results are re-rendered in the scholarship list according to the new filters and sort order.

user_workflows.name: Paginate through scholarship results
steps: User views the first page of scholarship results with current filters and sort.
User clicks a pagination control (e.g., next, previous, or a specific page number).
The pagination component updates the page parameter in the URL to the selected page number, leaving other parameters unchanged.
The page controller calls searchScholarships with unchanged filters and sort_by, but with the new page value.
The scholarship list refreshes to show scholarships for the selected page while maintaining filter and sort context.
